title: Evaluating the balanced score card for strategic HRM in a higher education institution
abstract:
This thesis explores the applicability of the Balanced Scorecard (BSC) as a performance management tool to measure, manage, and improve Human Resource Management (HRM) performance in higher education institutions (HEI). Using a case study approach at an internationally operating, multi-campus university, the research addresses strategic and operational challenges in HRM and investigates how the implementation of the BSC can drive both individual and institutional effectiveness.

The research critically examines the broader trends impacting the higher education sector —globalisation, technological advancements, and demographic shifts — and their implications for human resources. It identifies key academic theories, concepts, and frameworks related to HRM and performance management, forming the foundation for a problem definition and research questions. Through the design and implementation of a tailored HR-BSC, the research evaluates its impact on aligning HR practices with institutional goals, addressing organisational complexities, and fostering continuous improvement.

Reflecting the interdisciplinary and practical focus of the Doctor of Business Administration (DBA) programme, this thesis integrates published theories on performance evaluation with real-world application, offering actionable recommendations for enhancing HRM practices in HEI. By doing so, it contributes to the ongoing scholarly debate on adapting corporate performance management models to academic contexts and highlights the critical role of strategic HRM in sustaining institutional competitiveness.

Preface 
 
This thesis was accepted as a dissertation by the Faculty of Business and Law at the 
Anglia Ruskin University, submitted in July 2025. It was written while I was working as 
Campus Director at Schiller International University in Heidelberg, Germany. Its 
completion was supported by numerous people to whom I would like to express my 
gratitude. 
First and foremost, I would like to thank my doctoral supervisor, Dr Fergus Bolger, who 
supported my choice of topic, accompanied me during the long process of writing the 
thesis, and continually drew my attention to unclear aspects and potential for 
improvement. Additionally, for his continuous support, especially in the "hot" phase 
before submitting the thesis.  
I would like to thank Dr Paul Higgins not only for taking on the second opinion, but above 
all for the valuable suggestions and the joint supervision meetings.  
I would like to express my sincere thanks to my leadership at the organisation, Manuel 
Alonso (PhD) and Dan Finuf. Over the long period of my part-time doctorate, they have 
shown the greatest understanding for my situation and have supported me with all the 
means. I would like to thank all the participants in the expert interviews for their 
willingness to take part in my survey and for their openness in answering the sometimes 
admittedly sensitive questions. 
I would like to express my heartfelt thanks to Alex Ward for proofreading the thesis and 
for the many valuable hints, tips and emotional support.  
Finally, I thank my family for their unwavering support and the confidence they have 
given me throughout this project. Without them, I would certainly never have come this 
far. 
Tanja Hertel-Ward 
 
 
 
 
 
2 
ABSTRACT 
 
FACULTY OF BUSINESS AND LAW 
DOCTOR OF BUSINESS ADMINISTRATION 
 
"Evaluating the Balanced Scorecard for strategic HRM in a Higher Education 
Institution: A Case Study Approach" 
Tanja Hertel-Ward 
July 2025 
 
Abstract 
This thesis explores the applicability of the Balanced Scorecard (BSC) as a performance 
management tool to measure, manage, and improve Human Resource Management (HRM) 
performance in higher education institutions (HEI). Using a case study approach at an 
internationally operating, multi-campus university, the research addresses strategic and 
operational challenges in HRM and investigates how the implementation of the BSC can drive both 
individual and institutional effectiveness. 
The research critically examines the broader trends impacting the higher education sector —
globalisation, technological advancements, and demographic shifts — and their implications for 
human resources. It identifies key academic theories, concepts, and frameworks related to HRM 
and performance management, forming the foundation for a problem definition and research 
questions. Through the design and implementation of a tailored HR-BSC, the research evaluates 
its impact on aligning HR practices with institutional goals, addressing organisational complexities, 
and fostering continuous improvement. 
Reflecting the interdisciplinary and practical focus of the Doctor of Business Administration (DBA) 
programme, this thesis integrates published theories on performance evaluation with real-world 
application, offering actionable recommendations for enhancing HRM practices in HEI. By doing 
so, it contributes to the ongoing scholarly debate on adapting corporate performance management 
models to academic contexts and highlights the critical role of strategic HRM in sustaining 
institutional competitiveness. 
Keywords: Human Resource Management, Higher Education Institutions, Performance Management, Balanced Scorecard.

Chapter 1 
1. Introduction
1.1. 
Context of Thesis 
Technological advancements and the rapid expansion of the global economy in the 21st 
century, coupled with shifting market conditions, have compelled many businesses to 
increasingly adopt an international approach to remain competitive (Crupi et al., 2021). 
Between 2019 and 2023, the global economy experienced moderate growth. In 2019, the 
world's GDP was approximately $88.0 trillion USD. By 2023, it had increased to around 
$106.0 trillion USD, indicating a growth of about 20% over the four-year period (World 
Bank, 2024; Macrotrends, 2024). In 2023, the total value of world trade in goods and 
commercial services was approximately $30.5 trillion USD. This represents a 2% decline 
compared to the previous year, 2022. However, when compared to the pre-pandemic 
levels of 2019, the trade volume in 2023 was still up by 6.3%, indicating a recovery and 
growth in global trade activities over the period (World Trade Organisation, 2023). These 
figures reflect broader economic shifts and illustrate the resilience and gradual expansion 
of the global economy and trade, despite challenges such as the COVID-19 pandemic 
and other economic disruptions during this timeframe.  
Amid these global changes, the competitiveness of both businesses and nations now 
hinges on the strategic application of technology, global integration, performance 
optimization, and sustained investment in research and development (Gordon, 2013; 
Ferreira et al., 2021; World Economic Forum, 2023). In this evolving landscape, Higher 
Education Institutions (HEIs) have become vital knowledge hubs, driving innovation and 
fostering global economic growth through knowledge creation and distribution  
(Barkhordari et al., 2019; World Bank, 2022). However, the intensifying global competition 
places substantial pressure on universities and research institutions to accelerate the 
commercialization of new knowledge and adopt innovative methods for sharing essential 
expertise (Lang, 2013; Chesbrough, 2020; Diamandis et al., 2020). These developments 
 
 
9 
underscore the role of higher education as a catalyst for addressing modern economic and 
technological challenges.  
In view of these demands, megatrends in higher education (HE), such as globalisation, 
demographic changes, digital transformation, regulatory changes, are reshaping the 
sector on a global scale. These trends have intensified competition, as universities 
compete for students as well as for top talent, both of which, according to Knight and 
Zhang (2022), are needed to remain successful in a rapidly changing higher education 
landscape (Knight, 2018). The interplay between multiple megatrends creates further 
dynamics that are hard to model and predict (World Economic Forum, 2020). For 
instance, demographic shifts intersect with globalisation, reshaping patterns of student 
mobility and, in turn, influencing enrolment strategies. Additionally, each university 
operates under unique conditions, including its geographical location, cultural context, 
institutional priorities, and operational structure. These differences make it challenging to 
generalise predictions about future developments in higher education.  
As such, these trends each bring unique challenges and opportunities that demand 
closer exploration. For instance, globalisation has substantially transformed higher 
education. The Organisation for Economic Cooperation and Development (OECD, 2023) 
notes that international student enrolment grew by 4% annually before the pandemic, 
highlighting a sustained demand for globalised education. According to Mirka Martel (IIE, 
2024), international student enrolment skyrocketed in the 2022–23 academic year, 
surpassing pre-pandemic levels and landing at over one million students. Similarly, the 
6th IAU Global Survey on the Internationalisation of Higher Education (IAU, 2023) 
highlights key trends in internationalisation around the world comparing practices across 
private and public HEIs and institutions in different regions. The survey discloses that 
academic leaders increasingly recognize the importance of internationalisation, even at 
institutions that considered it a low priority previously. 
Further, student mobility remains a central focus as a critical element of globalisation in 
higher education and plays a key role in fostering cultural exchange, enhancing 
academic collaboration, and building global competencies. Student mobility refers to the 
 
 
10 
movement of students across geographic, cultural, and institutional boundaries for 
educational purposes (Knight, 2018; OECD, 2023; UNESCO, 2023). Additionally, 
activities to foster collaboration across borders to increase the student base have grown 
substantially over the past five years (IAU, 2024), for example, international cooperation 
through joint degree programmes, or capacity building through training and development. 
For instance, imagine a university in Africa partnering with a European university to 
create a joint degree programme in sustainable agriculture. At the same time, capacity-
building efforts focus on training faculty, or improving infrastructure at the African 
university to ensure long-term sustainability of the programme (DAFM, 2023).  
According to the OECD (2023), Asian and African countries are the largest source 
regions for international students, with significant outbound mobility rates primarily to 
Europe and North America. Meanwhile, destinations like China, Germany, and the UAE 
are emerging as increasingly attractive study hubs, reflecting a diversification of student 
mobility patterns.  
Similarly, the United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organisation (UNESCO, 
2022) reported the recent history of global student flow as shown in Figure 1, forecasting 
over 9 million international students globally by 2030:  
 
Figure 1: Global Student Mobility (Source: UNESCO website) 
 
 
11 
The significant growth in international student enrolment over the last two decades has 
led many universities to adopt global recruitment strategies for both students and faculty, 
aiming to enhance student numbers, diversity, and to attract talent in order to remain 
competitive. For instance, universities in English-speaking countries such as the U.S., 
U.K., and Australia compete to attract students from regions like Asia and Africa, 
emphasizing flexibility and accessibility in their offerings to stand out (OECD, 2023). 
Hence, branch campuses, multi-campus universities, joint degree programs, and online 
offerings or digital universities have become critical strategies for tapping into 
international markets and reflect efforts to outcompete rival institutions by catering to 
students who prefer local study options or specialised cross-border educational 
programmes (Knight, 2018; Knight & Zhang, 2022).  
Such multi-campus institution models, like Schiller International University (SIU) — the 
focus of this thesis — intensify the already competitive landscape by targeting diverse 
regional markets. Another example is INSEAD, branding itself as “The Business School 
for the World,” operating campuses in France, Singapore, and Abu Dhabi, thus directly 
competing with top-tier institutions across Europe, Asia, and the Middle East (INSEAD, 
2024). Likewise, the Spanish business school IE University acquired a campus in New 
York, granting it the authority to offer U.S. degrees. This move allows IE to provide 
master's programs in business and sustainability, positioning it among the few foreign 
institutions accredited to operate independently in the U.S (IE, 2024). Such models allow 
institutions to attract diverse student demographics, while also pressuring local 
universities to innovate and differentiate themselves to remain competitive.  
The described dynamic is evident in the ongoing race to secure resources, not just in 
student recruitment but also in attracting and retaining top-tier faculty and staff. As 
Altbach and Knight (2007) highlight, faculty mobility plays a critical role in global 
engagement by facilitating international research collaborations and cross-cultural 
knowledge exchange. These global networking opportunities not only enhance the 
professional development of faculty members but also strengthen the internationalisation 
efforts and reputation of the institutions they represent (Zhang et al., 2024). Faculty 
 
 
12 
mobility is closely tied to the growing competition for talent in higher education, as 
institutions increasingly aim to attract, retain, and exchange top academic professionals.  
As stated by Usher (2022), between 2006 and 2018, the number of higher education 
institutions in 56 countries, accounting for 95% of global enrolments, grew by 51%, 
averaging seven new institutions daily. This rapid growth has created a global talent 
race, particularly in fields with limited specialists or evolving study areas. Top-ranked 
universities and well-funded institutions often dominate this competition by offering better 
compensation, research funding, and career development opportunities, leaving smaller 
or less-resourced universities at a disadvantage (Altbach, 2015).  
Shifting demographics further complicate the HE landscape. While global migration has 
diversified student bodies ethnically and culturally, declining or stagnating birth rates and 
aging populations in regions like Europe and North America have led to reduced 
domestic student enrolment, (UNESCO, 2023). A McKinsey (2020) analysis of US higher 
education trends publicised that growth in the sector is slowing, with undergraduate 
enrolment projected to decline significantly from 2025 onwards. Similarly, The Chronicle 
(Bauman, 2024) reports an anticipated "enrolment cliff", primarily in the US, with the 
college-going population expected to shrink by as much as 15% over the next five to ten 
years. According to The Chronicle, this decline stems from interconnected demographic 
trends, such as falling birth rates, aging populations, migration patterns, and economic 
shifts influencing education demand. For many institutions, particularly smaller or less 
competitive ones, declining enrolments translate into reduced tuition revenue, posing 
serious financial challenges.  
Competition in higher education is additionally fuelled by quality measures, particularly 
global rankings such as the QS World University Rankings and Times Higher Education 
(THE). According to Hazelkorn (2015), these rankings significantly influence university 
reputations and play a pivotal role in student decision-making. By evaluating institutions 
across metrics like teaching quality, research output, and internationalisation, rankings 
create strong incentives for universities to excel. High rankings not only attract students 
and faculty but can also secure research funding and valuable partnerships, driving 
 
 
13 
institutions to compete intensely for top positions. Elite universities such as Harvard and 
Oxford consistently lead these rankings, setting benchmarks that compel other 
institutions to enhance their performance in pursuit of similar recognition.  
As another megatrend, technological advancements are rapidly transforming teaching, 
learning, and administrative processes, requiring universities to embrace digital 
innovation. The global e-learning market is projected to grow to $457 billion by 2026, 
reflecting a significant shift toward technology-enabled education (Statista, 2023). 
Institutions increasingly use AI-driven analytics, learning management systems (LMS), 
and HR information systems (HRIS) to streamline operations and improve decision-
making. For example, Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT) incorporates artificial 
intelligence into both its academic programs and operational processes, setting a global 
benchmark for technology integration in higher education (MIT, 2003). This shift 
highlights the need for tech-savvy staff, digital literacy training, and data-driven strategies 
to enhance learning experience, institutional efficiency and effectiveness.  
These megatrends present significant challenges for higher education institutions (HEIs), 
compelling them to adapt their internal structures, processes, and strategies to navigate 
these evolving socio-economic conditions. While these shifts create opportunities for 
HEIs to expand their global presence and modernize their operations, they also bring 
complexities that demand careful consideration. How organisations respond to these 
changes has been the subject of extensive scholarly debate, particularly in the realms of 
strategic alignment, resource allocation, administrative processes and innovation. 
In general, scholarly debates focus on how organisations must adapt to balance global 
demands with local contexts, integrate technological advancements into their operations, 
and respond to shifting workforce and demographic trends. For HEIs, these discussions 
often circle around aligning institutional goals with broader socio-economic trends, 
developing sustainable funding models, and leveraging technology to enhance both 
teaching and research capacities. For example, a recurring theme is the tension between 
preserving academic freedom and autonomy while ensuring accountability and 
operational efficiency in an increasingly globalised and market-driven environment 
 
 
14 
(Altbach et al., 2009). The tension between academic freedom and accountability/ 
efficiency arises from the inherently different priorities and expectations associated with 
each concept. For example, academic freedom allows faculty and researchers to pursue 
knowledge, teaching, and research without external interference. It emphasizes 
creativity, intellectual exploration, and autonomy, which are crucial for fostering 
innovation and critical thinking. This freedom enables faculty to challenge prevailing 
norms and explore diverse ideas, even if they deviate from institutional or market 
priorities. In contrast, accountability and efficiency focus on measurable outcomes, 
standardised procedures, and alignment with strategic goals or market demands. These 
principles often require transparency, performance metrics, and adherence to institutional 
policies to ensure resources are used effectively and produce tangible results.  
Academic freedom allows faculty to pursue research and teaching that may not produce 
quick or easily measurable results, making it hard to fit into standardised performance 
metrics. Professors might also push back against policies like required teaching 
evaluations or research targets, viewing them as constraints on their professional 
autonomy. In other sectors, particularly the tech-industry, light-touch management has 
been used to balance flexibility with accountability through outcome-based metrics. While 
such models are occasionally proposed for the higher education sector, their relevance 
remains contested due to the distinct cultural norms, governance structures, and public 
responsibilities that define universities. 
In the Literature Review, diverse perspectives on how higher education can successfully 
adapt to these megatrends will be explored. Key arguments will be examined regarding 
the suitability of adopting corporate-like performance management frameworks, such as 
Strategic Human Resource Management models (Wright and McMahan, 1992), Human 
Capital Management (HCM), the Balanced Scorecard (Kaplan and Norton, 1992), to 
align institutional strategies with global trends. These examples represent a broader 
range of models that will be evaluated. Furthermore, the review will assess whether such 
frameworks are appropriate for the unique mission and structure of HEIs, given their 
emphasis on academic, research, and community outcomes rather than purely financial 
metrics. The aim is to provide a comprehensive understanding of how Schiller 
 
 
15 
International University (SIU) can respond strategically to these socio-economic changes 
while addressing the specific challenges and opportunities they present. This critical 
assessment will form the basis for understanding the role of SHRM and performance 
management tools in navigating the complexities of a rapidly evolving educational 
landscape.  
 
1.2. 
Background: The Role of Human Resource Management  
 
The megatrends mentioned above present significant challenges for Higher Education 
Institutions (HEI), forcing them to adapt internal structures and processes and respond 
quickly to remain competitive in the long-term (Panizzon & Barcellos, 2020).  
In this context, the Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD, 
2023) reports that universities across OECD countries allocate about two-thirds of their 
budgets to Human Resource Management (HRM), highlighting the central role of faculty, 
staff, and administrators in driving key areas such as teaching, research, and operations.  
By prioritizing HRM in their budgets, universities underscore the importance of recruiting, 
retaining, and developing skilled professionals who directly influence research quality, 
student satisfaction, and the institution’s overall reputation (Hunter et al., 2023; Asio & 
Riego de Dios, 2019; Pongton & Suntrayuth, 2019). Figure 2 provides an overview of 
university expenditures with salaries as the biggest portion: 
 
 
16 
 
Figure 2: Share of Total University Expenditures, by Type, Selected Years, 2000-01 to 2020-21 (Source: Usher, 2022) 
Conversely, this heavy spending on salaries and benefits creates challenges because 
these costs are not easy to adjust. Salaries are often tied to long-term contracts, 
agreements, or legal obligations, which makes it difficult for universities to reduce or 
increase these expenses quickly. Over the past five years, U.S. universities have 
consistently spent 60-70% of their budgets on salaries and employee benefits (NCES, 
2023; College Board, 2023), showing how fixed these costs are. 
This rigidity makes it hard for universities to respond to financial challenges or take 
advantage of new opportunities. For example, if funding is reduced or the economy slows 
down, universities may need to freeze hiring, lay off staff, or make major cuts to reduce 
salary costs. On the other hand, if they want to grow quickly, hiring more staff means 
finding stable funding for long-term salary commitments. Public universities in the US, 
such as those in California have faced these issues, with rising salary costs, or labour 
costs being difficult to adjust to market conditions, and budget cuts forcing them to raise 
tuition, which can make education less affordable and affect student enrolment (California 
 
 
17 
State University Budget Office, 2021). While spending heavily on HRM highlights the 
critical role of people in universities, it also shows why strategic financial planning and 
flexible HR strategies, such as adjunct faculty or online-based positions, are needed to 
keep institutions sustainable.  
In this context, Strategic Human Resource Management (SHRM) plays a pivotal role in 
enabling universities to adapt to global trends by implementing aligned workforce 
strategies. SHRM is understood as a set of practices aligned with a corporate strategy to 
respond to both external environments and internal needs. It involves decision-making 
related to organisational objectives, connecting the people essential to implementing a 
strategy with the goal of improving performance and gaining a competitive advantage 
(Armstrong, 2014; Schuler et al., 2014; Barney, 1991; Snell et al., 2007). For instance, by 
strategically managing student and faculty mobility, SHRM helps universities attract top-
tier talent, including faculty, researchers, and students, ensuring they remain relevant in a 
highly competitive sector. Specifically, HR teams develop strategies to draw international 
students by simplifying visa processes, creating robust support systems, such as 
dedicated international offices, orientation programmes, or counselling services, and 
fostering inclusivity through cultural exchange events, or international student 
organisations (Wahab, 2024; Keränen, 2023). Similarly, incorporating faculty mobility into 
hiring and workforce succession plans preserves institutional knowledge and enhances 
global engagement. A well-structured HR function is also instrumental in supporting digital 
transformation, equipping institutions to leverage technology by ensuring that the 
workforce is equipped with the necessary skills, resources, and mind-set to adopt new 
technologies effectively, thus meeting operational demands effectively. 
Lozano (2006) underscores the importance of faculty and staff as key Critical Success 
Factors (CSFs) in higher education, noting their vital role in shaping university operations 
and driving institutional change through their day-to-day roles and strategic initiatives, 
such as enhancing teaching quality, or promoting organisational change. Salas-Vallina et 
al. (2021) further demonstrate that investing in employees enhances engagement and 
drives innovation — essential elements in academic settings. 
 
 
18 
Kaufmann (2015) emphasises that most business leaders acknowledge SHRM’s role in 
enhancing organisational value in a global context. Notable business leaders, such as 
Jeff Bezos (Amazon founder, 2010), Sheryl Sandberg (COO of Facebook, 2008), and 
Satya Nadella (CEO of Microsoft, 2019) emphasize the need to align HR practices with 
strategic goals, recognizing SHRM’s critical role in fostering innovation, inclusion, and 
long-term success. For instance, under Satya Nadella’s leadership, Microsoft underwent 
a significant cultural and strategic transformation. Nadella prioritized creating a "growth 
mind-set" culture — an HR-driven initiative that directly supported Microsoft’s broader 
strategic objectives of fostering innovation and improving collaboration. Nadella 
encouraged a shift from a competitive, ‘siloed’ environment to a collaborative one. HR 
played a central role in designing and implementing training programmes focused on 
leadership development and fostering inclusivity, creating a culture that valued learning 
and adaptability. Inclusive workplaces ensure that employees feel valued, respected, and 
empowered, which can lead to a higher work morale and improved retention. 
Additionally, Nadella championed diversity as a strategic imperative, tying it directly to 
the company’s performance metrics. “This culture [Microsoft's culture] needs to be a 
microcosm of the world we hope to create outside the company. One where builders, 
makers, and creators achieve great things. But, equally important, one where every 
individual can be their best self, where diversity of skin color, gender, religion, and sexual 
orientation is understood and celebrated” (Nadella, 2018). Diverse teams have been 
shown to make better decisions because they consider a broader range of perspectives 
and seem to resonate better with global markets, enabling them to connect with a 
broader customer base (Hunt, et al., 2018). HR implemented targeted recruitment 
initiatives and established accountability for diversity goals at all management levels, 
thus reinforcing the alignment between talent strategies and broader organisational 
priorities. These SHRM initiatives led to measurable improvements in organisational 
performance, with Microsoft’s market capitalization increasing significantly, from 
approximately $300 billion in 2014 to over $2 trillion by 2023. Employee satisfaction and 
engagement scores also improved, contributing to higher productivity and innovation. 
This example illustrates how aligning HR practices with strategic goals can drive 
 
 
19 
organisational growth, enhance employee engagement, and strengthen company culture. 
By fostering collaboration and inclusion while tying performance metrics to strategic 
outcomes, Microsoft demonstrated the tangible benefits of SHRM in a competitive, 
globalised market. 
Research highlights the importance of SHRM as a key driver of operational efficiency 
and academic excellence (Kettunen et al., 2022; Siddeg, 2021; Chopra & Gupta, 2020). 
Wright and McMahan (1992) argue that managing human resources strategically by 
aligning HR practices with institutional goals creates a competitive advantage. SHRM 
enables organisation to effectively align workforce strategies with institutional priorities 
through initiatives such as recruitment, training, and motivational programs. For example, 
if a university aims to increase research output, it may focus on recruiting faculty with 
strong research expertise in high-impact fields. Similarly, strategic training and 
development programs equip employees with the skills necessary to meet institutional 
objectives. For instance, a university transitioning to digital learning platforms might 
prioritize digital literacy training for faculty to ensure they can effectively integrate new 
technologies into teaching. Incentive structures can also be tailored to align with strategic 
goals. For example, faculty who integrate digital tools in innovative ways to improve 
student engagement or learning outcomes could be eligible for performance-based 
bonuses or stipends.  
The key idea is that HR practices are not managed in isolation but are strategically 
planned and executed to help the institution achieve its specific goals, for example, 
enhancing academic quality, improving student satisfaction, or achieving a specific 
accreditation. At the individual level, SHRM supports employee development through 
ongoing training and performance evaluations. At the institutional level, it links workforce 
performance with key metrics like research productivity and teaching effectiveness. As 
argued above, universities face increasing global competition, embracing diversity, 
international recruitment, so effective performance management becomes essential for 
maintaining relevance and excellence.  
 
 
20 
University leaders increasingly recognize the need for tools that measure and manage 
both organisational and individual performance, fostering continuous improvement and 
agility in an evolving educational setting (Witczak et al., 2015; Drucker, 2007; Kwiek, 
2015; Alach, 2017). In this context, performance evaluation, including performance 
management and measurement, has emerged as a critical mechanism for translating 
strategic priorities into measurable outcomes and aligning institutional practices with 
broader goals. Performance management refers to systematically monitoring, evaluating, 
and improving employee performance to align with organisational objectives. This 
process includes setting expectations, ongoing feedback, performance appraisals, and 
developing strategies to enhance individual and organisational outcomes (Armstrong, 
2014; Aguinis, 2009). Performance measurement refers to the process of assessing and 
quantifying the outcomes of an individual, team or organisation in achieving their goals. It 
involves the use of Key Performance Indicators (KPIs), metrics, or benchmarks to assess 
progress and identify improvement areas (Neely et al., 1995). Performance evaluation 
assesses how well individuals, teams, or the organisation meet performance 
expectations. It often combines insights from both management and measurement 
processes, using Critical Success Factors (CSFs) and KPIs. In an organisation, CSFs 
help focus on the most important goals, while KPIs track progress and evaluate efforts in 
those areas (Panizzon and Barcellos, 2020). Together, they ensure clear priorities and 
accountability for achieving objectives.  
However, without robust performance measurement systems, HEIs lack the data-driven 
insights needed to identify strengths, address weaknesses, and drive improvements for 
individuals as well as for the entire organisation. Furthermore, technological 
advancements, demographic shifts, and evolving student expectations require adaptive 
strategies. Performance measurement systems can provide HEIs with the agility to stay 
relevant and responsive to these external pressures.  
For instance, the COVID-19 pandemic has heightened performance-related challenges in 
higher education, including financial strain, declining student enrolment, and the rapid 
shift to digital learning, making strategic performance management even more important 
(Roberts, 2019; Ali, 2020; Williamson et al., 2020). In this context, evaluating employee 
 
 
21 
performance — particularly among academic and administrative staff — has become 
increasingly critical, as individual contributions directly influence broader organisational 
performance and the institution’s ability to adapt and thrive in a rapidly changing 
environment. Institutions had to not only manage financial and operational efficiency but 
also ensure they were prepared to adapt quickly to unforeseen circumstances, like to 
pandemic’s disruptions. A study by De Vos et al. (2020) highlights that organisations with 
robust SHRM practices, such as flexible work policies and digital upskilling, were better 
able to transition to remote work environments. Additionally, companies that had pre-
existing crisis management and contingency plans embedded in their SHRM strategies 
demonstrated greater operational continuity during COVID-19, for example their ability to 
rapidly reallocate resources (Society for Human Resource Management [SHRM], 2023). 
Carnevale and Hatak (2020) found that organisations with SHRM practices, such as 
transparent communication channels, had a more engaged workforce during the 
pandemic. Universities and businesses that implemented SHRM-driven well-being 
initiatives reported better student and employee satisfaction levels during remote 
operations. In contrast, organisations without SHRM frameworks often struggled with 
inconsistent policies, insufficient digital infrastructure, and lower employee engagement, 
which exacerbated challenges during the pandemic.  
The emphasis on HR in academic institutions significantly influences how performance 
management is applied. Institutions with strong HR systems are better equipped to 
implement comprehensive performance management practices that foster engagement, 
professional development and alignment with institutional goals. Conversely, those with 
weaker HR systems may struggle to adopt practices that effectively enhance 
performance. 
Despite growing recognition of the link between HRM performance evaluation and 
organisational success, significant gaps remain in understanding how personnel 
performance evaluation directly influences university outcomes, as stated by Allui (2016). 
While corporate performance evaluation practices have been extensively studied, 
universities present unique challenges, as their success metrics, such as educational 
outcomes, research quality, and community engagement, differ substantially from those 
 
 
22 
in the corporate sector. Existing performance evaluation models, designed for business 
contexts, often fail to address the complexities of higher education. 
Addressing this gap is essential, as universities depend heavily on skilled faculty and 
staff as well as streamlined processes to achieve their strategic objectives. Effective 
performance evaluation offers actionable insights into individual contributions, aligning 
faculty and staff efforts with broader institutional strategies. Robust performance 
evaluation systems potentially provide significant benefits to universities at both 
individual and institutional levels. For individuals, they set clear expectations, identify 
areas for development, and enhance accountability. At the institutional level, these 
systems offer transparency into how investments in human resources contribute to key 
outcomes, such as improved teaching quality, increased research productivity, and 
enhanced student experiences. This dual focus fosters a balanced approach to 
performance management by aligning personal accountability with effective team 
collaboration. It also helps identify talent gaps, reward high performers, and provide 
professional development opportunities, ensuring universities attract and retain top talent 
in a competitive environment. 
However, these positive outcomes are not guaranteed. The success of performance 
evaluation systems depends on several factors, such as gaining support from the 
institution, aligning the system with strategic goals, and making sure it fits the unique 
needs of higher education. If the system is poorly designed or applied, it can lead to 
resistance, confusion, or even lower morale among faculty and staff.  
This research focuses on standardizing and optimizing HR practices at Schiller 
International University (SIU) through the application of Performance Management 
Frameworks. By aligning organisational strategies with performance indicators, the goal 
is to ensure that institutional activities contribute effectively to strategic objectives while 
fostering continuous improvement (Kaplan & Norton, 1996; Armstrong, 2014). 
The long-term sustainability of this emphasis on human resources in higher education 
remains uncertain, as technological advancements, particularly in artificial intelligence 
 
 
23 
(AI), are currently transforming the landscape. This issue will be revisited in the 
discussion chapter. 
 
1.3. 
Motivation of the Research 
 
As seen above, the higher education sector is deeply interconnected with global markets, 
policies, and societal shifts. A change in one region (e.g., government funding 
programmes or changed visa regulations) can ripple across the world, affecting student 
mobility, faculty recruitment, and funding. 
In the case of a small higher education institution with four international campuses 
(located in the USA, Spain, France, and Germany), such changes can significantly 
influence its operations. For instance, factors like stricter visa regulations pose significant 
hurdles to both student mobility and faculty recruitment. For students, visa restrictions 
can disrupt the seamless transition between campuses, a key feature of SIU's global 
educational model. For faculty, such regulations complicate international hiring. 
Moreover, these restrictions can reduce diversity, a cornerstone of SIU's identity.  
Founded in 1964, Schiller International University (SIU) has always prioritized a 
globalised educational approach, recognizing that international perspectives are key to 
addressing contemporary challenges and fostering a "globally literate, interculturally 
competent citizenry" (Palmer Group Investors, 2012). In 2006, SIU was acquired by a 
U.S.-based investor group, transitioning the institution into a private, for-profit 
organisation with headquarters in the United States. In 2014, SIU entered into a 
cooperation agreement with a British university, enabling students to earn both European 
and American degrees through its Bachelor’s and Master’s programmes. While this 
partnership added value to the institution’s offerings, it also increased administrative 
burdens and the need for rigorous alignment of academic and administrative standards 
across campuses. Without a strong, centralised HR and performance management 
system, the institution struggled to manage these additional responsibilities effectively. 
 
 
24 
In 2017, I assumed the role of Campus Director at SIU’s German campus in Heidelberg. 
At the start of this research in 2020, SIU employed approximately 14 full-time faculty 
members and 56 administrative staff across its locations. In addition, adjunct faculty were 
part of a broader teaching pool, typically delivering one or two courses per academic 
year. Collectively, this team supported a student body of around 1,300, including online 
learners. At that time, human resource management (HRM) had not been systematically 
implemented at the institutional level. Each campus operated largely independently, with 
HR processes tailored to local needs — even though SIU promoted cross-campus 
mobility and offered dual degree opportunities. This decentralised approach undermined 
consistency and efficiency, resulting in misaligned HR practices that did not fully support 
the university’s broader strategic objectives. Key processes such as recruitment, on-
boarding, and professional development varied significantly between campuses, leading 
to disparities in staff competencies, experiences, and satisfaction levels. 
These inconsistencies affected not only internal operations but also weakened SIU’s 
ability to present a cohesive and competitive image to prospective students, faculty, and 
institutional partners. An overview of campus staffing figures is presented in Table 1:

The university’s organisational structure was poorly defined, with a mix of centralised and 
decentralised functions managed across different locations. For example, key leadership 
roles such as the President (based in Spain), Chief Financial Officer (CFO, based in the 
 
 
25 
USA), and Provost (based in the USA) were centrally staffed but geographically 
dispersed, making cohesive decision-making and strategic alignment more difficult. 
 
Figure 3: Organisational Chart Schiller International University SIU 2020 (Source: SIU Management) 
Although all branches are represented at senior management level, this locally dispersed 
leadership structure has created communication gaps and logistical inefficiencies, further 
weakening the institution's ability to implement coherent HR strategies and adapt to 
global trends. Meanwhile, HR practices, shaped primarily by the conditions and needs of 
individual campuses, result in inconsistencies and a lack of alignment. This fragmented 
structure has created significant challenges in unifying HR strategies and ensuring 
consistency across the institution. The absence of a centralised HR department further 
exacerbates these issues, contributing to operational inefficiencies, misaligned 
processes, and limited standardisation. Combined with cultural differences, this structural 
gap also reinforces broader organisational challenges, including a lack of transparency, 
unclear objectives, and undefined performance indicators — factors that hinder its ability 
to support and monitor progress toward its ambitious growth agenda. While this strategy 
 
 
26 
has been described as one of "immediate growth," it remains vague, lacking both 
concrete goals and actionable steps.  
Furthermore, the university faces distinct challenges across its campus locations, each 
requiring tailored responses. For example, in Germany, the expansion of English-
speaking programs at other universities has increased competition, offering more options 
to international students and leading to a decline in the university’s campus population. 
Meanwhile, in the US, accreditation and qualification standards fall short of those at 
competing institutions, affecting the university's competitive position. With respect to 
HRM strategies, U.S. HRM is performance-focused, while European models prioritize 
social responsibility and labour rights (Andolsek & Stebe, 2005). These region-specific 
challenges necessitate strategic HR interventions. The university must adapt its HR 
strategies to address increasing competition in Germany and improve accreditation 
standards in the U.S., etc., ensuring that both academic and administrative functions 
align with the institution's overall goals.  Given the significant cultural, legal, and 
operational differences between Spain, France, Germany, and the United States, fully 
centralising HR functions across all campuses is unlikely to be feasible or effective. 
Labour laws, working time directives, employee representation requirements, and 
recruitment norms differ widely. For example, works councils are mandatory in Germany 
and France but not in the U.S. or Spain, and national accreditation and qualification 
standards vary. However, SIU could pursue a hybrid HR model: one that establishes a 
shared strategic HR framework and a set of global principles, such as on performance 
evaluation, talent development, and leadership expectations, while allowing for flexible, 
locally adapted implementation in line with national regulations and campus-specific 
needs. This balanced approach could improve coordination and standardisation without 
ignoring the practical constraints of a multinational higher education environment. 
More broadly, facing that student enrolment is either stagnating or declining ("enrolment 
cliff", as described earlier) and competition intensifying, the university leadership intents 
to closely assess institutional performance. This includes not only financial and 
administrative factors but also, and especially, personnel performance, as human 
resources are the university's most critical asset.  
 
 
27 
SIU faces critical challenges related to regulatory pressures, fragmented HR practices 
without a dedicated HR person, a lack of strategic coordination, and the operational 
complexity of managing international campuses. As a result, performance cannot be 
systematically assessed or improved, administrative processes remain inconsistent 
across the four campuses, and valuable staff development opportunities are missed. 
These issues hinder SIU’s ability to respond effectively to external demands and to 
realize its full potential as a globally oriented university. At the same time, higher 
education is undergoing profound change. Institutions must navigate rising expectations 
for accountability, digital transformation, global competition, and demographic shifts. For 
a university like SIU, the question is not simply how to improve HR operations, but how to 
do so in a way that is responsive, adaptive, and strategically aligned. 
To address these challenges, this research explores whether a structured HRM 
framework can better align SIU’s strategic goals with HR practices across its global 
campuses. It does not assume that applying corporate-style HR models will automatically 
succeed in a university context. On the contrary, higher education presents unique 
challenges that may limit the usefulness of such models, including a strong emphasis on 
academic autonomy, complex governance structures, and diverse stakeholder demands. 
After assessing several models, the Human Resource Balanced Scorecard (HR-BSC) 
was selected not as a predetermined solution, but as a tool worth testing due to its 
potential to link HR activities with institutional objectives. While widely used in the 
business sector, its relevance to higher education, especially within decentralised, 
international institutions like SIU, remains largely untested. The research critically 
examines if and how such a tool can clarify priorities and support strategic alignment. 
In doing so, it seeks to contribute to a broader understanding of how HRM practices can 
evolve to meet the complex realities of contemporary higher education, especially in 
globally dispersed settings.  
 
 
 
 
28 
1.4. 
Identification of Key Academic Concepts (and frameworks) 
 
This section outlines the conceptual framework that structures the DBA thesis, providing 
a foundation for the research context, key concepts, and variables. It also connects the 
existing literature with the research question, ensuring alignment between theory and the 
study's focus.  
Positioning of the study: 
This research interlinks three literatures: Human Resource Management, Performance 
Evaluation, University Performance as shown in Figure 4:  
 
Figure 4: Positioning of the Study (Source: Author) 
This approach ensures the relationship between strategic HR practices and institutional 
outcomes is well-defined, making it easier to assess their impact on achieving 
organisational goals. This integration enhances the understanding of how HRM drives 
 
 
29 
performance in academic institutions. To position the study effectively, it is essential to 
clearly define and contextualize the key concepts linking these areas. 
Human Resource Management 
HRM focuses on managing an organisation’s workforce to meet operational needs, while 
SHRM takes a more strategic approach, aligning HR activities such as recruitment, 
development, and engagement with broader institutional objectives. This research 
explores how SHRM aligns SIU’s HR activities (recruitment, development, alignment, 
evaluation) with broader university objectives to enhance performance. The distinction 
between HRM and SHRM will be explored further in subsequent sections.  
Performance Evaluation 
Performance evaluation in higher education involves systematically assessing both 
individual and organisational performance to ensure alignment with strategic objectives. 
While traditional systems often focus on output metrics, this research explores how the 
Human Resources Balanced Scorecard (HR-BSC), as a strategic tool, can evaluate and 
enhance performance across financial, operational, and qualitative dimensions. It 
integrates HR metrics like staff development, engagement, and retention with the 
institution’s mission, offering a comprehensive evaluation framework. Incorporating HR-
related Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) into the BSC framework could help the 
university to assess the impact of strategic HR practices on broader outcomes. KPIs are 
specific, measurable metrics tracking progress toward strategic objectives. In higher 
education, KPIs may include student satisfaction scores, research output, staff 
engagement, and financial health.  
University Performance  
University performance refers to how well higher education institutions meet their goals, 
which can include a mix of academic excellence, research productivity, student 
satisfaction, and financial sustainability. This research suggests that university 
performance can be significantly influenced by the effective integration of HR strategies 
 
 
30 
with performance management systems. In this context, university performance 
encompasses not only traditional academic and research outcomes but also broader 
measures such as faculty and staff satisfaction, operational efficiency, and the ability to 
adapt to external challenges such as demographic changes or shifts in education 
delivery models. This expanded view of university performance aligns with the balanced 
scorecard approach, which ensures that performance is measured holistically across 
multiple dimensions.  
Expanding on these concepts within the research framework will clarify how strategic 
HRM practices and performance management tools interact to influence university 
performance. 
As previously noted, megatrends continue to drive interest in performance indicators to 
facilitate organisational success, thereby highlighting the importance of SHRM, also in 
higher education. SHRM ultimately enables the alignment of individual contributions with 
institutional objectives, positioning human resources as a strategic asset for long-term 
growth and competitiveness.  
At SIU, where HR-related expenditures account for 61% of the overall budget (see 
Appendix A), the lack of a strategic HR plan or a dedicated HR team highlights a notable 
deficiency. Implementing SHRM could enhance the return on these investments by 
improving core outcomes such as teaching quality and institutional reputation, while also 
reinforcing the strategic value of human capital. 
To better understand the complexity of university's case and the research focus of this 
thesis, Figure 5 illustrates the key performance influencers on the internationally 
operating higher education institution, SIU: 
 
 
31 
 
Figure 5: Performance Influences of the University SIU (Source: Author) 
SIU’s overall performance is shaped by external market conditions (light blue), internal 
structures and business processes (dark blue), and the absence of standardised HRM 
practices (light orange). These external and internal factors significantly influence HRM 
at SIU by affecting how HR strategies are developed and aligned with organisational 
goals. As a service-oriented organisation, SIU views its future success and 
organisational development as heavily dependent on employee performance. Therefore, 
effectively managing these influences allows HRM to play a key role in the institution’s 
success in a competitive, globalised educational environment. External factors such as 
globalisation, competition, and customer expectations, combined with internal factors like 
processes and financial structures, necessitate an adaptive and strategic approach to 
HRM, focused on long-term goals to support SIU’s mission and performance.  
A 2019 OECD benchmarking review highlighted that, despite higher education systems 
being highly internationalised and globally competitive, there is limited international 
comparison on how internal processes, such as teaching, learning, research, and 
 
 
32 
funding, are organized (OECD, 2019). This lack of standardisation is particularly relevant 
for SIU, which operates across multiple campuses without a centralised HR department 
or structured HR processes. 
To address SIU’s specific challenges, eight prominent SHRM and performance 
management frameworks were systematically reviewed and evaluated. The selection 
process began with a comprehensive literature review to identify models with the 
potential to align HRM with institutional performance. Among the frameworks considered 
were Wright and McMahan’s SHRM model, the Human Capital Management (HCM) 
framework, and the Balanced Scorecard (BSC), along with others such as High-
Performance Work Systems (HPWS), the AMO framework, the HR Value Chain, Ulrich’s 
HR Model, and Guest’s HRM-Performance model. 
All eight frameworks were assessed using predefined criteria, including relevance, 
comprehensiveness, usability, flexibility, empirical validation, and practical support. This 
structured approach ensured a robust foundation for identifying a framework capable of 
guiding SIU’s HR transformation in alignment with its broader strategic objectives. The 
inclusion of multiple models ensures a well-rounded analysis, allowing for a nuanced 
understanding of how different strategic HR approaches can support SIU’s long-term 
growth and competitiveness. While each model offers distinct insights, emphasis was 
placed on those most applicable to SIU’s strategic and operational context. 
After this systematic evaluation process, the Balanced Scorecard emerged as the most 
suitable framework. The BSC has been adapted for HRM to align HR strategies with 
organisational goals (Yeung & Berman, 1997; Klepić, 2019; Boudlaie et al., 2020). It 
stood out for its comprehensive nature and proven track record in aligning organisational 
activities with strategic goals, while also being flexible enough to be adapted to the 
unique HR needs of a university setting. The BSC was particularly attractive due to its 
focus on non-financial measures, such as internal processes, stakeholder satisfaction, 
and learning and growth — key areas of concern for SIU. Stone et al. (2020) see HR 
facing challenges in developing a divisional BSC, particularly in addressing employee-
related needs across areas like customer service and sales. HR can pioneer BSC 
 
 
33 
implementation by integrating business processes and customer-oriented services, 
contributing to a unified scorecard. As a service function, HR uses the BSC to assesses 
talent acquisition, employee performance and engagement, and internal processes, 
aligning these with organisational strategy. Implementing the BSC in HR often requires a 
cultural shift, where (HR) leaders need to effectively communicate the benefits to gain 
support from stakeholders (Rühle & Wagner, 2017; Kaplan & Norton, 2001). 
In the context of SIU, this involves tailoring the BSC to align HR functions, such as 
recruitment, development, and performance management, with yet to be defined 
strategic objectives. For example, the learning and growth perspective could track HR 
metrics like staff training and development, ensuring these activities directly support long-
term institutional goals like academic excellence and student satisfaction. Meanwhile, the 
internal processes perspective could evaluate the efficiency of HR workflows, fostering 
continuous improvement. This structured framework enables SIU to consistently 
measure HR outcomes while ensuring these efforts contribute to its broader goals. 
The research draws on theories from strategic management, performance management, 
and organisational behaviour, all of which are explored in depth in the literature review. 
Its focus lays on exploring the applicability and benefits of an HR-BSC in HE, aiming to 
identify HRM practices that support evaluating employee performance within a goal-
driven framework. In this thesis, SIU, a multi-campus private university is investigated, 
using the BSC as a performance evaluation tool applied to HR. Kaplan and Norton’s BSC 
is customized to SIU’s context, linking strategic objectives with HR practices. The tool’s 
value will be assessed by analysing the university's HR situation before and after 
implementation. To effectively manage this process, Action Research will be employed 
as the guiding methodology.  
Action Research, as defined by Lewin (1946), is a cyclical process involving planning, 
action, observation, and reflection, aimed at both solving a problem and generating 
knowledge. In the context of this study, the implementation of the HRM performance tool 
will be treated as an intervention — a strategic action introduced to align HR practices 
with strategic goals and enhance organisational performance. The Action Research 
 
 
34 
approach allows for the active participation of stakeholders, in this case, leadership, 
faculty, and administrative staff, throughout the intervention.  
By continually gathering feedback, adjusting the tool, and re-assessing the outcomes, 
this methodology ensures that the HR-BSC remains responsive to the university’s 
evolving needs. This cyclical process of reflection and action is particularly valuable in 
complex academic environments, where the interaction between staff and organisational 
goals is dynamic and multifaceted.  
Incorporating Action Research into this study provides a meaningful framework for 
assessing the effects of the intervention. It facilitates real-time data collection on the 
tool’s impact on HR practices, organisational alignment, and performance improvement, 
making it possible to compare outcomes before and after the HR-BSC is introduced. 
Moreover, Action Research emphasizes the continuous improvement of both the 
intervention and the research process itself (Reason & Bradbury, 2001), ensuring that 
the HR-BSC is not only implemented but also refined and adapted based on empirical 
findings and stakeholder feedback. Thus, this Action Research-driven intervention 
introduces new processes, metrics, and cultural shifts within the university, contributing 
meaningfully to the field of HRM in higher education by demonstrating the utility of an 
HR-BSC as a performance management tool in this sector. 
Initially, interviews with university stakeholders will establish the baseline HRM status. 
After several months of BSC implementation, a second round of interviews will evaluate 
its impact. The outcomes will provide insights into the effectiveness of the HR-BSC in 
enhancing HRM within the field of higher education. 
 
1.5. 
General Research Question and Objectives 
 
Actively measuring organisational and employee performance is critical for improving the 
effectiveness, competitiveness and long-term sustainability of higher education 
institutions (Avenali et al., 2023).  
 
 
35 
As discussed in this introduction, megatrends such as globalisation, demographic shifts, 
and technological advancements have intensified the need for universities to align their 
strategies with human resource management practices to remain competitive. SHRM 
offers mechanisms to reach this alignment, but its successful implementation requires 
robust systems for measuring individual and organisational performance. As discussed 
above, the HR-BSC was identified as a leading method for performance measurement. 
While its efficacy in improving performance has been well-documented in corporate 
settings, its application in the higher education (HE) sector remains underexplored. As 
seen above, the HE sector presents unique challenges, including the need to balance 
academic freedom, address resistance to change, navigate standardisation efforts, etc. 
Many institutions, including Schiller International University (SIU) — the focus of this 
investigation — have yet to fully embrace SHRM, making it a critical area of study. These 
considerations form the basis of the central research question (RQ) of this thesis:  
"Does the implementation of a strategic and structured Human Resource 
Management system improve the performance of SIU?" 
The research question referring to the drive to improve performance is built on the 
tensions arising from growing competition, international coordination and local adaptation 
of HR policies and practices. Hence, there is a growing emphasis on not only enhancing 
the attractiveness and scientific output of universities but also on improving the efficiency 
of resource allocation (Avenali et al., 2023; Boon et al., 2019; Hoyer and Gilb, 2006). 
After comparing and evaluating several HR performance frameworks, the Balanced 
Scorecard seems to be the best approach to address the research question. Adapted to 
focus on HR metrics like faculty development and operational efficiency, it could enable 
clear, measurable objectives while respecting the university’s decentralized culture 
(Kettunen et al., 2022; Smith et al., 2021). By promoting transparency and structured 
feedback, the HR-BSC can support improved performance in often-overlooked areas, 
confirming its potential strategic value (Horváth & Seiter, 2013; Kallenberg, 2018; Smith 
et al., 2021).  
 
 
36 
In order to generalise beyond the specific case of SIU, the following subsidiary questions 
and objectives will be explored: 
• 
Does the implementation of an HR-BSC at SIU influence individual and/or 
           organisational performance — or does it show limited or no measurable effect on  
           either? 
• 
Does the HR-BSC framework influence key HR functions (such as recruitment,  
           development, and resource allocation) within the university, and if so which ones? 
This second question aims to evaluate the mechanisms by which the HR-BSC improves 
HR processes and operational efficiency. It allows for an understanding of specific HR 
metrics and their alignment with strategic goals.  
Following the research questions, the overall research aim is to get a better 
comprehension of the relationship of human resource management and performance in 
an international university setting. The main objective of this study is to determine how 
strategic HR management, and the implementation of an HR Scorecard can assist the 
process of improving higher-education performance. Furthermore, the research attempts 
to achieve the following objectives:  
• 
Demonstrate how aligning HR metrics with strategic objectives creates a more 
cohesive and actionable framework for evaluating performance at SIU.  
• 
Integrate two key business management domains, strategic HR management 
(SHRM) and organisational performance improvement, by testing the HR-BSC 
model at SIU.  
Central to this research is the idea that aligning HR practices with institutional goals 
creates a feedback loop where HR strategies and performance measurement reinforce 
each other to drive grow and effectiveness. The underlying hypothesis is that alignment 
ensures organisational resources, particularly human capital, are directed toward 
strategic priorities, fostering efficiency, innovation, and accountability, resulting in 
improved performance. This hypothesis is grounded in strategic human resource 
management (SHRM) theory, which posits that integrated HR systems contribute to 
 
 
37 
improved organisational performance by aligning people management with long-term 
strategic goals (Wright & McMahan, 2011; Becker & Huselid, 2006). Misalignment, on the 
other hand, can lead to fragmented efforts, inefficiencies, and missed opportunities for 
achieving long-term objectives. 
At SIU, the HR-BSC is intended to form part of the broader corporate strategy by laying 
the groundwork for a centralised Human Resources department. 
The research question will be revisited and refined following the literature review and 
addressed in the methodology chapter. Following that, SIU’s organisational structure is 
analysed regarding its BSC suitability.  
 
1.6. 
Expected Contribution  
 
The purpose of the research is to develop and implement a performance evaluation tool, 
based on the Balanced Scorecard (BSC) by Kaplan and Norton (1992), for HRM for the 
case university and to observe if the implementation is beneficial for the organisation. 
The implementation of this HRM performance tool at SIU serves as a comprehensive 
intervention aimed at transforming HR practices, aligning them with strategic goals, and 
enhancing overall organisational performance.  
As mentioned earlier, in order to get an in-depth understanding of the topic, a thorough 
literature review clarifies the key academic themes, theories, concepts and frameworks 
of strategic human resource management (SHRM) and performance management, 
keeping the higher education sector in mind. Additionally, the review identifies potential 
gaps in literature concerning HRM and performance management in higher education.  
In this way, the thesis makes a dual contribution to both research and practice. From a 
research perspective, the thesis addresses a notable gap in the literature by critically 
examining how strategic HRM and performance evaluation systems can be adapted to 
the unique context of multi-campus universities. While corporate HRM performance 
models have been widely studied, their application in higher education remains 
 
 
38 
underexplored. By focusing on this sector, the thesis deepens understanding of how 
HRM tools, such as the HR-BSC, can address the distinct challenges of a university. 
Additionally, it integrates findings into a multilevel theoretical framework, offering new 
insights into the interplay between strategic alignment, individual contributions, and 
organisational performance in higher education. 
From a practical perspective, the thesis provides actionable recommendations for 
university leadership and HR professionals managing a multi-campus institution. By 
identifying deficiencies like inconsistent HR practices, misalignment with goals, and weak 
performance metrics, it proposes evidence-based strategies to address these issues.  
Managing performance in higher education requires adapting to global trends while 
improving internal operations. SIU must develop systems that support both strategic 
goals and responsiveness to change. HR metrics like retention, development, and 
satisfaction indicate operational health and influence outcomes such as teaching quality 
and student experience. This research tests whether aligning HR with strategy — via the 
HR-BSC — can improve performance. For example, to boost student satisfaction, SIU 
can link HR KPIs like mentoring frequency to strategic goals. 
Although the focus is on SIU, the findings may offer transferable insights for other 
globally oriented universities seeking to implement performance management systems 
that balance local and institutional needs. 
In the context of higher education, institutional success depends on the performance of 
faculty, staff, and administrators. By tying individual and collective contributions to 
broader goals like academic excellence, student satisfaction, and operational efficiency, 
the HR-BSC provides a potential practical tool for sustainable improvement. If 
successful, this study will contribute to a deeper understanding of how HRM tools and 
practices influence long-term university performance by offering a model for strategic 
HRM in higher education, with potential relevance beyond SIU. 
 
 
 
39 
1.7. 
Outline of Thesis 
 
In this thesis, I address the awareness gap in performance evaluation and HRM within 
higher education leadership, offering a foundation for further detailed research. I draw 
upon and contribute to various research streams in organisational and management 
literature, focusing specifically on how HR performance evaluation occurs in academic 
institutions and its potential influence on institutional performance. 
The structure is divided into six interconnected chapters, each contributing an essential 
part to the overarching theoretical framework (section1.4). With this framework, I will 
illustrate the relationship between HR performance evaluation and institutional success in 
higher education. In each chapter, I explore key elements that build toward a 
comprehensive understanding of HRM in the academic sector. In particular, in this thesis, 
I examine factors like performance measurement tools, the unique challenges of 
decentralised university structures, and the cultural context of academia, contributing to a 
holistic approach to HRM and performance evaluation in universities: 
Chapter two reviews the literature on HRM and performance management, both in 
general and specific to the higher education sector. It provides a theoretical background 
for the dissertation. As part of this review, several HR performance tools were assessed. 
The chapter was continually updated as the understanding of the topic evolved 
throughout the research process. 
Chapter three covers the Balanced Scorecard (BSC) model by Kaplan and Norton (1992) 
as a framework for performance management in HRM and higher education. After an 
introduction to the theory of the BSC, the requirements and consequences of 
implementing the tool within personnel management are explored. 
Chapter four is an overview of the thesis’ methodology and outlines the overall plan and 
structure by explaining the research design and describing the research questions and 
process of answering them in detail. It is covering the case study and intervention part 
which was developing and implementing a BSC for HRM practices related to a university 
by using qualitative methods to discover any changes and improvements. The primary 
 
 
40 
goal of this chapter is to provide a clear and comprehensive explanation of how the 
thesis was conducted. 
Chapter five is an analysis of the data of the research study by presenting the methods 
used to analyse the collected data and interpreting the results. In it the rigor of the 
research is demonstrated and a clear understanding of how the research questions are 
addressed is provided. 
Chapter six concludes the thesis by advancing a theoretical framework on performance 
management in HRM in HE and outlining the contribution to theory, directions for future 
research, limitations, as well as some implications of the findings for practice. 
 
 
41 
Chapter 2 
 
2. Literature Review – General Overview  
 
This chapter explores and links key themes relevant to the thesis topic, offering a critical 
review of existing literature on the role of Strategic Human Resource Management 
(SHRM) in performance evaluation, both in general and within academic institutions. The 
primary aim is to investigate how performance, particularly in the field of HRM, might be 
measured and improved, with a specific focus on the higher education sector. 
The chapter is divided into three main sections. The first provides a broad overview of 
the literature on SHRM and performance management, establishing a theoretical 
foundation. The second narrows the focus to the evaluation of HR performance within 
higher education. The final section evaluates and considers the suitability of different HR 
performance frameworks for the specific organisational context of SIU. Selecting an 
appropriate framework is a critical step, as it shapes how HR activities are interpreted, 
measured, and potentially aligned with strategic goals. 
Subsequent sections highlight the contributions of key scholars in SHRM and 
performance management, providing insights into current thinking and practical 
applications. This review also examines the role of performance frameworks in the 
development of Key Performance Indicators (KPIs), which may support the alignment of 
HR activities with institutional priorities. Rather than assuming effectiveness, this chapter 
seeks to understand whether and how such frameworks can offer a structured means of 
improving both individual development and broader organisational outcomes. The 
literature is critically analysed to explore the potential, but also the limitations, of linking 
HRM strategies with performance management in higher education settings. 
Moreover, the chapter explores the increasing interest among higher education 
institutions in measurable performance factors linked to human resources. As institutions 
face increasing pressure to demonstrate accountability and efficiency, HR functions are 
 
 
42 
being evaluated more rigorously based on their strategic contribution and measurable 
outcomes. This trend has prompted the development of structured approaches to staff 
qualification standards, performance tracking, and long-term strategic HR planning.  
In addition, the literature review examines key market trends shaping the higher 
education landscape — such as internationalisation, evolving stakeholder expectations, 
technological advancements, and shifting funding models. These external pressures 
have increased the demand for data-driven, performance-oriented HR practices that can 
adapt to a more competitive and complex global environment. To enhance the review’s 
practical relevance, examples of universities known for their performance management 
are included. These cases demonstrate how theoretical models are applied in real-world 
academic settings and offer valuable insights into how performance management can 
effect institutional success.  
 
2.1. 
Strategic Human Resource Management and Performance Management 
 
This chapter reviews the literature on SHRM and performance management, exploring 
their theoretical foundations, practical applications, and relevance to improving 
institutional outcomes in higher education. 
 
2.1.1. History of HRM and SHRM: An Overview 
 
The foundations of what is now known as Human Resource Management (HRM) can be 
traced back to the industrialization of the 19th century, when factories began to 
implement more structured approaches to managing workers. However, the term HRM 
itself only gained prominence during the mid- to late-20th century, as technological 
advancements, evolving social values, and shifting economic conditions prompted more 
formalized and strategic approaches to workforce management. Today, HRM 
encompasses key functions such as recruitment, training, compensation, employee 
relations, and workplace health and safety. 
 
 
43 
HRM advanced significantly in the U.S. during the 1960s and 1970s, driven by the need 
for a large, skilled workforce in response to economic growth and increased competition. 
New labour laws, such as the Equal Pay Act of 1963, the Civil Rights Act of 1964, and 
Title IX of the Education Amendments of 1972, aimed to protect workers' rights and 
promote equality. These laws highlighted the need for more sophisticated HR practices 
to comply with legal requirements and foster a fair workplace. The focus shifted from 
traditional personnel management, which was primarily administrative and reactive, to a 
more strategic and comprehensive approach in response to the evolving complexities of 
managing people within organisations (McGregor, 1960). 
Strategic Human Resource Management (SHRM), by contrast, represents an evolution 
of traditional HRM. It emerged in the 1980s, emphasizing the alignment of HR practices 
with an organisation’s long-term strategic objectives. While HRM tends to focus on 
administrative efficiency and day-to-day people management, SHRM takes a broader, 
more proactive role, integrating HR decisions with the overall business strategy to 
enhance organisational performance and sustainability.
