44 
The emergence of SHRM in the 1980s marked a turning point. It emphasized long-term 
planning, performance management, and organisational development as key tools for 
achieving competitive advantage. SHRM encompasses practices such as human capital 
management, corporate social responsibility, and performance alignment (Armstrong, 
2006, 2014). This shift underscored the strategic role of HR in driving performance and 
sustainability. 
In 1992, Wright and McMahan expanded the SHRM concept by emphasizing its role in 
achieving competitive advantage. They argued that HR should not only support 
operational functions but also cultivate distinctive organisational capabilities by aligning 
HR practices with strategic goals. Subsequent scholars like Arthur and Boyles (2007), 
MacDuffie (1995), and Delery and Doty (1996) further explored SHRM by investigating 
high-performance work systems (HPWS) and their impact on outcomes. Huselid (1995) 
and Becker & Huselid (1998) focused on the strategic design of specific practices like 
recruitment, compensation, and appraisal systems. 
An example of SHRM in practice is Google’s use of the Objectives and Key Results 
(OKR) framework. By making OKRs transparent across the organisation, Google 
ensures that individual goals are aligned with company strategy, thereby enhancing both 
collaboration and accountability (Doerr, 2018). 
The distinction between HRM and SHRM is also important in international contexts. For 
institutions like SIU operating across different countries, managing cultural and legal 
diversity adds complexity. Traditional HRM functions may differ widely across countries, 
but SHRM requires a unified strategy that still allows for regional adaptations (Delery & 
Shaw, 2001; Ahammad, 2017).  
Research consistently links SHRM to improved organisational performance. Strategic 
alignment of HR practices has been shown to increase productivity, financial outcomes, 
employee satisfaction, and stakeholder engagement (Wright et al., 1994; Paauwe & Blok, 
2015). This thesis builds on that literature by investigating whether implementing a 
strategic HR performance system at SIU can lead to similar improvements in 
performance, transparency, and institutional effectiveness. 
 
 
45 
2.1.2. Conceptual Frameworks for SHRM: An Overview 
 
The field of SHRM is evolving alongside organisational changes. Over time, best 
practices have been developed and established (Perucci, 2018). These developments 
have transformed the human resource function from being reactive, dogmatic, and 
administrative to being proactive, pragmatic, and strategic (Boxall, 1996; Pattanayak, 
2020). SHRM is now viewed as a decision-making approach aligned with organisational 
objectives, connecting people essential for strategy implementation to ultimately gain a 
competitive advantage (Delaney et al., 2017; Armstrong, 2014; Schuler et al., 2014; 
Wunderer, 2011; Snell et al., 2007; Martin-Alcázar et al., 2005; Truss et al., 1994).  
This strategic approach is supported by a variety of conceptual frameworks and models 
that offer structured methods for understanding, designing, and implementing HR 
practices in alignment with strategic goals. These bases combine theories from various 
fields, including management, organisational behaviour, and economics, to explore the 
relationship between HRM practices and organisational performance. Four major models 
offer analytical bases for specific HRM components: the Fombrun model (1984), which 
focuses on four core functions — selection, appraisal, development, and rewards — and 
their interdependence; the Harvard model (1984), which incorporates multiple 
stakeholders and highlights the strategic importance of HRM; the Warwick model (1990), 
which examines HRM through five interconnected elements: outer context, inner context, 
business strategy, HRM context, and HRM content; and the Guest model (1997), which 
stresses the significance of employee engagement in achieving organisational outcomes.  
While these models underscore the strategic role of HRM in managing people, they fall 
short in offering mechanisms to measure and monitor HR’s specific contributions to 
overall business performance. 
Armstrong (2014) highlights several more general concepts, an extract is shown below:

Scholars continue to debate which concept adds the most value to HRM, particularly 
within the Best Fit vs. Best Practice debate (Purcell, 1999; Stavrou et al., 2010; Majovski, 
2016). For instance, can universities adopt a hybrid approach, tailoring HR practices to fit 
the specific demands of academic environments while incorporating universally 
recognized best practices (Ninnemann et al., 2020; Schuler & Jackson, 1987).  
Several frameworks complement the theories discussed and will be explored further in 
Chapter 2.3. These frameworks emphasize the strategic role HR plays in enhancing 
performance. By aligning human capital with institutional goals and responding to 
external pressures, they could offer HR professionals actionable strategies, addressing 
both internal operations and external challenges (Wright & McMahan, 1992; Becker & 
Huselid, 2006; Cascio & Boudreau, 2016). Becker and Huselid (2006) argue that 
organisations must first assess their current HR practices to identify areas where HR can 
better align with strategic goals.  
In the context of this research, this involves conducting a detailed analysis to understand 
the institution's specific needs and the state of its HR operations. These insights will be 
gathered through interviews, which will form the foundation for developing the specific 
HR performance management system. From this analysis, a SHRM framework can be 
selected, that best fits its unique context, ensuring that HR initiatives are closely 
integrated with broader institutional objectives (Becker & Huselid, 2006; Rossidis et al., 
2021). It will be tested, if this approach enables HR to play an active role in promoting the 
organisation’s long-term success and competitiveness.  
 
 
47 
2.1.3. SHRM Perspectives 
 
This section presents key theoretical perspectives on SHRM and their relevance to 
evaluating the implementation of a performance management tool within SIU. These 
perspectives offer conceptual viewpoints for analysing whether the HR-BSC can align 
with the university’s strategic objectives and improve HR-related outcomes in its 
complex, multi-campus environment. 
Delery and Doty (1996) identify three main SHRM perspectives — universalistic, 
contingency, and configurational — while Martin-Alcázar et al. (2005) later add a fourth, 
the contextual perspective. Together, they offer a comprehensive framework for 
understanding how HR practices affect individual and organisational performance: 
1. Universalistic Perspective: Assumes that certain best practices (e.g., training, 
performance appraisals) universally improve performance by fostering a skilled and 
motivated workforce (Pfeffer & Veiga, 1999; Brewster, 2017). 
2. Contingency Perspective: Argues that HR practices must align with an organisation's 
specific characteristics (e.g., size, structure, or strategy) to be effective (Jackson & 
Schuler, 1995; Wright et al., 2001). 
3. Configurational Perspective: Emphasizes internal consistency and synergy among 
HR practices. A well-aligned HR system can produce stronger outcomes than 
individual practices in isolation (McDuffie, 1995). 
4. Contextual Perspective: Focuses on adapting HRM to both internal dynamics and 
external influences such as legal frameworks, cultural norms, and market conditions 
(Martin-Alcázar et al., 2005). 
These perspectives show that SHRM is not a one-size-fits-all approach. Instead, the 
choice and combination of HR strategies should reflect an organisation’s specific context.  
In this research, they inform the evaluation of whether the HR-BSC model supports both 
standardised best practices and locally tailored solutions at SIU. For example, the 
universalistic perspective provides a basis for identifying practices beneficial across all 
SIU campuses, while the contingency and contextual views emphasize the need for 
 
 
48 
adaptability to local needs and external pressures. The configurational lens stresses the 
importance of coherence across HR practices, a key consideration in assessing whether 
the HR-BSC fosters integrated performance management. 
This understanding of SHRM and its multiple perspectives provides a conceptual 
foundation for evaluating HR practices in higher education. To further ground this 
research, the next section offers a brief historical overview of performance management, 
tracing its evolution and relevance to modern HR strategies, whereas Section 2.3, 
reviews the suitability of various HR tools in light of these perspectives, with a focus on 
how they may support strategic alignment, operational efficiency, and responsiveness to 
SIU’s internal and external challenges.  
 
2.1.4. History of Performance Management: An Overview 
 
In the context of increasingly complex and competitive organisational environments, 
leaders are under pressure to ensure that their institutions not only operate efficiently but 
also align with long-term strategic goals. In response, two concepts — SHRM and 
performance management (PM) — have become central to both theory and practice. 
SHRM reframes the role of human resources from a primarily administrative function to a 
strategic partner in achieving organisational success, while PM provides structured 
approaches to measuring and managing progress toward those goals. Together, these 
concepts form the theoretical backbone of tools like the Human Resource Balanced 
Scorecard (HR-BSC), which aim to link individual and organisational performance with 
broader institutional objectives. 
Before evaluating which performance management framework best suits SIU’s needs, it 
is essential to understand how performance management has evolved historically, and 
how it has increasingly been integrated into HR and strategic thinking. 
The origins of PM can be traced to the 1950s, when Peter Drucker introduced the 
concept of Management by Objectives (MBO). This marked a significant shift from top-
down evaluation methods toward more collaborative goal-setting between managers and 
 
 
49 
employees. By the 1970s, organisations began to develop competency-based systems, 
which emphasized the skills and behaviours needed to meet organisational objectives 
and linked employee development with broader performance outcomes. 
In the 1990s, Kaplan and Norton’s Balanced Scorecard (BSC) further transformed 
performance thinking by introducing a multidimensional framework. Moving beyond 
financial metrics, the BSC incorporated additional perspectives, customer satisfaction, 
internal processes, and learning and growth, highlighting the need for alignment between 
individual performance and organisational strategy. This holistic approach has had a 
lasting influence on both private and public sector performance systems, including in 
higher education. 
The 2000s brought about a wave of digital transformation in HR and performance 
management. New technologies enabled real-time data collection, goal tracking, and 
feedback systems, making performance management more agile and responsive. At the 
same time, the concept of performance expanded. Andersen and Fagerhaug (2002) 
described performance as encompassing not just productivity but also innovation, 
employee engagement, and sustainability — dimensions essential for long-term success. 
They argued that leaders must be able to measure, monitor, and act on performance at 
all levels of the organisation to adapt to change effectively. 
Recent literature reflects a growing concern with bridging the gap between theory and 
practice. Schleicher et al. (2018), in their comprehensive review of four decades of 
performance management research, point out that despite extensive practical innovation, 
the academic field still lacks a unified, theory-driven foundation. They conclude: “despite 
progress in the study of PM, there is much work yet to be done in developing a 
comprehensive, conclusive, and systems theory–grounded body of scientific knowledge 
about PM systems that can better inform PM in practice” (p. 2239). Similarly, Levy et al. 
(2017) highlight discrepancies between evolving practices in organisations and the 
fragmented support these receive from academic models.

As performance management has developed from a narrow focus on efficiency and 
output to a more comprehensive system, its emphasis has shifted toward goal alignment, 
employee development, and continuous feedback. Enabled by technological advances 
and data-driven tools, modern performance management supports both individual growth 
and strategic organisational outcomes. 
This evolution highlights the relevance of tools like the HR-BSC, which link SHRM and 
performance management by turning strategic goals into measurable HR actions. 
Understanding this shift is key to assessing whether such tools suit SIU’s complex 
environment of academic, operational, and regional demands. 
This foundation enables a critical assessment of the most appropriate framework for SIU 
and situates the research within broader efforts to advance SHRM and performance 
management in higher education. 
 
 
51 
2.1.5. Performance Management Concepts and Frameworks: An Overview  
 
The concept ‘performance management’ evolved as part of the broader leadership 
movement towards improving organisational effectiveness and aligning individual 
performance with strategic organisational goals (Armstrong, 2005). An integral part to 
managing and improving performance within an organisation are performance 
management frameworks and tools. Frameworks are structured approaches that guide 
the entire process of performance management within an organisation. They can provide 
a theoretical basis and offer a comprehensive set of guidelines including specific 
processes, steps and techniques to ensure effective implementation. Their orientation is 
strategic and covers all aspects of performance management. In contrast to frameworks, 
tools are specific applications or techniques used to implement the processes defined by 
performance management frameworks. They provide the operational capabilities needed 
to carry out performance management tasks (Armstrong, 2009; Daniels and New, 1994). 
Several performance management frameworks are commonly used across different 
industries due to their effectiveness and adaptability. Literature shows, that particularly 
the 1990s were a transformative decade for performance management frameworks due 
to globalisation, technical advancement, shifts towards knowledge-based economies and 
increased demands for accountability and transparency. Table 5 provides some of the 
most widely used frameworks:

The use of performance management concepts, if properly implemented, can enable 
managers to set appropriate objectives, to monitor if these objectives are achieved, to 
manage strategy execution, to make necessary adjustments, and to deliver results 
(Grüning, 2002). 
As mentioned above, literature distinguishes performance management from 
performance measurement regarding organisational performance (Taouab & Issor, 2019; 
Neely et al., 1995). Performance management focuses on individual performance and 
strategic alignment, involving activities like setting goals and using data to improve 
 
 
53 
organisational outcomes. In contrast, performance measurement emphasizes quantifying 
performance through KPIs and ensuring the validity of these systems (Kloot & Martin, 
2000; Neely et al., 1995). Neely and colleagues highlight that while measurement 
focuses on tracking and evaluating performance, management is about using these 
metrics for strategic improvement. Despite the distinction, there is considerably more 
research on performance measurement than on its practical application in performance 
management, suggesting a gap in the literature.  
The performance concepts are mostly built around key business dimensions, which are 
considered comprehensively to ensure a holistic approach to evaluating organisational 
success:  
• 
Financial Performance: Often measured by metrics like revenue, profit, return on 
investment (ROI), and shareholder value (Harvard Business Review, 1992). 
• 
Operational Performance: Focuses on efficiency and effectiveness in processes, 
supply chain, and production. Metrics may include cycle time, defect rates, and 
process efficiency (Goldratt, 1984). 
• 
Employee Performance: Evaluating the productivity and contributions of individual 
employees or teams. Metrics encompass employee satisfaction, productivity, turnover 
rates, and skill development (McGregor, 1960). 
• 
Market Performance: Evaluating an organisation's competitive position and market 
share. Metrics may include market growth rate and brand perception (Chen Kim & 
Mauborgne, 2005). 
• 
Sustainability and Corporate Social Responsibility (CSR): Evaluating an 
organisation's environmental and social impact. Metrics include responsible sourcing, 
and social responsibility initiatives (Willard, 2002). 
It sets the foundation for evaluating SIU's HR strategies by focusing on key business 
dimensions crucial for success.  
 
 
54 
The following chapter will delve into SHRM and its role in aligning HR performance with 
broader organisational goals, providing the theoretical framework necessary to integrate 
these performance metrics into a cohesive HR management strategy. 
 
2.1.6. SHRM, Performance, and Organisational Behaviour  
 
Over the last 30 years, SHRM has been increasingly recognized for its impact on 
organisational performance (Kooli & Abadli, 2022; Govand & Nabaz Nawzad, 2021; 
Becker and Gerhart, 1996). Scholars and practitioners have focused their attention on 
human resources as a key organisational source for success and competitive advantage 
(Holbeche, 2022; Anwar & Abdullah, 2021; Liu, et at., 2007). Central to this view is the 
belief that organisational performance is inextricably linked to the performance of its 
people — regardless of the organisation’s size, purpose, or sector (Bisoux, 2004; 
Bossidy & Charan, 2002).  
Organisational behaviour (OB) provides essential insight into this relationship by 
exploring how individuals and groups function within organisations, and how their 
attitudes, motivations, and interactions affect workplace outcomes. Concepts from OB, 
such as employee engagement, leadership style, group dynamics, and organisational 
culture, help explain why certain HRM practices influence performance and how they can 
be effectively implemented. For example, employee engagement is closely tied to job 
resources and meaningful work, which HR can support through development 
opportunities and participatory goal setting (Bakker & Demerouti, 2008). Strong group 
dynamics, supported by collaborative HR practices, improve team effectiveness 
(Kozlowski & Ilgen, 2006). Likewise, a learning-oriented organisational culture, reinforced 
by HR policies, aligns employee behaviour with strategic goals (Schein, 2010). OB 
theories also guide the design of performance systems, for example, motivation theories 
(e.g., Vroom’s expectancy theory, 1964) shape incentive structures, by ensuring that 
rewards are clearly linked to effort, performance, and personal value, thereby enhancing 
motivation. Other theories, e.g. Herzberg’s Two-Factor Theory (Herzberg et al., 1959), 
 
 
55 
emphasize clear goals, perceived fairness, and meaningful incentives to drive effective 
employee performance. 
Consultancies like McKinsey (2021) reinforce this behavioural view by advising 
organisations that impact and value realisation requires not an alignment of corporate 
performance with people performance management. According to the consultancy, a firm 
may desire market leadership but will only achieve it, if it has built the organisational and 
human resources accordingly. From this performance management perspective, HRM 
must align with organisational goals while fostering the psychological conditions 
necessary for individual success.  
A large body of empirical research supports the positive correlation between integrated 
bundles of HRM practices and improved performance outcomes (Arthur et al., 2021; 
Delery & Doty, 1996; Huselid, 1995; McDuffie, 1995; Guthrie, 2001). Kumar (2010) notes 
that organisations with more strategically integrated and behaviourally informed HR 
systems tend to outperform their competitors. Similarly, Noe (2010) emphasizes that HR 
practices influence strategic execution through productivity and performance control 
mechanisms. If performance management focuses not only on measuring but also 
stimulating employee performance (Den Hartog et al., 2004), it becomes a dynamic tool 
for continuous improvement — at both the individual and organisational levels.  
Meanwhile, Guest (2001) underscores persistent difficulties in theory-building and the 
operationalization of HRM practices and their impact on performance. Despite significant 
progress in understanding the links between HRM and performance, many studies are 
limited by methodological issues, such as small sample sizes, or single-respondent bias 
(often HR managers), which frequently result in a lack of attention to employee 
perspective. This is problematic because the success of HR initiatives often depends on 
employee perceptions and behavioural responses, which are central concerns in OB. 
This research aims to address both theoretical and empirical challenges by adopting a 
more robust and comprehensive approach to studying the links between HRM and 
performance in the context of higher education. Unlike studies that rely on data from a 
single respondent, this thesis gathers qualitative data from multiple stakeholders within 
 
 
56 
the organisation, including leadership, faculty, staff, administrative personnel, and 
students. This approach provides a more holistic perspective, capturing diverse views 
and reducing bias, thus offering a clearer picture of how HRM practices affect 
performance at SIU. 
 
2.1.7. Summary SHRM and Performance Management  
 
This section reviewed the literature on SHRM and performance management, 
underscoring their interconnected role in enhancing organisational effectiveness. 
Research consistently demonstrates that aligning HR practices with an institution’s 
strategic goals contributes to improved organisational performance by leveraging human 
capital as a key resource (Wright & McMahan, 1992; Becker & Huselid, 2006).  
The literature shows that integrated HRM systems, encompassing recruitment, 
development, evaluation, and reward structures, can yield sustained improvements in 
both employee and institutional outcomes. However, some challenges persist, including 
methodological limitations in measuring HRM’s impact and the need to account for 
contextual influences such as organisational culture, governance structures, and external 
pressures like demographic change and globalisation. 
Performance management, as discussed in this review, complements SHRM by focusing 
on the continuous assessment and enhancement of both individual and organisational 
capabilities.  
For this study, performance will be understood in line with Anderson and Fagerhaug’s 
(2002) multidimensional framework. This includes not only productivity, but also 
innovation capacity, ethical conduct, environmental responsibility, and the ability to 
attract and retain talent. By synthesizing these perspectives, this research positions 
SHRM and performance management as essential levers for organisational 
development, also in higher education. The introduction of a structured performance 
management tool, specifically the HR Balanced Scorecard (HR-BSC), discussed in 
 
 
57 
Chapter 3, aims to operationalize this alignment. The goal is to create a dynamic, 
strategic, and context-sensitive approach to HR performance that supports SIU’s broader 
institutional ambitions and offers insights transferable to similar academic settings. 
 
2.2. 
SHRM and Performance Management in Higher Education  
 
Higher education institutions are human resource-driven, relying heavily on the expertise, 
engagement, and collaboration of faculty, staff, and administrators to deliver high-quality 
education, foster research, and ensure institutional success. In this Section, SHRM and 
performance management literature in the field of Higher Education is reviewed. First, 
works on (S)HRM are analysed, and market trends influencing HRM are discussed. The 
review of the main management performance literature follows in the next sub-section, 
including examples from universities.     
 
2.2.1. Strategic Human Resource Management in Higher Education  
 
As previously discussed, a motivated and well-managed workforce is vital for achieving 
institutional goals. In higher education, the importance of fostering educational and 
research excellence highlights the need for tailored, strategic HR practices that align with 
the unique demands of the sector. Gwizdala (2011) emphasizes the importance of 
university employees, stating that an institution's success largely depends on the 
“competence, commitment, and motivation” of its workforce to fulfil its mission and goals 
(p. 243). Similarly, Szelagowska-Rudzka (2018) highlights that employee satisfaction has 
a direct impact on the quality of education and research outcomes. According to 
Mohiuddin et al. (2022), institutional success relies on the quality of human resources 
and robust personnel management strategies. Tokarz-Kocik (2016) sees effective HR 
practices, that are essential in the corporate sector, as equally critical in higher education 
for fostering institutional excellence. 
 
 
58 
The term human resources in higher educating comprises academic staff (faculty), 
managerial staff (administration) and specialists (professional support, operations). As 
stated by Bossu and Brown (2018), the role of managerial staff and specialists is growing 
in importance and numbers, and consequently accounts for an increasing share of total 
human resource expenditure. Jackson (2001, p. 404) points out that “Universities 
typically spend around 60 per cent of their income on staffing yet until recently paid little 
attention to the management of those staff or to the personnel management function.” In 
2019, university HR spending in Europe increased to ~70% on average (The Guardian). 
The OECD (2020) reported cost amounts of two-thirds of current expenditures. As 
Desrochers and Kirshstein (2014, p. 1) observed “higher education’s workforce must be 
considered in any analysis of rising costs. The higher education workforce — from 
tenured professors to part-time adjuncts, and from executives and professionals to 
support staff — is changing rapidly.” According to Gordon and Whitchurch (2007), higher 
education institutions now see human resources as central to their organisation and 
essential for capacity building. They recognize that institutional performance hinges on 
the ability to attract, retain, reward, and develop staff who can perform diverse roles 
responsively, expertly, and flexibly in order to not overspend. Recruiting qualified 
employees can be influenced by different factors, e.g. public sector employment law, 
external quality assurance systems (e.g. accreditation agency), system of employment 
contracts (e.g. tenor track), financial sustainability, etc. (ENQA, 2025).  
The former Higher Education Funding Council for England (HEFCE, 2010, in Curran) 
reported developments in the composition of the workforce by academic, managerial and 
professional support roles and noted a shift in roles, especially regarding professional 
service roles from a more operational to a more strategic, or institutional, approach. 
Additionally, academic administration and management have become increasingly 
complex as institutions grow larger and more multifaceted, multiplying tasks in what Kerr 
(2001, p. 5) calls modern "multiversities." This has also increased the need for skilled 
management and administration at the institutional level. In the past decade, universities 
have developed specific HRM services, mostly focusing on the quality of their academic 
staff for teaching and research. However, the success of a university likewise depends 
 
 
59 
on the quality of its administration and management services. Thus, as Pausits (2015) 
points out, effective management and academic functions rely on high-quality HRM 
practices. According to van den Brink (2013; p.180) and based on assumptions of 
Lorange (2006), the “composition and quality of (academic) staff is vitally important for … 
the reputation and competitive position of universities and institutions in the academic 
community”. As argued by Aboramadan and Dahleez (2020), commitment and 
engagement are particularly crucial in academia. Thus, the quality and survival of higher 
education institutions rely on having a highly engaged and committed staff (Nazir and 
Islam, 2017). 
In a recent study, Vrba (2024) examines the role of (S)HRM in higher education, its 
impact on education quality and the challenges of implementation. He states that HR 
planning, including task analysis, cost assessment, and workforce requirements, is 
essential for promoting educational functions and achieving academic goals. Provided 
recommendations include addressing talent gaps, improving work process effectiveness, 
enhancing staff knowledge, and fostering closer cooperation between faculties and HR 
departments.  
Conversely, in the last few years, the landscape of higher education has been 
significantly influenced by factors such as the Covid pandemic, generative AI, and 
geopolitical shifts, potentially reversing globalisation trends, which are reshaping 
institutional priorities and strategies. This topic will be further explored in the discussion 
chapter.  
Several other scholars have examined HRM in higher education (e.g., Chen et al., 2009; 
Lew, 2009; Malik et al., 2010; Rowley, 1996), however, research on HRM strategies 
improving university performance remains limited (Nazir and Islam, 2017; Wilkins et al., 
2017). As Wilkins et al. (2017) point out, the use of structured HR performance 
management tools in university contexts is underexplored, suggesting a significant 
research gap. Most previous studies focus on faculty morale (Rosser, 2004), staff job 
satisfaction (Smerek and Peterson, 2007), and professors’ intention to quit (Johnsrud et 
al., 2000). Bailey et al. (2018) found only limited research on the relationship between 
 
 
60 
HRM practices and contextual performance, such as commitment and innovative 
behaviours. Additionally, researchers and practitioners confirm that most universities do 
not have a separate department dedicated to performing the HR functions (Menon, 2015; 
Taamneh et al., 2021). 
As said, this research addresses that gap and aims to enrich the HRM literature in higher 
education by investigating the relationship between HRM practices, organisational 
commitment, and performance improvement in an academic context. With respect to 
relevancy of the study, the following chapter presents recent market trends and HRM 
developments that illustrate the importance of personnel performance and the integration 
of HRM in the university setting.   
 
2.2.2. Market trends that influence HRM in Higher Education  
 
The global HE market is undergoing a rapid and far-reaching transformation, shaped by 
multiple factors including technological advancements, shifting student demographics, 
increasing cost sensitivity, and the growing influence of international ranking systems. In 
this evolving landscape, adaptability has become a crucial competency for higher 
education professionals. 
This need for adaptability is reflected in labour market projections. According to a World 
Economic Forum analysis (Zahidi, 2023), administrative roles in higher education are 
expected to grow by over 9% between 2023 and 2033. At the same time, the global 
higher education market is forecasted to expand at a compound annual growth rate 
(CAGR) of 10.4% from 2023 to 2028 (EMR, 2023), highlighting the sector’s dynamic and 
accelerating pace of change. 
To better understand the relevance of HR performance management in this context, the 
following section outlines key market trends, including internationalisation, evolving 
stakeholder expectations, technological progress, and shifting funding models. Here, 
internal stakeholders refer to faculty, staff, and students, while external stakeholders 
 
 
61 
include employers, whose needs are increasingly shaping the student expectations and, 
by extension, the functions of various university departments and HR practices. 
Together, these forces are reshaping the structure and strategy of higher education 
institutions and are poised to significantly influence the role of performance management 
and strategic human resource management (SHRM) in universities. Understanding these 
trends is essential for aligning HR practices with institutional goals and maintaining 
competitiveness in the global higher education arena. 
 
2.2.2.1. 
Internationalisation 
 
Trow (2007) identified three basic stages of higher education developments worldwide: 
elite, mass, and universal access - which benefits from internationalisation strategies in 
the higher education sector.  
The term internationalisation, or globalisation, is increasingly used as frameworks for 
higher education research (Tight, 2021; de Wit & Altbach, 2021). Globalisation refers to 
“the reality shaped by an increasingly integrated world economy, new information and 
communications technology, the emergence of an international knowledge network, … 
and other forces beyond the control of academic institutions” (Altbach et al., 2009, p. 7). 
This promotes the idea of reshaping the role and institutional structure of higher 
education institutions (Maringe & Foskett, 2010; Beck, 2012; de Wit & Altbach, 2021).  
Another factor following the general globalisation trend is student mobility (Altbach, et al., 
2019). According to Altbach et al. (2019, p. 8) these “students also add international 
diversity to an academic environment”. According to UNESCO and OECD statistics 
(2022), international student numbers increased from 1.35 million in 1955 to 2.75 million 
in 2010 and 6.3 million in 2020. In comparison to 4.8 million in 2015, this means an 
increase of 32%. In accordance with this, and influenced by the global pandemic 
restrictions, practitioners see global learning platforms on the rise (OECD, 2020; 
Williamson, 2021; Wood, 2022). The amplified number of students results in rapidly 
growing size of involved academic institutions, higher education systems and an evolving 
 
 
62 
governance structure of higher education (Mok & James, 2005; de Wit & Altbach, 2021). 
The efforts of internationalisation comprise wide-ranging modifications, including policy 
changes, organisational restructuring, curricular revision, and financial implications 
(Leask, 2009; Hong, 2020; Crowley-Vigneau et al., 2020).  
Recently, globalisation bears a significant impact on universities’ employment strategies. 
The ongoing transformation and competition, such as the appearance of new university 
models and learning concepts requires leaders to pay attention to the reputation, quality 
and the success of the institution (Sanders, 2020). The expected consequence is a 
search for ‘best practice’ while reflecting local differences and practicalities (Sanders, 
2020; Tremblay et al. 2013). Vaiman and Brewster (2015) argue that universalistic 
approaches mostly fail when considering several geographical locations and their 
differences. They analyse cross-national differences regarding HRM philosophies, 
policies and practices, with different influence on the decision making ability and flexibility 
within HRM functions and practices. Andolsek and Stebe (2005) emphasize, as an 
organisation extends its activities into other countries, the impact of globalisation on 
management and as well human resource development is significant. In many global 
higher education systems, the role of academics in institutional governance is 
diminished, while administrative and professional staff have taken on more 
responsibilities. These include tasks such as marketing, income generation, meeting 
accountability requirements, and responding to stakeholder needs. This shift represents 
a significant change in academic careers as well as the culture and governance of higher 
education institutions, necessitating adjustments in HRM strategies. Macfarlane (2011) 
refers to this phenomenon as ‘deskilling’ of academic staff and ‘up-skilling’ of 
administrative or professional support staff. These role specializations are important 
changes that HRM strategies must address to ensure alignment with evolving 
institutional demands and governance structures.     
The challenges and opportunities associated with global engagement in higher education 
are interconnected, requiring institutions to navigate them carefully to maximize benefits 
while managing potential difficulties. To this end, institutions must develop 
comprehensive strategies that support cross-border initiatives and prioritize the global 
 
 
63 
orientation of their campuses. Moreover, governments and international bodies play a 
role in facilitating and regulating these efforts in transnational education. 
Accordingly, internationalisation strategies in higher education can have a significant 
impact on HR strategies within academic institutions. The implementation of 
internationalisation initiatives often requires adjustments in HR policies, practices, and 
resource allocation to support faculty and staff engagement in global activities (Altbach et 
al., 2011; Tsiligiris, 2021). Hence, universities often develop specific HR policies and 
practices tailored to their internationalisation goals.  
 
The discussion chapter will revisit recent developments, highlighting how student 
mobility, international staff recruitment, or cross-border governance influence HR 
practices and strategic planning. By examining these factors, the study will provide 
insights into the ways HRM strategies can be adapted to meet the demands of an 
increasingly globalised higher education landscape. 
 
2.2.2.2. 
Stakeholder Expectations  
 
Higher education involves many stakeholders, so called internal and external, including 
students, alumni, donors, parents, other institutions, accrediting agencies, vendors, 
employers, government bodies, etc., as well as administrative staff and academic faculty 
(Marshall, 2018). The complex interplay of stakeholder perceptions and expectations 
affects universities’ activities regarding cost, quality (accreditations and rankings), access 
(talent, funds), and technology. However, several scholars argue that some groups have 
a relatively low 'risk' associated with their 'stake' and may not truly qualify as 
stakeholders. Despite this, universities, as large-scale organisations, must balance these 
diverse societal voices. Several authors point out, that over the past two decades, two 
stakeholder groups in particular — students and employers — have gained a widely 
accepted role in various higher education affairs (Watson, 2012; Leisyte & Westerheijden 
2014; Lisá et al., 2019).  
 
 
64 
The expectations of current students are formed by unique personal, academic, and 
professional desires. Students expect agile, insightful, and proactive educational 
experiences and a degree that offers advanced placements, or job opportunities and 
better employment benefits (Lisá et al., 2019; Cicha et al., 2021; Ziatdinov & Cilliers, 
2022).  
The expectations of companies lately shifted to hiring graduates with specific skills, e.g. 
computer skills, people skills, and abilities, e.g. to innovate, or to perform under pressure, 
next to academic knowledge (Lisá et al., 2019; Bae et al., 2022; Okolie et al., 2022). 
Thus, HEI are in need to develop and offer programs which target competencies. 
Competency-based education is the approach to align student knowledge and skills 
based on employer needs, while personalizing courses based on a student’s learning 
pace (Gervais, 2016; Williams, 2018).  
 
2.2.2.3. 
Advancements in Technology 
 
The rapid shift towards online learning, accelerated by the Covid pandemic and changing 
student demands, has created an immediate need for improved HRM in universities. 
According to Statista (2023), the online education sector saw a growth of 15.4% in 2019 
(pre-pandemic) and expected a growth of 13,4% in 2023. Changing the mode of 
educational delivery from face-to-face interaction to digital, or online delivery, has 
transformed the higher education sector (Altbach et al., 2019). Ongoing developments 
include, for example, course development and assessment, a shift in how academic work 
is organised, student and academic mobility and changes in conditions of academic work 
(OECD, 2020). Accordingly, practitioners see global learning platforms on the rise 
(OECD, 2020; Williamson, 2021; Wood, 2022). However, providing flexible access to 
education takes time, as management and HR need to develop a clear strategy, design 
digital teaching and raise internal motivation (Hochschulforum Digitalisierung, 2018). In 
addition to online courses, online learning platforms and a global network, universities 
need to implement artificial intelligence (AI) into their portfolio (Ziatdinov and Cilliers, 
 
 
65 
2022). According to various international reports, AI is one of the currently emerging 
technology in HE (Educause, 2019; Baker and Smith, 2019; OECD, 2020). Closing the 
innovation gap is seen as a necessary role of universities, as technology transfer seems 
lately as important as teaching and research (Toivanen and Suominen, 2015). 
The long-term impacts of technological changes will be debated further in the discussion 
chapter. These changes affect not only teaching but also broader institutional strategies, 
as universities must prioritize innovation and technology transfer alongside teaching and 
research. As trends evolve, HR strategies must adapt to equip faculty and staff for the 
increasingly digital and globalised educational environment. 
 
2.2.2.4. 
Funding 
 
As state funds became scarce, and tuition fee revenue is dropping, education leaders 
need to explore other funding sources, such as continuing education, or private donors 
(Skinner, 2019; Compagnucci & Spigarelli, 2020). Capital campaigning in the higher 
education sector becomes more and more ambitious. For instance, the University of 
Michigan raised $5 billion in its fundraising campaign, which was led by 1.600 fundraising 
volunteers and 550 development staff members (Lessnau, 2018). Following this, the 
University of North Carolina tried to raise $4.25 billion (Moss, 2017), while the University 
of Washington’s fundraising goal of $6 billion was achieved in 2022 (Sudermann, 2022). 
According to the OECD (2020), shifts in funding significantly impact the academic labour 
market and institutional priorities. As traditional revenue streams decline, universities 
must become more competitive and responsive to the needs of external stakeholders, 
including students, industry partners, and private donors. These funding changes are 
reshaping both the composition of the academic workforce and the strategic focus of 
universities. 
For instance, to manage costs, institutions increasingly rely on adjunct or contract-based 
staff, which are less costly than tenured faculty, affecting the stability and career 
 
 
66 
progression of academic staff. This shift alters the traditional structure of academic 
careers (OECD, 2020). Additionally, reduced public funding pushes universities to seek 
private partnerships and external grants to secure revenue, aligning research priorities 
more closely with market demands, such as technology transfer and applied research 
(Aithal & Maiya, 2023). For instance, a 2023 report highlighted that the UK's current 
innovation efforts are overly dispersed and extremely dependent on universities, 
necessitating integration into a broader industrial strategy (FT, 2024). This focus on 
external funding sources influences universities to prioritize fields that offer greater 
opportunities for external investment, fundamentally changing the academic landscape. 
 
2.2.3. Performance Management in Higher Education 
 
Performance management in higher education has been a topic of ongoing discussion 
and evolution, with institutions continually adapting their approaches to new trends and 
thus to potentially meet the changing demands of students, faculty, and other 
stakeholders. The transition from a traditional collegial model to a more managerial 
approach, as noted by Deem (2009), reflects more than a structural change — it marks a 
shift in institutional logic. The collegial model prioritised shared governance and 
academic autonomy, while the managerial model emphasizes strategic planning, 
measurable outcomes, and efficiency (Mayer, 2024; Stephenson et al., 2023; Wiley, 
2020). Although this shift may enhance competitiveness and financial resilience, it has 
also been critiqued for reducing academic freedom and increasing administrative liability 
(Magalhães & Veiga, 2022). Balancing these priorities remains a challenge for modern 
universities. These challenges are closely tied to this research, which looks at how 
strategic HR practices and performance can support both institutional goals and 
university values.  
This tension between institutional goals and academic values is central to ongoing 
debates about the role of performance management in universities. Kelchen (2018) 
observed a shift from input-based metrics, like resources, to outcome-based metrics, 
 
 
67 
such as graduation rates and employment outcomes (Kelchen, 2018; Kuh et al., 2015). 
While this shift may improve transparency and accountability, it can also result in 
reductive thinking — oversimplifying educational quality and encouraging a focus on 
short-term, measurable outputs at the expense of deeper, long-term learning objectives. 
These critiques align with broader concerns about performance cultures that prioritise 
efficiency over educational impact. 
The growing reliance on data-driven decision-making has similarly yielded mixed results. 
While quantitative performance metrics help inform resource allocation and institutional 
planning, they also come with limitations. Messick (2013) argues that reducing academic 
work to key performance indicators risks undermining intrinsic motivation among staff, 
especially when individual contributions — such as mentoring, interdisciplinary 
collaboration, or experimental pedagogy — are not easily quantifiable. These issues are 
especially salient for international institutions like Schiller International University (SIU), 
where performance frameworks must account for diverse academic cultures, regulatory 
systems, and operational contexts. 
Furthermore, while faculty development programs are necessary for professional growth, 
they often focus on meeting institutional goals rather than encouraging innovation in 
teaching and research (Gordon & Whitchurch, 2010). Similarly, accreditation processes, 
while vital for quality assurance, are frequently criticized for promoting standardisation at 
the expense of flexibility and responsiveness (Kumar et al., 2020). Institutions may 
become preoccupied with meeting accreditation requirements at the expense of adopting 
new, experimental approaches to teaching and learning.  
This managerial shift also has implications for HR management in higher education. 
Research-intensive institutions, according to Altbach (2015), often underemphasize 
HRM, while teaching-oriented universities may adopt more proactive HR practices 
focused on staff engagement and professional development. However, several scholars 
note that, across both contexts, limited financial resources and insufficient HR expertise 
hinder the development of strategic HRM systems (Salmi, 2009; Knight & Trowler, 2000; 
Kulik, 2014). Moreover, the risk of reducing complex issues to simplistic metrics extends 
 
 
68 
to diversity and inclusion initiatives. Bensimon and Malcom (2023) caution that when 
diversity goals are assessed solely through quantitative targets, institutions risk symbolic 
compliance without substantive change. 
These critical perspectives highlight the broader concern: how can performance 
management systems borrowed from the corporate world be meaningfully adapted to the 
academic context? Scholars have investigated the tension between academic goals and 
managerial demands for efficiency and measurable outcomes and analysed how these 
models influence faculty, students, and administrators, particularly concerning 
engagement, satisfaction, and the risk of losing creativity and academic innovation. For 
faculty and administrators alike, performance models may be perceived as bureaucratic 
tools that erode the professional discretion and creativity traditionally associated with 
academic work (Magalhães & Veiga, 2022; Mayer, 2024). Illustrative cases, such as the 
University of California implemented metrics to assess diversity goals but faced criticism 
for oversimplifying complex social issues into numeric indicators (Bensimon & Malcom, 
2023). Likewise, the widespread use of accreditation as a proxy for performance 
management, highlighted by Kumar et al. (2020), often leads universities to prioritize 
compliance and standardisation over educational innovation. 
While corporate models provide valuable tools for performance measurement and 
improvement, they must be adapted to align with the unique goals and long-term impacts 
of academic institutions. A hybrid approach, combining the practicality of corporate tools 
with a focus on academic values, may represent the most effective solution.  
This discussion indicates the need for further research on effectively applying 
performance management in higher education, particularly in the context of HR practices. 
Despite the growing attention to performance in higher education, relatively few studies 
address how these frameworks function across culturally and structurally diverse 
institutions. This gap is particularly relevant for multi-campus, international universities 
such as SIU, where HR systems must reconcile strategic coordination with local 
responsiveness.   
 
 
69 
In light of these challenges, this current research project critically evaluates whether a 
strategic performance management approach can balance the demands for 
accountability and efficiency with the broader goals of higher education. This involves not 
only implementing robust systems but also addressing their potential limitations and 
ensuring alignment with the unique values and mission of an academic institution, like 
SIU. 
 
2.2.3.1. 
Performance Management Examples from Higher Education 
Practice 
 
Performance management measures are widely used by higher education institutions 
globally to assess, track, and improve various aspects of their operations. To make the 
literature review more comprehensive and relevant, specific examples of universities 
known for their commitment to performance management and measurement are 
highlighted in the following section. These examples not only strengthen the thesis 
argument but also provide a practical foundation for analysis by showcasing how leading 
institutions implement these strategies. By examining such examples, this section will 
offer insights into the real-world application of performance management in higher 
education. Particular attention will be given to whether and how these institutions apply 
performance measures within HRM, and if strategic tools such as the BSC are being 
utilized in this context. While it is challenging to provide an exhaustive list of universities 
employing these measures due to their widespread adoption, the following institutions 
are highlighted for their scholarly contributions and strategic leadership in the field of 
performance management and measurement: 
- 
Harvard University: 
Harvard University is well-known for its rigorous performance management and 
measurement systems, which cover academic and administrative areas. They use data 
analytics to assess student outcomes, faculty research productivity, and overall 
institutional effectiveness. These assessments are conducted at the department level 
 
 
70 
and advise on promotions and tenure (www.hr.harvard.edu/performance-management). 
While the BSC framework was pioneered by scholars at the Harvard Business School, 
there’s no direct evidence that Harvard University itself uses the BSC as its primary 
performance management framework in HRM. 
- 
Stanford University: 
Stanford University focuses on outcome-based performance, using metrics for academic 
excellence, research, and student success, with a holistic approach to faculty 
assessment (www.hshr.stanford.edu). However, there is no evidence that the university 
applies the BSC concept in HRM. 
- 
University of Texas System: 
The University of Texas System is a large public university system that employs 
performance measurement to track student enrolment, graduation rates, research 
expenditures, and faculty productivity (www.utsystem.edu). However, there is no 
evidence that the university applies the BSC concept in HRM. 
- 
University of California: 
The University of California (UC) is known for its robust performance measurement and 
accountability framework, which includes key metrics related to student success, 
research activity, and diversity and inclusion, with UCLA’s Faculty Excellence review 
assessing research, teaching, and service (www.uc.edu). At UC, the traditional four BSC 
dimensions have been applied, however not strongly connected to HRM. 
- 
Imperial College London: 
Imperial College London uses KPIs to evaluate research, teaching quality, and 
institutional performance (www.imperial.ac.uk). It applied a BSC including leading 
(output) and lagging (impact) measures, however, there is no evidence that the institution 
applies the BSC concept in HRM. 
- 
University of Melbourne: 
 
 
71 
The University of Melbourne in Australia measures faculty, student outcomes, and 
research for decision-making (www.unimelb.edu.au). However, there is no evidence 
confirming that the university uses a formal, documented BSC concept. 
- 
National University of Singapore: 
The National University of Singapore (NUS) has a comprehensive performance 
management system that tracks faculty research, student outcomes, and rankings 
(www.nus.edu.sg). However, there is no evidence that NUS applies the BSC concept. 
- 
Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT): 
MIT assesses academic excellence, research impact, and financial sustainability through 
data-driven decisions, including peer review and student feedback (www.mit.edu).  
However, there is no evidence that the university applies the BSC concept in HRM. 
- 
University of Oxford: 
The University of Oxford employs performance management to track academic quality, 
research output, and institutional reputation through faculty assessments, including 
teaching and research evaluations (www.ox.ac.uk). A ‘people and organisational 
development’ team supports the university in HR matters, however, there is no evidence 
that the university uses a BSC to link HR performance management to institutional 
performance. 
These specific institutions show that performance management systems are not just 
theoretical concepts but are actively implemented in top-tier universities. This helps 
ground the research in real-world practices, showing how performance management 
contributes to viable success in prestigious settings. 
It's important to note that universities may use performance management measures in 
various ways and may have specific indicators tailored to their unique missions and 
goals. Performance management in higher education typically covers aspects related to 
teaching, research, student outcomes, faculty productivity, financial sustainability. The 
examples show that each university's approach to performance management is shaped 
 
 
72 
by its unique institutional priorities and objectives. However, based on a review of 
publicly available information on university websites, it appears that most institutions tend 
to apply performance management on a departmental or unit-specific basis, rather than 
adopting a more holistic, university-wide approach. In the researcher’s view, this can 
result in fragmented efforts, where performance management is focused on isolated 
areas rather than being integrated across the institution to support broader alignment. 
Accordingly, need for a holistic performance management tool at SIU stems from 
challenges such as inconsistent indicators across campuses, limited transparency, and 
the demand for greater adaptability. A unified system like the HR-BSC may help align 
operations and improve cohesion, but its effectiveness in delivering these outcomes will 
be critically evaluated in this research. 
 
2.2.3.2. 
Personnel Performance in Higher Education  
 
Research demonstrates that effective HRM practices improve employee commitment 
and performance, thereby enhancing organisational outcomes (Alqudah, 2022; McClean 
& Collins, 2011; Chen et al., 2009; Shahzad et al., 2008). While SHRM is well-studied in 
corporate contexts, its application in higher education is less explored (Wilkens et al., 
2017; Smeenk et al., 2008), with a notable gap in standardised metrics for measuring 
SHRM's impact on university performance (Cappiello & Pedrini, 2017; Govand & Nabaz, 
2021). However, scholars acknowledge the critical role of personnel performance in 
academic institutions' success, encompassing faculty, staff, and administrators (Pandit & 
Paul, 2023). Yang and Lv (2016) argue that SHRM can improve university recruitment, 
optimize resource allocation, and establish effective appraisal and compensation 
systems. High employee competence and commitment are essential for maintaining 
quality standards (de Kok et al., 2003), necessitating universities to measure and 
develop both academic and administrative staff.  
There has been limited effort to adapt performance management systems, commonly 
used in business administration, to higher education institutions. While HR functions may 
 
 
73 
be similar, the tools and techniques vary between businesses and universities. In 
Germany, for example, higher education staff are often "administered" rather than 
actively "managed" (Pellert et al., 2008, p. 13). This distinction reflects a more 
bureaucratic and procedural approach, where personnel decisions follow standardised 
rules, regulations, and civil service frameworks, with limited emphasis on strategic 
alignment, performance-based incentives, or active talent development. In contrast, 
being “managed” implies a more dynamic, strategic approach, where employees are 
guided, motivated, and evaluated in line with organisational goals. This difference 
underscores the challenge of applying business-derived performance tools, like the HR-
BSC, in academic environments that traditionally prioritize autonomy and formal 
structures over performance optimization. 
Research and literature on this topic often focus on specific areas such as teaching, 
research, service, and administrative responsibilities, as shown in Table 6:
