Beyond the averages, some differences in variability also emerged. Paris showed the 
highest standard deviation in course satisfaction scores (0.83), indicating wider variability 
in instructional quality — some courses received very high ratings, while others were 
rated poorly. In contrast, Madrid displayed more consistent satisfaction ratings across 
courses, pointing to a more uniform quality of teaching at that campuses. The range (Max 
- Min) indicates the spread of scores, helping to determine whether satisfaction ratings 
were relatively consistent or highly varied. The gap between Min and Max suggests 
variability in student experiences across different courses.  
The findings indicated that location influences student satisfaction, potentially due to 
differences in faculty expertise, teaching or grading methods, or institutional support. The 
lower satisfaction levels observed in HDB may point to systemic instructional challenges 
 
 
160 
or stricter grading practices. The exact causes for the differences deserve further 
investigation within the institution, e.g. by academic leadership of SIU. 
While this analysis highlights meaningful differences across campuses, it is important to 
note that aggregating data at the course level makes it difficult to determine whether 
variation in satisfaction scores reflects instructor effectiveness, student expectations, 
course content, or campus-specific factors. Additionally, this is a cross-sectional analysis, 
meaning it provides a snapshot of satisfaction levels at a specific point in time, rather that 
tracking trends. However, since this dataset serves as a baseline to support the broader 
research and inform the interview questions, the current level of analysis is sufficient. It 
offers a useful foundation for identifying potential HR-related issues around student 
satisfaction, that were further explored through qualitative interviews in the next research 
phase. Unfortunately, no additional surveys, for example, assessing administrative 
performance, employee satisfaction, or alumni reviews were conducted by SIU at that 
time, making it difficult to assess departmental effectiveness.  
This initial data analysis phase provided baseline insights into parts of SIU’s HRM 
practices and areas requiring further investigation, such as standardised HR policies 
and processes. These findings informed the interview questions and helped shape the 
design of the HR-BSC framework by identifying potential performance drivers, including 
the level of policy formalisation, adoption of digital HR tools, and the use of structured 
evaluation processes.  
To validate early interpretations and strengthen the qualitative phase, these preliminary 
findings were shared with SIU leadership (Provost, CFO, DoM, Dean). Their input 
helped ensure that subsequent data collection reflected the institutional context 
accurately. Additionally, the interview questions were directly informed by baseline 
insights and tailored to address specific gaps. For example, discrepancies in faculty 
evaluation practices led to questions such as: “Can you describe how faculty 
performance evaluations are conducted at your campus, and what challenges you 
experience with the current system?” This allowed participants to elaborate on local 
practices, perceived fairness, and implementation challenges, resulting in more focused 
 
 
161 
and meaningful responses. The following sections present the interview findings, 
analysing how the research design contributed to identifying key themes in the early 
implementation of the HR-BSC. 
 
5.3. 
Interview Methods  
 
This research is grounded in classical pragmatist, drawing on the foundational 
perspectives of Dewey (2007) and Peirce (1907/1998 and 2012), which emphasise 
practical problem-solving, iterative learning, and the use of empirical inquiry to support 
meaningful institutional improvement. Utilizing case study and action research 
methodologies, it explores HR challenges at Schiller International University (SIU) and 
evaluates the impact of implementing a HR-BSC on HR improvements and eventually 
overall institutional performance.  
Qualitative data was gathered through interviews with key personnel, as outlined in 
Section 4.4.2.3. The interview questions were carefully aligned with research objectives 
to generate meaningful insights. A pilot test helped refine the structure and clarity of the 
questions, while an iterative approach allowed for adjustments to the interview guide and 
follow-up prompts based on feedback and emerging themes. These modifications 
ensured that the interviews enabled a thorough exploration of HR performance 
challenges in an international university setting. 
 
5.3.1. Data Analysis - Interview Round 1 
 
This phase of the qualitative data analysis involved sixteen respondents from different 
campuses, as described in the Methodology chapter. Before conducting interviews, 
existing research and HR best practices were reviewed to identify common challenges 
and frameworks used in HR transformations, which also informed the interview 
 
 
162 
questions. The interviews provided first-hand insights into key HR practices and 
management, as well as technology use within SIU. The primary goal of this analysis 
was to systematically examine HR-related challenges and opportunities, informing the 
development of the HR-BSC as a strategic framework for improvement. 
The interview data was subjected to thematic analysis, which involved both the 
identification of recurring patterns, key themes, and discrepancies across respondents 
(emerging or appearing themes) and the refinement of preliminary themes based on 
initial observations and the researcher’s understanding on the organisational context 
(priori themes). Themes such as HR policies and structure, strategic planning and 
alignment, performance management and employee satisfaction, as well as technology 
and HR digitalization appeared as the primary categories for understanding HR 
challenges and opportunities at SIU and are further defined below. As the analysis 
progressed, these themes were modified and refined to better catch the nuances of the 
data. The first round of interviews provided direct qualitative evidence of HR-related 
inefficiencies. Recurring issues were categorized into major themes, forming the basis for 
the BSC implementation and Round 2 analysis. Through this process, it became possible 
to assess the use of HR practices while also uncovering potential inefficiencies, 
misalignments, and areas requiring intervention. Additionally, this analysis facilitated an 
evaluation of HR’s role in institutional performance, particularly in relation to HR policies 
and processes, faculty and staff satisfaction, and leadership effectiveness. It also 
provided an evidence-based foundation for assessing the university’s HR maturity level, 
positioning SIU’s practices within broader academic HR frameworks. The qualitative 
depth of the interviews allowed for a contextualized understanding of HR challenges, 
ensuring that the HR-BSC design would not only be theoretically robust but also 
practically applicable within SIU’s operational context. 
The complete interview responses, serving as the primary dataset for this analysis, are 
available in Appendix I, offering full transparency into the raw qualitative data that 
informed these findings. 
 
 
 
163 
5.3.1.1. 
Overview - NVivo Software 
 
As mentioned above, for this research, the software programme NVivo was acquired for 
a qualitative data analysis of the interview responses. NVivo can be used to organize, 
analyse, and visualize unstructured data, such as interview transcripts. The first step in 
NVivo analysis was to import the interview transcripts into the software. These were 
categorized based on different respondents and roles (e.g., Deans, etc.). Each 
transcript was stored as a separate source document within the NVivo database. NVivo 
enables coding, which is the process of tagging pieces of text to specific themes. In this 
case, codes (initial themes) based on key areas of HR concerns, included for example, 
strategic planning and communication, HR structure and policies, process development, 
and performance management, as discussed above and shown in Table 17:

164 
Each sentence or paragraph from the interviews that fit into one of these categories was 
coded accordingly. NVivo allows for multiple coding, so a single statement could be 
tagged under more than one theme. New themes emerged during interview Round 2 as 
interviewees highlighted new HR developments, challenges, and unexpected 
developments following the HR-BSC implementation. These were not part of the 
original framework but became important in post-interview analysis (see sub-chapter 
5.6.). 
To analyse the data further, NVivo was used for its ability to support a systematic and 
transparent approach to qualitative analysis. The software provides tools such as word 
frequency queries to identify commonly used terms in the interviews, sentiment analysis 
to assess the tone of responses, and thematic coding to identify recurring topics and 
patterns. These features helped ensure that the analysis was not only comprehensive 
but also methodologically consistent. The use of NVivo contributed to the development 
of structured, evidence-based insights, ultimately informing the design of a data-driven 
HR-BSC. The following sections offer a more in-depth analysis of the key findings. 
 
5.3.1.2. 
Thematic Coding Results 
 
Thematic analysis of the interview data revealed several key concerns about human 
resources at SIU.  
The following dominant themes are illustrated with direct quotes to highlight 
respondents’ experiences and observations. 
One of the most prominent themes identified in the interviews was the issue of 
strategic planning and communication, mentioned in 85% of responses. While 
strategic planning exists at some level, respondents consistently noted that it is neither 
effectively communicated nor consistently implemented across the institution. Several 
participants highlighted the lack of a clear vision beyond accreditation requirements and 
 
 
165 
growths expectations, with some expressing frustration over the inconsistent flow of 
information from leadership to staff. Employees reported confusion about how 
institutional objectives are translated into actionable steps. One respondent captured 
this frustration: "We know that there’s some sort of strategic plan, but nobody really 
knows what it entails. It’s mostly tied to accreditation and doesn’t seem to reflect our 
actual day-to-day priorities." (Faculty Member). Another respondent echoed similar 
concerns: "Information from leadership rarely reaches us in a clear and timely way. I 
feel like important decisions are made at the top, and we're only informed after things 
are already finalized." (Administrative Staff Member). 
Moreover, several respondents highlighted the inconsistent flow of information from 
leadership to staff, creating disconnection across different campuses: "We have regular 
meetings at the campus level, but broader institutional updates feel fragmented. 
Sometimes you hear important news from colleagues before it’s officially announced." 
(Director Position). 
Another major theme that emerged was the absence of a structured HR department 
and standardised policies, mentioned by 100% of respondents. reflecting widespread 
concerns about the decentralised nature of HR functions. Campus directors indicated 
that they were required to make independent decisions regarding hiring, on-boarding, 
and performance management, leading to inconsistent recruitment practices, reactive 
hiring processes, and limited on-boarding support for new employees — except for 
minimal IT training provided to faculty. A faculty member described this issue candidly: 
"New faculty members sometimes arrive on campus without any clear understanding of 
their role or what’s expected. There’s no proper on-boarding – apart from a brief IT 
session." Another respondent emphasized the lack of standardisation and structure: 
"There’s no real HR team. Everything HR-related lands on my desk – hiring, on-
boarding, payroll issues – and honestly, we’re just making things up as we go." (Director 
Position). This absence of standard HR processes has resulted in a highly fragmented 
and inefficient system, making long-term workforce planning nearly impossible. "We 
 
 
166 
don’t have time for strategic hiring – it’s mostly ‘we need someone now, let’s get this 
person on-board quickly.’ There’s no clear structure." (Director Position). 
Performance management was also identified as a critical issue, mentioned in 90% of 
responses, with most respondents noting the absence of structured performance 
evaluations. Faculty and staff assessments are conducted mainly for accreditation 
purposes rather than as a tool for employee development. One faculty member voiced 
concerns about the lack of a structured review system: "Our performance reviews seem 
to happen just to tick a box for accreditation reports — they don’t actually help us 
improve or set career goals." Another administrator voiced similar concerns: "I’ve been 
here for three years and haven’t had a proper performance review yet. There’s no 
feedback, no development conversations — just annual meetings that feel more like a 
formality." (Administrative Staff Member). 
The lack of key performance indicators (KPIs) for HR effectiveness, and leadership 
evaluation mechanisms such as 360-degree feedback were frequently cited: “We’ve 
never had a 360-degree feedback system. Managers and directors are rarely evaluated, 
which means there’s no accountability for leadership effectiveness. We don’t know 
whether managers are doing a good job until problems start arising” (Administrative 
Staff Member). 
Also, employee engagement and leadership development were recurring concerns 
across multiple interviews, raised in 80% of responses. Interviewees consistently 
reported that there is no systematic way to measure employee satisfaction, and 
engagement surveys are not conducted at an institutional level: "We’ve never had an 
employee satisfaction survey. We’re asked for input in informal ways, but there’s no 
proper structure to gather meaningful feedback." (Administrative Staff Member). 
Leadership training and professional development opportunities are limited, and faculty 
retention has been identified as a growing concern. Some respondents cited low 
motivation among employees, stemming from the lack of career growth opportunities 
and professional development initiatives: "If you want to develop your skills, you have to 
pursue it on your own. There’s no clear support from the university." (Director Position). 
 
 
167 
Another respondent commented on limited leadership training opportunities: 
“Professional development is almost non-existent. If you want to grow as a leader, you 
have to look for your own opportunities outside the university. There’s no clear path for 
internal career progression.” (Director Position). Low faculty retention rates were 
frequently mentioned, with some respondents linking this trend to poor professional 
development support and limited career progression opportunities: "It’s difficult to retain 
good faculty because there’s no clear growth path. Once people realize their career 
won’t move forward here, they leave." (Dean Position).  
Technology and HR efficiency emerged as another pressing theme in 75% of 
responses. HR processes remain largely manual, with no HR information system in 
place: "HR still works like it’s stuck in the 1990s — Excel sheets, paper forms, and lots 
of back-and-forth emails." (Dean Position). While some departments, such as 
admissions, have integrated technology effectively, HR functions continue to rely on 
outdated and inconsistent methods. Certain campuses have experimented with tools for 
tracking work hours, but there is no unified system to manage HR operations across the 
university: "We’ve been testing a work-hour tracking tool, but there’s no alignment 
between what we do here and what other campuses are doing." (Director Position). This 
fragmented approach to technology has resulted in inefficiencies, data inaccuracies, 
and delays in resolving HR issues: "HR issues take ages to resolve because everything 
is manual, and data is scattered across different systems." (Administrative Staff 
Member). 
Overall, the thematic coding of the interview data indicates widespread inefficiencies in 
HR management at SIU. The absence of a structured HR function and standardised 
processes, combined with weak performance evaluation systems and a lack of 
employee engagement initiatives, presents substantial challenges. Addressing these 
themes will be critical in the development of an effective HR-BSC to align HR processes 
with the university’s strategic goals.  
 
 
 
168 
5.3.1.3. 
Word Frequency and Sentiment Analysis 
 
The word frequency and sentiment analysis of the interview data provided further 
insight into the recurring concerns and confirmed the perception of insufficient HRM at 
SIU. The word frequency analysis was used to validate the noted themes. The most 
frequently mentioned words across all interviews were “HR” (98 times), “strategy” (67 
times), “performance” (51 times), “process” (44 times), and “structure” (38 times). These 
terms reflect the primary areas of concern among respondents, with a strong emphasis 
on HR functionality, the need for strategic planning, and the lack of structured 
processes and performance management systems. However, while frequency analysis 
is useful for identifying common terms and general themes, it offers limited insight into 
the underlying context, emotional tone, nuance and meaning behind given answers. For 
instance, frequent mentions of the word “strategy” could reflect positive aspirations or 
deep frustration — without qualitative context, this distinction remains unclear. To 
address this limitation, it is necessary to evaluate interview transcripts in more depth, 
where respondents provided detailed, often candid, reflections on institutional 
weaknesses. This qualitative layer adds meaning to the frequency data and allows a 
more accurate interpretation of stakeholder perspectives. The frequent mention of 
"strategy" illustrated this ambiguity - while strategic planning is recognized as important, 
many respondents feel it poorly implemented in practice. As one interviewee noted: "We 
keep hearing about ‘strategy’ from leadership, but I don’t see it reflected in HR 
practices. It’s all about accreditation rather than building a long-term plan for faculty and 
staff." (Administrative Staff Member). This quote highlights the disconnect between 
strategic rhetoric and operational reality, reinforcing the need to move beyond surface-
level frequency data and engage with the substance of stakeholder concerns. 
Accordingly, while the high occurrence of “HR” in the interviews suggests importance, a 
deeper look reveals that respondents actually view human resources as a fundamental 
weakness within the institution. This becomes evident in the qualitative responses, 
where 95% of interviewees expressed concern about the lack of a formal HR structure 
and the ad-hoc nature of HR decision-making across campuses: "HR is supposed to be 
 
 
169 
a guiding function in any institution, but here, it feels like an afterthought. Decisions are 
made without clear policies, and each campus does things differently." (Faculty 
Member). 
Similarly, the prominence of "performance" suggests further concerns, however without 
qualitative context, it is unclear whether respondents are critiquing the absence of 
evaluation, the quality of existing processes, or both. A closer look at the transcripts 
clarifies this point. One interviewee commented: “Performance evaluations don’t really 
exist in a meaningful way. There’s no process to measure effectiveness, no KPIs — just 
informal discussions that don’t lead to any real development” (Director Position). This 
example reveals specific frustrations with the lack of structure and accountability, 
illustrating how qualitative insights help explain the deeper meaning behind the 
recurring terms. 
The terms "process" and "structure" appeared frequently in discussions about 
inconsistent HR practices and the decentralised approach to HR decision-making. Here, 
several respondents noted that HR processes are either missing entirely or vary 
considerably across campuses, leading to confusion and inefficiency: "Every campus 
handles HR differently. There’s no clear process for on-boarding, no structured 
approach to recruitment, and no consistency in how policies are applied." (Director 
Position).  
The sentiment analysis of the responses shows that the overall tone of the interviews 
was predominantly negative, with 65% of statements expressing dissatisfaction with 
HR-related processes. While this may be partly influenced by the interview design, 
which focused on identifying challenges and areas for improvement, the use of open-
ended questions allowed for a range of perspectives. Moreover, the consistency of 
critical feedback across roles and campuses suggests that the negative sentiment 
reflects a broader pattern rather than a result of response bias induced by the 
questioning approach. The most common concerns included the absence of an HR 
department, reactive hiring practices, lack of performance management, and limited 
professional development opportunities. Respondents expressed frustration over the 
 
 
170 
fact that HR policies are either non-existent or inconsistently applied across different 
campuses: "HR here feels like a crisis management team rather than a proactive 
department. We only get involved in HR matters when there’s a problem, never in a way 
that helps employees grow." (Administrative Staff Member). Additionally, many 
interviewees pointed to a lack of leadership evaluation and employee engagement 
initiatives, which they believe contribute to declining motivation and retention issues.  
Neutral sentiment accounted for 25% of responses, primarily in discussions about daily 
operations, accreditation requirements, and the general structure of the university. 
These responses were more descriptive, outlining how HR currently functions rather 
than expressing strong opinions about its effectiveness. One respondent pointed out 
that: "We know that accreditation requires some level of HR documentation, and that’s 
happening, but beyond that, there’s no real structure to HR policies." (Administrative 
Staff Member). 
Only 10% of responses carried a positive sentiment, with some interviewees 
recognizing opportunities for professional development and expressing optimism about 
the potential for improvement. However, even in these cases, respondents noted that 
professional growth opportunities are either unsupported or inconsistently available 
across different departments: "There are good opportunities for development if you 
know where to look, but there’s no clear support from HR, because there is no ‘real HR’. 
It’s up to the individual to figure it out." (Dean Position). 
The word frequency and sentiment analysis confirm the clear need for a structured, 
strategic approach to HR at SIU. The dominant negative sentiment and recurring 
concerns about HR inefficiencies reveal substantial gaps in HR strategy, structure, and 
processes, particularly in areas such as recruitment, performance management, 
employee engagement, and leadership evaluation. While employees recognise the 
importance of talent and change management, the absence of supporting systems 
prevents consistent implementation. The lack of standardised HR practices across 
campuses has resulted in fragmented employee experiences: “HR is different 
depending on where you are. Some campuses have structured recruitment processes, 
 
 
171 
while others rely on personal networks and informal hiring. It’s completely inconsistent.” 
(Administrative Staff Member). 
To address these issues, a coordinated approach is needed — one that establishes 
clear policies, defines responsibilities, and integrates performance metrics to enable 
accountability and institutional learning.  By addressing these concerns and leveraging 
the few identified strengths, SIU could create a more structured HR environment. 
 
5.3.1.4. 
SWOT Analysis on NVivo Results 
 
A SWOT analysis categorizes findings into strengths, weaknesses, opportunities, and 
threats. It helps identify areas where SIU excels, challenges it faces, external 
opportunities it can leverage, and risks that need mitigation. The SWOT analysis from the 
NVivo results provided a structured overview of SIU’s HR situation, highlighting internal 
challenges and external factors affecting HR performance and strategic planning. It 
served as a valuable foundation for building a BSC that aligns HR processes with 
strategic goals. The key insights are summarized below: 
 
 
172 
 
Figure 16: SWOT Analysis of SIU University (Source: Author) 
In a SWOT analysis, the internal and external views refer to different aspects of an 
organisation's environment that influence decision-making and strategic planning. The 
internal view includes strengths and weaknesses and focuses on factors within SIU’s 
control — it’s resources, capabilities and operational structures. Strengths include, 
among other things, internationalisation, accreditation compliance and experienced 
leadership, which contribute to institutional credibility and academic stability. However, 
these strengths are not leveraged to establish structured HR policies or workforce 
planning. Weaknesses are substantial, primarily the absence of a formal HR department, 
lack of standardised processes, and poor performance management. HR functions are 
decentralised, with Campus Directors making ad-hoc decisions on hiring, on-boarding, 
and evaluations. Recruitment is reactive rather than strategic, and performance reviews 
focus on accreditation standards rather than employee development. Employee 
engagement and leadership assessments are not systematically measured, making 
retention and workplace concerns difficult to address. Yet, the external view, including

opportunities and threats, assesses factors outside the organisation’s control that can 
impact its performance. Opportunities include creating a centralised HR department, 
implementing structured performance management, and leveraging HR technology. 
Moreover, leadership evaluation tools, such as 360-degree feedback, could enhance 
managerial effectiveness, while HR automation could streamline processes like 
performance evaluations, employee engagement surveys, and payroll. On the other 
hand, threats involve employee dissatisfaction, declining engagement, and accreditation 
risks due to HR inefficiencies. Without structured performance management and 
professional development, faculty and staff turnover may increase, creating operational 
instability. Additionally, HR gaps could jeopardize accreditation compliance, affecting 
SIU’s reputation and ability to attract qualified faculty and potential students. 
The SWOT analysis highlighted the need for a strategic, data-informed HR approach. By 
leveraging these insights, SIU could prioritize HR initiatives based not only on perceived 
gaps but also on existing assets, such as its accreditation strengths and leadership 
experience. At the same time, it allows for targeted intervention in areas of weakness, 
such as inconsistent performance management, decentralised HR practices, and the 
slow adoption of HR technologies. Strengths and opportunities, on the other hand, 
highlight aspects that can be enhanced to drive institutional growth. Beyond organizing 
qualitative insights from interviews, a SWOT analysis translates them into quantifiable 
KPIs, making it easier to establish measurable HR goals. For example, weaknesses like 
the absence of structured performance evaluations can be addressed through 
quantifiable KPIs such as % of staff and faculty with completed annual performance 
reviews. Strengths, such as accreditation compliance can be leveraged and enhanced 
using KPIs like number of strategic HR contributions to accreditation renewals. And 
opportunities, including improved employee engagement and career development, can 
be tracked using for example the participation rate in professional development 
programs. 
Since not all HR improvements can be implemented simultaneously, this approach also 
helps prioritize initiatives based on urgency and feasibility. Additionally, its simple and 
visual structure makes it an effective tool for gaining stakeholder buy-in.  
 
 
174 
The described findings guide the creation of a HR-BSC, ensuring HR initiatives align with 
institutional goals and drive measurable improvements in performance management. 
Ensuring that the HR-BSC captures only the most relevant and impactful performance 
measures prevents unnecessary or redundant tracking. 
Implementing HR technology for efficiency would help streamline processes such as 
recruitment, performance tracking, and payroll. Introducing a university-wide HR 
management system would improve data tracking, automate key processes, and support 
a more structured approach to HR management. Standardizing these processes across 
campuses will ensure that HR policies are applied consistently, enhancing overall 
efficiency and accountability within the organisation. 
The interview results, together with the results from the literature review and internal 
document analysis build the basis for the design and implementation of the HR-BSC.  
 
5.3.2. 
Defining the SIU HR-BSC Framework: Key Considerations 
 
The development of the HR-BSC framework at SIU followed a structured process, 
drawing primarily on insights from stakeholder interviews, supplemented by baseline data 
analysis and leadership input, to ensure alignment with both institutional strategy and 
employee expectations. This phase focused on translating findings from interview Round 
1 into a concrete strategic direction for HRM at SIU.  
The first stage involved analysing interview responses from faculty, staff, and leadership 
to identify recurring HR challenges and strategic gaps (see section 5.3). The interview 
findings highlighted the importance of a structured, strategic HR approach to strengthen 
recruitment, performance management, and employee engagement at SIU. To validate 
and refine these insights, a two-day online workshop was conducted with SIU’s 
leadership team, including the CEO, Provost, Campus Directors, Director of Admissions, 
and Dean of Academics. The workshop was held virtually due to geographical distances 
and ongoing COVID-19 travel restrictions. Moderated by the Professor of Strategy, it was 
 
 
175 
designed as an interactive, participatory forum, combining a short presentation of the 
interview results with breakout discussions and real-time collaborative exercises.   
The workshop had three primary objectives: (1) to present and discuss the key findings 
from the first round of interviews, (2) to collectively define SIU’s institutional priorities and 
strategic orientation, and (3) to align HRM priorities with the broader goals of the 
university. Participants were encouraged to share their perspectives on HR-related 
challenges in open discussions, validate the emerging themes from the qualitative 
analysis, and co-create a shared vision for the future direction of the university. One of 
the key outcomes of the workshop was the redefinition of SIU’s strategic orientation and 
institutional goals. SIU’s vision was revised to emphasize its role as an international hub 
for academic exchange and leadership development: “A place where students from all 
corners of the world converge to exchange ideas, challenge norms, and grow together in 
a supportive, inclusive environment.” (CEO, SIU). This vision reflects SIU’s commitment 
to fostering a global learning community. Additionally, five core elements were identified 
as central to SIU’s mission, shaping its approach to education and institutional 
development: 
1. Qualified teaching faculty with an international orientation – ensuring that students 
benefit from diverse academic perspectives and global expertise. 
2. Problem-oriented interdisciplinary approaches – integrating knowledge across 
disciplines to address real-world challenges. 
3. Experiential learning with practical applications – emphasizing hands-on learning to 
bridge theory and practice. 
4. Social and ecological responsibility – embedding ethical considerations and 
sustainability into academic programs. 
5. Efficient resource management – ensuring financial and operational sustainability in 
university governance. 
Recognizing the importance of stakeholder input, the workshop participants agreed to 
further refine the strategic objectives following the HR-BSC implementation and interview 
Round 2. Hence, the workshop facilitated the creation of a strategic roadmap, linking 
 
 
176

The newly defined strategic objectives are supported by initiatives developed during the 
leadership workshop. Further outcomes from this workshop and the resulting 
presentation contain additional topics beyond the scope of the HR-BSC project and are 
therefore included in Appendix J for reference.  
However, a central outcome of the workshop, and highly relevant to the HR-BSC, was 
the decision to define SIU’s value proposition. While the mission /vision outline why the 
university exists and where it aspires to be long-term, the value proposition explains what 
unique benefits the university offers its primary stakeholders and potentially why they 
should choose it over alternatives. In this case, it is intended to distinguish the university 
through its academic model, global orientation, and student experience with an emphasis 
on employability, experiential learning, and interdisciplinary education. These priorities 
directly shaped the HR-BSC design, particularly in relation to faculty recruitment, staff 
development, and process management by introducing more nuanced HRM 
requirements. For instance, global orientation, as a value proposition pillar, translates 
into a need for cross-campus staff mobility and intercultural competence. This is then 
reflected in the internal process perspective of the BSC, where metrics such as faculty 
 
 
177 
collaboration across campuses can be tracked. KPIs for employability, as a value 
proposition, might include graduate employment rate, or employer feedback. By defining 
a clear value proposition, SIU converts its broad mission into a competitive potential that 
shapes HR recruitment, program development, and performance metrics in the HR-BSC. 
Further, SIU’s strategic goals — growth, differentiation, and operational enablement — 
align closely with the HR-BSC perspectives. Growth relates to the learning and growth, 
internal process, and employee perspectives, as it depends on faculty development, 
scalable HR processes, and staff engagement. Differentiation is supported by the 
employee and learning perspectives, through the recruitment of globally oriented faculty 
and development of interdisciplinary teaching skills, while the internal process 
perspective reinforces this through structured support for innovative learning. 
Operational enablement and value delivery align with the internal process and financial 
perspectives, emphasizing efficient HR systems and strategic resource management. 
These connections ensure the HR-BSC supports SIU’s broader mission and strategic 
direction. Table 19 summarizes the alignment:

With strategic goals in place, the next step was to link HR initiatives to these priorities. 
Based on the discussions, specific HR priorities were identified, including standardizing 
processes, enhancing development programs, and improving evaluation methods. Each 
of SIU’s strategic objectives required strong HR support in terms of people, processes, 
 
 
178 
and systems, for example, through implementing a structured governance model, 
defining roles and responsibilities, and establishing consistent reporting structures.  
The HR-BSC framework translates these strategic priorities into actionable HR practices 
across its four perspectives. It ensures that HR actively contributes to SIU’s growth, 
differentiation, and operational excellence by driving standardised processes, strengthen 
faculty performance, enhancing student experience, and checking that human capital is 
effectively leveraged to accelerate the university’s strategic plan. SIU is so positioned to 
systematically track HR performance, measure its strategic impact, and continuously 
refine practices to support long-term success. The next section outlines how each of the 
four BSC perspectives has been applied to shape SIU’s HRM approach. 
 
5.4. 
Building the Balanced Scorecard 
 
The HR-BSC at SIU must function as a people-driven enabler of institutional strategy, 
ensuring that HR practices directly support the university’s ‘2026 strategic objectives’, as 
defined in the workshop. By aligning HR initiatives with organisational growth, academic 
transformation, student experience, and operational excellence, SIU can build a 
workforce that is engaged, high-performing, and adaptable to institutional changes. The 
BSC’s four perspectives provide a structured framework for organizing HR efforts. The 
following section describes how each of the BSC perspectives is applied to SIU’s HR 
strategy. 
The financial perspective within the HR-BSC focuses on ensuring financial 
sustainability by optimizing workforce-related expenditures. At SIU, this means managing 
human resources cost-effectively across its four international campuses, while balancing 
institutional investments with tuition-driven revenue streams. Given SIU’s multi-campus 
structure, faculty recruitment, compensation models, and HR-driven operational 
efficiencies must be carefully optimized to maintain financial sustainability. One of the 
key challenges SIU faces is the variability in labour costs, regulatory requirements, and 
varying salary expectations across different countries (USA, Spain, France, and 
 
 
179 
Germany). This requires a structured approach to workload distribution, performance-
based contracts, and resource allocation that aligns with SIU’s global budget constraints. 
Additionally, as a tuition-dependent institution, the integration of HR cost analysis with 
student enrolment projections is critical for long-term financial planning. Investing in 
training Deans and Campus Directors in financial decision-making on a broader scale 
can further enhance budgetary accountability and enable decentralised, yet well-
governed, resource distribution. Aligned with SIU’s strategic objectives to “differentiate” 
and “enable operations,” there is also a need to redefine, standardize, and implement 
financial processes across campuses. This includes leveraging digitalization, enhancing 
technological capabilities, and optimizing campus infrastructure to improve operational 
efficiency and support sustainable growth. 
The customer perspective in HR emphasizes enhancing faculty and staff capabilities to 
meet the evolving stakeholder expectations, aligning directly with SIU’s strategic goals of 
“differentiation” and developing a strong “value proposition.” SIU operates in a 
competitive international higher education market, where students increasingly view 
themselves as customers with high expectations for flexibility, quality, and career-
oriented learning experiences. HR plays a key role in ensuring that faculty and staff 
possess the pedagogical, cross-cultural, and industry-relevant expertise required to meet 
these expectations. Given SIU’s multi-campus and mobility-driven academic model, 
hiring faculty who can adapt to diverse student demographics and teach across different 
learning environments (online, hybrid, and in-person across multiple countries) is 
essential. Furthermore, as SIU wants to expand its programmes and executive 
education, specialized faculty recruitment and ongoing professional development in 
emerging fields are necessary to maintain relevance in a rapidly changing job market. 
Additionally, HR strategies must support a student-centred service model, ensuring that 
administrative staff and faculty are trained to provide personalized academic advising, 
mobility support, and cross-campus integration, strengthening student retention rates and 
ultimately SIU’s institutional reputation. 
The process perspective highlights HR’s role in optimizing institutional workflows to 
support SIU’s strategic goals. Given SIU’s international structure, with campuses in four 
 
 
180 
countries, standardizing HR processes presents a substantial challenge. A unified yet 
adaptable framework is needed — one that ensures consistency while accommodating 
variations in local labour laws and administrative requirements. Key HR functions such 
as recruitment, on-boarding, performance evaluation, and contract management must be 
streamlined to reduce fragmentation and inefficiencies. Historically, the absence of a 
centralised HR system at SIU led to inconsistent practices across campuses, particularly 
in hiring and performance management. The implementation of the HR-BSC, alongside 
the introduction of integrated HR software, addresses these issues by automating key 
processes, standardizing procedures, and improving transparency in decision-making. 
To sustain these improvements, SIU must also cultivate a culture of continuous process 
refinement. As the institution evolves academically and operationally, HR policies must 
be regularly reviewed and updated to remain relevant and effective. Supporting this 
transformation requires targeted training for faculty and staff in digital tools and workflow 
automation, ensuring that SIU remains agile and efficient. These efforts directly support 
SIU’s strategic pillars of “enable operations,” “differentiate,” and “grow,” reinforcing HR’s 
critical role in driving institutional performance through effective process management. 
The development perspective focuses on continuous investment in faculty and staff 
competencies to drive institutional performance and innovation. Given SIU’s ambition to 
establish itself as a growing, globally recognized, high-quality institution, investing in 
faculty and staff development is crucial for sustaining academic excellence and 
institutional growth. The HR-BSC highlights the importance of structured professional 
development programs, leadership training, and interdisciplinary collaboration to ensure 
that SIU can meet the demands of set goals in a rapidly evolving higher education 
landscape. Unlike traditional universities with strong research funding, SIU’s focus is 
predominantly on teaching and student mobility, requiring HR to implement teaching-
focused mentorship programs, faculty exchange initiatives, and continuous upskilling 
opportunities. Additionally, given SIU’s multi-campus model, HR must facilitate 
international collaboration among faculty, promoting shared best practices and cross-
campus academic initiatives and processes. Ensuring clear career progression pathways 
will be key to attracting and retaining high-quality faculty members while fostering an 
 
 
181 
institutional culture of innovation, pedagogical excellence, and student engagement. 
Altogether, these development-focused efforts support SIU’s broader strategic objectives 
— including “enable operations,” “value proposition,” “differentiate,” and “grow” — by 
creating a learning culture that fuels academic innovation and strengthens institutional 
capacity for long-term success.  
To ensure effective implementation, the HR-BSC had to be widely understood and 
adopted across all university units. Given the structural variations across SIU’s 
campuses and departments, the framework had to strike a balance between flexibility 
and consistency, allowing localized adaptation while preserving strategic coherence. 
Standardisation efforts began early to ensure that while decentralised units maintain 
operational autonomy, they align with institution-wide strategic priorities and KPIs. 
Following recommendations from the first round of interviews, SIU leadership recognized 
the need for a reliable HR technology system to enhance standardisation in processes 
and performance tracking. As a result, SIU invested in ‘Personio’, an HR software 
platform as the central tool to establish a unified foundation for transparency and 
consistency. This system enabled the centralization of key administrative functions, such 
as file and record keeping, vacation tracking, leave requests, payroll processes, and 
performance evaluation, all of which became accessible online. In parallel, institution-
wide access to Outlook calendars and Microsoft Teams improved communication, 
coordination, and visibility of staff availability across locations. 
Furthermore, in support of the HR-BSC rollout and SIU’s strategic realignment, new 
positions were created and additional personnel were hired, particularly in global and 
cross-campus roles, such as a Chief Operations Officer (COO), Chief Academic Officer 
(CAO), Dean of Students, Director of Institutional Effectiveness, Head EDTech, 
Controller, etc. as shown in the new organisational chart, marked in green: 
 
 
182 
 
Figure 17: SIU Organisational Chart as a Result of the BSC Project (Source: SIU Leadership Workshop) 
These roles were designed to strengthen institutional capacity and ensure that the 
university could effectively implement and sustain its HR transformation.

A key feature of the HR-BSC template is the inclusion of KPIs for each perspective, with 
three to five core metrics per field to maintain clarity and traceability. By defining both 
target and current values, the scorecard provides a structured mechanism for 
performance tracking, ensuring that HR initiatives contribute meaningfully to

improvement.  Figure 18 provides an example of a HR-BSC dashboard that maps HR 
KPIs to SIU's strategic objectives: 
 
Figure 18: SIU HR-BSC Dashboard (Source: Author, SIU Heidelberg Campus) 
By aligning HR initiatives with SIU’s strategic objectives, the HR-BSC framework ensures 
that human capital is effectively managed, developed, and optimized to support financial 
sustainability, academic differentiation, operational efficiency, and institutional growth. 
Additionally, the BSC serves as a critical communication tool within SIU, ensuring that 
employees at all levels understand and engage with the university’s strategic direction. 
For it to be truly effective, it must focus on essential elements that make its objectives 
clear, accessible, and actionable. The balancing mechanisms within the BSC provide a 
structured way to prioritize strategic initiatives, ensuring alignment between top 
management and operational teams. While each perspective of the HR-BSC is typically 
given equal weight, adjustments may be necessary based on organisational needs and 
emerging challenges. Following Kaplan and Norton’s corporate scorecard model, the 
 
 
185 
HR-BSC was tailored to SIU’s specific strategic goals, integrating customized metrics, 
action programs, and performance indicators to enhance HR-driven decision-making. 
Despite the advantages of a structured approach, SIU faced several developmental 
challenges, in both academic and administrative areas. Faculty members often view 
academic self-governance as time-consuming and lacking incentives, leading to low 
motivation to participate in university management and strategic planning. Additionally, 
analysis results from the interview Round 1 revealed that organisational change creates 
stress and uncertainty, potentially reducing employee commitment to institutional 
transformation. These findings highlight the importance of addressing the emotional and 
cultural dimensions of change alongside structural reforms. 
Another key challenge SIU faced was the fragmentation between university units. As 
mentioned, administrative staff, faculty, and central university functions often operated in 
silos, with limited collaboration and alignment. While administrative structures follow 
hierarchical models, faculties and campuses tend to function more autonomously, 
creating gaps in strategic coordination. Existing processes across campuses remained 
partially standardised, with many policies being locally adapted rather than uniformly 
implemented. Addressing these challenges through HR policies that foster collaboration, 
enhance transparency, and introduce structured incentive mechanisms will be crucial in 
ensuring SIU’s long-term success and institutional coherence.  
A future HR department is expected to assess individual performance drivers in 
coordination with university leadership to ensure alignment with institutional objectives, 
as for example, the weighting of HR objectives may also inform future compensation 
models. 
 
5.5. 
The Implementation of the BSC 
 
Scholars and business experts have extensively discussed the implementation of the 
BSC over the years, providing insights into its benefits, challenges, and best practices. 
 
 
186 
As the original creators of the Balanced Scorecard concept, Kaplan and Norton (1996) 
have written extensively on its implementation. They provide a comprehensive guide to 
implementing and using the BSC. They emphasize the importance of aligning the 
scorecard with strategic objectives and ensuring that it becomes an integral part of the 
organisation's management processes. Authors collectively highlight the importance of 
engaging employees at all levels, adapting to change, and leveraging technology for 
effective implementation (Niven, 2002 and 2014; Buytendijk, 2008; Neely et al., 2002).  
It was essential to tailor the implementation process to the specific needs and context of 
SIU, such as its multi-campus structure. This made active involvement of operational 
staff vital, as it not only facilitated smoother implementation but also fostered stronger 
commitment and accountability, making strategic HR initiatives more sustainable. To 
support this, operational engagement was embedded through two main tools. First, 
Campus Directors were responsible for monitoring and contextualising HR-BSC 
implementation at their sites, aligning strategic goals with local operations. Second, the 
use of Personio software enabled a standardised performance management process, 
with line managers conducting evaluations to align individual roles with institutional 
priorities.  
The HR-BSC implantation at SIU required a phased approach to ensure smooth 
execution and measurable impact. A 9-month implementation plan divided into three key 
phases provided a tight roadmap for integrating the HR-BSC into SIU’s HR operations:  
 
 
187 
 
Figure 19: HR-BSC 3 Phases Implementation Plan (Source: Author, SIU)  
This structured approach ensured a systematic and data-driven transition toward an 
integrated HR performance measurement system. 
Phase 1 focused on establishing a strong foundation for the successful implementation of 
the HR-BSC. The first key step was the formation of an HR task force, consisting of the 
four Campus Directors and the Dean, who served as project leads at their respective 
locations. To ensure clarity and alignment, a two-hour introductory workshop was held 
online. This session outlined the objectives of the HR-BSC, defined the specific 
responsibilities of the campus directors, such as training line managers on the use of 
Personio, and provided a forum to address initial questions and concerns. This 
preparatory step helped establish a shared understanding and facilitated a smoother 
transition into the implementation phase. A central aspect of this phase was the review 
and validation of KPIs across the four perspectives of the BSC framework. This step 
aimed to define clear HR objectives that aligned with the university’s long-term strategic 
vision while setting measurable targets for each KPI. To illustrate how these strategic 
objectives were translated into actionable HR practices, the following figure and table 
 
 
188 
present an excerpt of the HR-BSC framework and sample KPIs implemented at the Paris 
Campus — with particular emphasis on the development perspective, reflecting SIU’s 
commitment to faculty and staff growth, cross-campus collaboration, and long-term 
institutional advancement: 
 
Figure 20: BSC Learning and Development Perspective (Source: Author, SIU Workshop)  
The resulting table presents a structured overview of baseline HR-BSC indicators aligned 
with SIU’s overarching strategic objectives, such as “Grow,” “Differentiate,” “Enable 
Operations,” and “Enable Value Proposition.” Each KPI was designed to be standardised 
across campuses to enable consistent tracking and meaningful comparison. However, 
campus-specific differences, such as variations in labour costs, hiring processes, 
workweek standards, and cultural expectations, required careful adjustment to ensure 
fairness and relevance. These differences, and their implications for HR performance 
management, will be further explored in the discussion chapter. Adapting KPIs to local 
contexts ultimately supported the creation of a framework that remained actionable and 
strategically aligned across SIU’s global operations. 
 
 
 
189 
While the detailed KPIs for each perspective and campus are presented in Appendix k, 
the following table offers a specific example from the Learning and Development 
perspective at the Paris campus: 
 
Table 21: KPI – BSC Learning and Development Perspective (Source: Author, SIU) 
To support implementation, a communication and change management plan (Appendix 
L) was developed to engage faculty, employees, and key stakeholders, ensuring 
transparency, fostering buy-in, and minimizing resistance to change. The plan includes 
the phases ‘awareness, training, implementation, and evaluation’, including actions and 
communication methods. For example, a town hall meeting in the first month of the 
implementation and campus briefings, scheduled by the Campus Directors, were used to 
keep all four campuses informed and aligned. Hands-on training workshops were 
conducted to introduce new systems, accompanied by role-specific guides and peer 
mentoring. Evaluation metrics such as training completion rates, software adoption, and 
employee sentiment were defined to monitor engagement and guide continuous 
improvement. Together, these elements ensured the implementation process was 
transparent, inclusive, and adaptable across SIU's multi-campus environment. 
 
 
190 
Phase 2 of the HR-BSC implementation focused on integrating the Personio software as 
the central platform for tracking HR performance across all SIU campuses. To support 
adoption, Campus Directors and line managers received targeted training from an 
external HR agency on key functions such as performance reviews, absence tracking, 
and employee record management. Each Campus Director then entered baseline 
indicators and evaluation metrics into the system. However, inconsistencies in data 
collection across campuses, due to the previous lack of standardised processes, 
emphasised the need for greater alignment. In addition to performance tracking, 
Personio was used to support the standardisation of core HR procedures. With guidance 
from the HR agency, processes related to hiring, on-boarding, and performance 
evaluation were formalised and documented within the platform. Training sessions were 
also delivered to line managers and key staff, focusing on the use of the performance 
evaluation form and the development of clear, measurable KPIs aligned with institutional 
objectives. These efforts aimed to improve transparency and consistency across the 
institution, while also building internal capacity for practical HR management and 
continuous improvement in line with the HR-BSC framework. 
One of the most important milestones in this phase was the implementation of structured 
performance reviews based on measurable KPIs. These reviews provided a data-driven 
approach to faculty and staff assessments. For example, performance evaluations for 
administrative staff were set up to include criteria such as responsiveness, task 
completion rates, and participation in training sessions, while planned faculty KPIs 
focused on teaching effectiveness (based on student feedback), involvement in 
curriculum development, and engagement in interdisciplinary activities. These structured 
evaluation processes were designed and integrated into the Personio platform, marking a 
transition from informal assessments toward a more transparent and accountable 
performance management system (see Appendix M Template). While full implementation 
was still pending, the framework established the foundation for consistent, KPI-based 
evaluations aligned with institutional goals.  
To maintain a dynamic and adaptable HR-BSC framework, a feedback session was held, 
after three months, with university Provost, Deans and Campus Directors. The 2-hour 
 
 
191 
session was held online and provided an opportunity to report on the progress of the HR-
BSC implementation and to discuss any challenges, questions, or areas requiring 
additional support. These measures helped ensure that operational staff were 
meaningfully engaged in the rollout and refinement of the HR-BSC. 
The final phase focused on evaluating the effectiveness of the HR-BSC, optimizing 
processes, and ensuring its long-term sustainability. A comprehensive performance 
review was conducted, analysing data from a second round of interviews with 21 
stakeholders. The findings from this evaluation are detailed in sub-chapter 5.6. To 
promote transparency and institutional alignment, a final workshop was held where HR-
BSC results were presented to university leadership and interview participants. The 
session included an overview of interview findings as discussed in the following sub-
chapter. Participants shared feedback and discussed implementation challenges, such 
as the standardisation of processes. Breakout discussions focused on integrating KPIs 
into future evaluations, and explored ways to embed a data-driven HR culture. The 
workshop reinforced the long-term value of structured performance measurement for 
improving accountability and strategic alignment across SIU. 
This research resulted in the development of a university-wide HR-BSC framework 
tailored to SIU’s strategic goals, including defined KPIs and implementation guidelines. 
While it does not cover ongoing review sessions or the integration of complementary 
divisional or departmental scorecards, these areas represent opportunities for future 
research and institutional development. In particular, the creation of departmental-level 
scorecards could enhance strategic alignment across functions and campuses.  
 
5.6. 
Data Analysis - Interview Round 2  
 
The second round of interviews provided an in-depth assessment of how the HR-BSC 
implementation has influenced SIU’s HR practices, organisational processes, policies 
and culture. This phase included feedback from 21 respondents across different 
 
 
192 
campuses, among them four newly appointed members of the global team within the 
university’s newly established matrix structure. A detailed analysis of the interview 
responses and emerging themes is presented in Section 5.6.1. The new structure marks 
a shift from a siloed, campus-based model to a more integrated, collaborative framework. 
As drivers of this transformation, the global team plays a central role in enforcing 
standardisation and fostering cross-campus collaboration. Interviewing these new 
leaders helped evaluate how well HR-BSC principles are being understood and 
implemented, while also offering insight into on-boarding and institutional clarity. Their 
fresh perspectives complement those of returning staff, who were able to reflect on 
progress over time — providing a well-rounded view of HR-BSC’s current impact. 
Using NVivo once more, the analysis followed a structured and replicable approach, 
starting with data preparation, during which all interview transcripts were reviewed and 
uploaded into NVivo as textual sources. Each transcript was categorized by the 
interviewee’s role (e.g., Provost, Dean, Registrar, Faculty, COO), campus location (e.g. 
Tampa, Madrid, Paris, Heidelberg), and interview round (Round 1: Pre-HR-BSC; Round 
2: Post-HR-BSC). This classification enabled effective filtering and comparison across 
stakeholder groups and institutional contexts. Next, the transcripts were coded into 
relevant themes as described in Section 4.4.2.2., enabling the identification of key 
patterns and issues related to the HR-BSC initiative. Key coding categories included 
Strategic Planning & Communication, HR Structure & Standardisation, Employee 
Development & Satisfaction, Technology & Process Improvement, and Organisational 
Culture & Leadership. Coding was applied line by line, allowing patterns to emerge in 
how different groups perceived the state of HR before and after the introduction of the 
HR-BSC. Findings from Round 1 (Pre-HR-BSC) vs. Round 2 (Post-HR-BSC) were then 
compared, to evaluate changes in perceptions, practices, and the overall impact of HR-
BSC implementation. This involved using NVivo’s matrix queries and comparison tools to 
identify shifts in sentiment, recurring topics, and differences across roles and campuses. 
For example, increased mentions of standardisation and HR technology in Round 2 
responses were contrasted with the more frequent references to fragmentation and 
 
 
193 
uncertainty in Round 1. The structured categorization allowed for nuanced comparisons 
across stakeholder roles and campus locations.  
Finally, key takeaways were synthesized, and remaining gaps were identified to guide 
future improvements. These included areas where the HR-BSC had not yet fully 
addressed operational or communication challenges.  
 
5.6.1.

The thematic coding analysis examined the themes strategic planning and 
communication, HR structure and standardisation, technology and process improvement, 
employee development and satisfaction, and organisational structure and culture. A 
summary of findings is presented in the table below:
