The feedback revealed clear improvements in strategic planning and 
communication at SIU following the implementation of the HR-BSC, as mentioned by 
95% of respondents. One of the most noticeable changes was the revision and formal 
integration of the university’s mission and vision into the strategic plan. Unlike in the past, 
where these elements were unclear or inconsistently referenced, they are now 
prominently displayed across all campuses, reinforcing a shared institutional direction. As 
one respondent noted: “The official mission is hanging up... today, theoretically, I see a 
mission and how growth can happen” (Director Position). Communication has also 
evolved, with more structured efforts to circulate strategic objectives and operational 
updates. New initiatives such as quarterly Townhall meetings, Executive Committee 
sessions, and internal newsletters were widely acknowledged: “The strategic plan was 
communicated and followed up in Townhall and Executive Committee meetings” (Global 
 
 
195 
Position). “The strategic plan is communicated and reinforced, e.g. in Townhall meeting 
by CEO on how we are developing” (Global Position). Despite these efforts, 
communication remains a work in progress. Some interviewees pointed to inconsistency 
in how information is cascaded across levels: “The BSC helped with the communication, 
but there is still a long way to go – who to communicate with is sometimes still unclear” 
(Director Position). “Communication is still a weakness… due to little follow-ups” 
(Administrative Staff Member).  
The HR-BSC implementation has helped clarify key internal and external 
stakeholders, including employees, faculty, students, investors, and accreditation 
bodies, creating stronger alignment between HR policies and institutional priorities, as 
noted by 75% of interviewees. The focus on stakeholder engagement was evident in the 
way interviewees described how goals were now linked to measurable outcomes: 
“Performance appraisals now happen bi-yearly… Each manager defines development 
objectives for the year” (Global Position). Still, some raised questions about how these 
strategies translate into practice: “The strategic plan emphasises talent development, but 
it’s not clear how that connects to actual training opportunities or workload adjustments” 
(Faculty Member). 
The establishment of a formal HR function marked a major structural improvement, 
mentioned in 100% of responses. As one interviewee shared: “Yes, now we have one – 
this wasn’t the way in the past, but it is very helpful” (Global Position). Unlike before, 
when HR tasks were handled informally by Campus Directors with little consistency 
across campuses, SIU now has an external HR team supporting each campus, 
supplemented by local HR lawyers to ensure compliance with labour laws. While this 
marks a major step forward, the full development of an internal, centralised HR 
department remains a long-term goal. 
Standardisation also saw major gains. The implementation of the HR software 
Personio was cited by 95% of participants as a critical milestone: “Personio… helped 
standardise processes, it's good to see when colleagues are in or out of office across 
campuses — this makes communication and teamwork easier” (Director Position). “The 
 
 
196 
standardisation makes it easier to plan faculty sessions for all campuses” (Dean 
Position). Before the HR-BSC, HR-related tasks varied widely across campuses, with 
little consistency in how recruitment, on-boarding, informing and assessing were 
conducted. The introduction of automated HR processes has increased efficiency and 
transparency, for example, a standardised on-boarding process ensures that all new 
employees go through a structured introduction to the institution. However, some 
respondents noted that while standardisation has improved operations, it has also 
introduced challenges in balancing global policies with local campus-specific needs. For 
example, some faculty and staff feel that certain local labour laws, “salary structures, or 
operational considerations are not always adequately factored into centralised decision-
making” (Global Position). 
Nonetheless, talent management has been formally introduced, addressing one of the 
weaknesses identified in the earlier assessment. For instance, since the HR-BSC 
implementation, employees have access to structured performance evaluations, which 
now occur on a regular basis, as noted by 90% of respondents: “Performance appraisals 
are now standardised, though they still need improvement” (Global Position). 
“Performance review, objective setting, regular 1-on-1s… benchmark is set and KPIs for 
all” (Director Position). Unlike in the past, when evaluations were either absent or 
conducted solely for accreditation purposes, they now serve as a tool for defining areas 
for improvement, as well as financial bonuses or career development options. However, 
inconsistency across campuses remains a concern: “Processes are still not consistent… 
some locations apply stricter evaluation criteria than others” (Faculty Member). 
Professional development was another area of progress. Some campuses began 
offering structured training opportunities which were previously inconsistent and largely 
unsupported: “There are now internal and external opportunities, including workshops on 
IT, accreditation, and management…” (Administrative Staff Member). “I received training 
on CIE, with financial and time support” (Director Position). In some cases, SIU has 
begun financially supporting professional growth, such as covering tuition fees for 
employees pursuing further education. This marks a considerable shift from the earlier 
situation, where professional development was sporadic and lacked institutional backing. 
 
 
197 
With respect to technology integration, the use of additional tools like Salesforce for 
recruitment and Asana for project management further signalled improved operational 
processes and efficiency: “Asana is used as a project management tool… SIS is coming, 
bringing more standardisation” (Global Position). However, technology integration still 
faces several challenges. One of the most substantial gaps is the delayed 
implementation of the Student Information System (SIS), which was expected to 
streamline student and academic management. Several respondents (90%) highlighted 
the continued reliance on manual data entry and Excel spreadsheets, which not only 
slows down administrative processes but also increases the risk of errors. As one 
administrator noted, “We’re still relying on spreadsheets for processes that should be 
automated by now, and that creates a lot of duplication and mistakes” (Administrative 
Staff Member). Moreover, while the university has made strides in adopting new 
platforms, there is a learning curve associated with their use, and some employees 
expressed the need for more training and better introduction to these tools to maximize 
their benefits.  
The data analysis indicates further that employee satisfaction and leadership 
development remain an area for progress. While some respondents noted that some 
surveys exist, follow-up actions on feedback remain unclear. As one staff member 
observed, “We fill out surveys, but we never really hear what happens after that” (Faculty 
Member). Additionally, while discussions about leadership evaluations have begun, they 
have not yet been fully implemented. Some employees expressed a desire for more 
structured leadership assessment and coaching programs. One staff member 
commented, “There’s potential in leadership development, but it feels like we’re just 
scratching the surface” (Global Position). Similarly, change management, another 
crucial area of HR, is still under development and requires further attention to ensure 
smoother transitions in organisational processes. Thus, cultural integration remains a 
work in progress. While many institutional processes have been standardised, some 
employees still feel that the shift toward centralization has created adjustment 
challenges, particularly for those who were accustomed to operating with more autonomy 
at the campus level. As one campus administrator noted, “Centralisation makes sense 
 
 
198 
strategically, but it’s been hard for some staff to adjust to losing decision-making power” 
(Global Position).  
Overall, the implementation of the HR-BSC at SIU has brought substantial changes to 
the organisational structure and culture, shifting the institution from a fragmented, 
campus-specific approach to a more centralised and standardised operational model. 
This transformation is strongly supported by evidence from the second round of 
interviews, where multiple participants described the move away from ad hoc, locally 
driven HR practices toward a more coordinated and strategically aligned HR function.  
“Before, each campus did their own thing. Now we are starting to think more globally. 
The HR policies and recruitment processes are no longer just campus-specific.” (Director 
Position). “There’s a stronger sense now that we’re working as one university, not four 
separate campuses. The new matrix structure is helping connect the dots.” (Global 
Position). These responses illustrate how the fragmented campus-based model has 
evolved into a more institutionally unified approach, supported by the HR-BSC. 
Respondents also described how cross-functional roles and decision-making lines are 
emerging, aligning with the principles of a matrix structure, for example: “Now, we have 
functional leads in admissions, academics, and HR who work across campuses. This 
didn’t exist before — campuses were very isolated.” (Global Position). “It’s a big change 
from the old model. I report to both my campus leadership and to a global director. It 
creates some complexity, but it also helps us align.” (Faculty Member). These remarks 
show how the matrix system is operational, allowing for dual-reporting lines and cross-
campus collaboration, which were largely absent under the former structure. Figure 21 
illustrates the transformation to a matrix organisational structure, replacing the previously 
isolated campus-based model with a more integrated and collaborative framework, as 
shared with SIU employees during a Townhall meeting introducing the BSC project: 
 
 
199 
 
Figure 21: SIU Matrix Structure Development (Source: SIU, Leadership Workshop 2023) 
Interviewees emphasized that the matrix model has enabled more consistent 
implementation of HR processes and institutional policies across campuses. “The 
performance evaluations and HR tools like Personio are now used across all campuses, 
not just piloted in one location. That’s new, and it’s helping us create consistency.” 
(Global Position). “In the past, on-boarding and hiring were completely different in each 
campus. Now there’s an attempt to standardize it, which is possible because of the new 
structure.” (Global Position). This is strong evidence that standardisation is a direct 
outcome of the HR-BSC implementation and the shift to a matrix-based governance 
model. To align with the new organisational structure, four key positions were introduced 
—  Chief Operating Officer (COO), Dean of Students (Global), Global Head Registrar, 
and Accreditation and Compliance Officer (Global). This restructuring has improved 
cross-campus coordination, making it easier for teams to share resources, standardize 
procedures, and align institutional goals across different locations. A key aspect of this 
structural shift is increased transparency and accountability in decision-making: “I’m 
seeing more dialogue between functions — HR, academics, finance. That didn’t happen 
before. The structure is changing the culture.” (Dean Position).  
 
 
200 
Eventually, the HR-BSC implementation seems to have substantially strengthened SIU’s 
organisational structure, influenced the use of technology and process improvement, 
particularly in streamlining HR functions and administrative workflows, supported data-
driven decision-making, defined financial structures and processes, and has led to 
greater reliance on performance tracking tools and institutional data analysis.  
 
5.6.2. 
Word Frequency and Sentiment Analysis in NVivo (Round 2) 
 
The sentiment analysis tool assesses the emotional tone of responses related to the HR-
BSC implementation. This analysis helped assess the overall acceptance of the 
changes, revealing a predominantly positive outlook in the post-implementation phase. 
Specifically, 60% of the statements reflected improvements in HR structure, 
transparency, employee engagement, and technological enhancements. Additionally, 
25% of the responses were neutral, indicating observations without strong emotional 
valence, while 15% conveyed negative sentiment, often related to concerns about 
ongoing challenges or areas needing further improvement. Compared to the Round 1 
interviews conducted before the HR-BSC implementation, the sentiment shift was 
substantial. In Round 1, 65% of responses were negative, 25% were neutral, and only 
10% were positive. This contrast highlights a notable improvement in stakeholder 
perception following the introduction of the HR-BSC framework. The findings are 
illustrated in Figure 22: 
 
 
201 
 
 
Figure 22:: SIU Leadership Presentation - NVivo Sentiment Analysis (Source: Author) 
The word frequency analysis also showed a more positive outcome in comparison to the 
Round 1 interview results. After the implementation of the HR-BSC the most often used 
words were “Personio”, “standardised”, “matrix”, “training”, and “performance”, showing 
improvements in HR processes, leadership assessment, and technology adoption. 
Figure 23 visualizes how the HR-BSC has shifted SIU’s HR priorities from 
disorganisation toward standardisation and structured growth:  
 
Figure 23: SIU Leadership Presentation - Word Cloud Round 1 and 2 (Source: Author) 
 
 
202 
The analysis of the HR-BSC implementation at SIU revealed both substantial 
improvements as well as areas requiring further attention. The transition from a 
fragmented HR system to a more structured and standardised framework has led to 
notable progress in recruitment, performance management, professional development, 
and organisational transparency. However, despite these advancements, challenges 
remain in ensuring consistency across campuses, fully integrating HR technology, and 
strengthening change management processes. 
The complete comparative analysis report can be found in Appendix N. 
 
5.6.3. 
Remaining Challenges  
 
While SIU has made clear progress through the implementation of the HR-BSC, the 
interview responses also revealed persistent or new challenges that were not addressed 
in Round 1. 
Despite the momentous progress achieved through the implementation of the HR-BSC at 
SIU, a closer examination of the interview responses from Round 2 highlights several 
persistent challenges that hinder the full realisation of its potential. While improvements 
in HR standardisation, communication, and performance management are evident, 
certain structural gaps, implementation inconsistencies, and employee adaptation issues 
remain.   
One of the most frequently mentioned challenges is inconsistent application of HR 
policies across SIU’s multiple campuses. While HR processes have been formalized and 
standardised, interviewees indicated that their implementation varies considerably 
depending on location. For example, some respondents reported that performance 
evaluation criteria are applied more rigorously in certain locations, while others have 
been slower to adopt the structured appraisal process: "The HR processes have 
definitely been formalized, but the rollout has been uneven. Some campuses are doing 
evaluations properly, others still haven’t fully adopted the new system." (Director 
 
 
203 
Position). One interviewee pointed out: "We have guidelines for on-boarding and 
recruitment, but I know that some campuses are still hiring without following them. 
There’s no follow-up yet to ensure compliance." (Dean Position). Inconsistency, as such, 
undermine the standardisation goals of the HR-BSC and create inequities in employee 
experience across different campuses. These variations suggest that further training is 
for supervisors and follow-up actions are necessary to ensure fairness and consistency.  
On the other hand, when defining HR-BSC KPIs at SIU, some campus-specific 
differences had to be considered. Financial indicators, for example, were influenced by 
local labour costs, legal obligations, and currency fluctuations. Salaries and training costs 
varied widely, e.g., programs in Paris were more expensive than in Tampa, requiring 
campus-level benchmarking for accurate comparison. Additionally, internal process KPIs 
were shaped by regional hiring and labour practices. Visa regulations, notice periods, 
and workweek standards differ (e.g., 35 hours in France vs. 40 in the U.S.), affecting 
recruitment timelines and workload planning. In the customer perspective, cultural norms 
influenced expectations around performance, grading, and work-life balance, e.g., lunch 
break times in Madrid differ from those in Germany. Finally, local accreditation 
requirements also shaped KPI definitions, as well as learning KPIs needed to reflect 
regional engagement levels and language availability, such as lower training participation 
in Madrid due to limited Spanish-language content. Adapting KPIs to these local 
conditions ensured fair and realistic performance measures across SIU’s campuses. In 
summary, the following campus-specific differences had to be considered based on local 
requirements:

Another important challenge is the limited focus on change management throughout the 
HR-BSC implementation process. While employees acknowledged improvements in HR 
structure and process clarity, some employees — especially faculty members — still 
struggle to adjust to the new system. Interviewees reported that faculty autonomy in 
decision-making has historically been high, and the introduction of standardised policies 
has led to some resistance: "Faculty are used to doing things their own way. Now that 
there are centralised rules, there’s some pushback. We didn’t really get proper support or 
training to adjust." (Global Position). Although leadership engagement has improved 
since the HR-BSC rollout, some respondents noted that there is still a need for clearer 
mechanisms to hold managers accountable for performance and decision-making 
effectiveness: "There has been progress, yes, but also confusion. A lot changed quickly, 
and we didn’t have much guidance or a roadmap for adapting." (Faculty Member). "We 
now have better tools for evaluating staff, but there’s no equivalent system for evaluating 
leaders. It feels like managers are still unaccountable." (Administrative Staff Member). 
These remarks indicate that change management was not embedded as a core part of 
 
 
205 
HR-BSC implementation, leading to resistance and uncertainty. More support is needed 
to help employees adapt to the new organisational structure and expectations. 
With respect to technology advancements, employees reported that although the 
implementation of Personio, Salesforce, and Asana has improved operational efficiency, 
some still struggle with adapting to these digital HR tools. A major reason for this 
challenge is the lack of continuous technology training. While initial system rollouts 
included basic training, many employees reported that follow-up support was insufficient:  
"Asana has helped a lot, but some people still don’t know how to use it properly. After the 
first training, there hasn’t been much follow-up." (Administrative Staff Member). "We were 
given new tools, but not enough time or support to really learn them. It’s frustrating 
because it slows things down." (Faculty Member). 
Additionally, the delayed implementation of the Student Information System (SIS) means 
that some administrative tasks are still being handled manually, creating a disconnect 
between new HR technology and older processes. "Until the SIS is fully implemented, 
we’re stuck in a hybrid system — some things digital, some things still on spreadsheets." 
(Administrative Staff Member). To fully realize the benefits of these technological 
improvements, SIU must provide adequate training, and accelerate the implementation of 
key missing systems such as the SIS. 
While SIU’s HR-BSC implementation has successfully introduced structured HR policies, 
standardised processes, and improved HR technology adoption, several ongoing 
challenges need to be addressed for long-term success. The most pressing issues 
include inconsistent policy enforcement across campuses, resistance to change, delays 
in leadership evaluation implementation, and gaps in HR technology adoption. By 
proactively addressing these challenges, SIU could build on the success of its HR-BSC 
implementation and create a more engaged and high-performing workforce that aligns 
with the university’s long-term strategic objectives. 
 
 
 
 
206 
Chapter 6 
 
6. Discussion and Conclusions 
 
This chapter reflects on the key findings of the research, linking them to the literature and 
conceptual framework discussed in earlier chapters. It discusses the broader implications 
of implementing the HR-Balanced Scorecard (HR-BSC) in higher education, with a 
particular focus on Schiller International University (SIU). To begin, this section gives an 
overview of why the BSC was used, the challenges it addresses, and how it fits into the 
current trends shaping the higher education sector. Higher education is going through 
major changes, which makes it more important than ever to have structured systems for 
performance management and measurement. Kaplan and Norton’s Balanced Scorecard 
offers a helpful framework for tracking strategic goals and identifying areas that need 
improvement. It allows organisations to connect their long-term strategy to everyday 
actions by linking objectives, key performance indicators (KPIs), and progress measures.  
Previous research (e.g., Armstrong, 2006; Parameswaran & Pisharodi, 1994) has 
explored how strategic HR management, performance management, and the BSC are 
connected. Armstrong, for example, highlights the importance of aligning HR with the 
wider goals of an organisation, while Parameswaran and Pisharodi suggest that the BSC 
can help with strategic alignment. However, most of this research focuses on business or 
general settings, and there is still very little work looking at how the BSC can be used 
specifically as an HR performance management tool in universities. 
To help fill this gap, this research project looked at the implementation of an HR-BSC at 
SIU. It was hoped that this framework would provide structure to how HR performance 
was measured and reported to key stakeholders, track progress on strategic goals, and 
support decisions based on data. It was found that the balanced approach gave a 
broader view of HR work at SIU and showed that the BSC can support leadership in 
improving overall performance. This was also supported by the results from the second 
 
 
207 
round of interviews, which showed that staff noticed better visibility of HR metrics in 
leadership meetings, stronger connections between professional development and 
strategic goals, and greater transparency and accountability in HR processes. 
These findings lead to the conclusion that at SIU, establishing a formal HR department 
would offer an opportunity to continue building on these improvements. This department 
could keep using the HR-BSC to guide its planning, identify key areas for development, 
and respond to future challenges. These challenges include the ongoing effects of the 
COVID-19 pandemic, the rise of new technologies like generative AI, and geopolitical 
changes that may affect international education. Together, these trends make it essential 
for universities like SIU to build flexible and responsive HR systems that can support both 
academic and administrative needs in a fast-changing environment. 
The rest of this chapter will examine the findings more closely, discuss how they relate to 
the literature and the conceptual framework, and consider what they mean for practice. It 
will also reflect on the research’s limitations and contributions, and offer final conclusions 
and recommendations. 
 
6.1. 
Evaluation of Research Findings 
 
The research findings highlight the crucial role of structured HR management in 
enhancing institutional efficiency and employee performance. In the case of SIU, the 
implementation of the HR-BSC offered a structured framework that helped align HR 
activities with strategic goals. The process not only stimulated improved reporting and 
performance monitoring but also supported a broader cultural shift toward transparency 
and strategic alignment.  
The HR-BSC's most noteworthy contributions were seen in the areas of HR structure, 
clarity of roles and responsibilities, and the standardisation of processes. Several 
interviewees in the second round reported that the structured approach of the BSC 
encouraged greater transparency, and helped shift HR from an operational support 
 
 
208 
function to a more strategic role. HR metrics were now more visible in leadership 
discussions, creating a stronger sense of accountability. These positive effects seem 
closely tied to the BSC's multidimensional framework, which allowed SIU to move 
beyond ad hoc HR activities and start integrating them with broader university objectives. 
In this sense, the BSC functioned as more than just a measurement tool — it became a 
catalyst for broader organisational reflection. 
However, the findings also reveal that progress was uneven across departments and 
campuses. Inconsistencies in how HR practices were applied, as well as weak follow-up 
in change management initiatives and communication, limited the BSC’s full potential. 
For example, while the standardisation of HR processes was initiated, not all campuses 
implemented these changes uniformly. Staff in some areas expressed uncertainty about 
how performance data would be used, suggesting that the culture of performance 
accountability is still developing. These inconsistencies may be partially explained by 
differences in local campus cultures, differing levels of managerial capability, or varying 
degrees of buy-in from staff.  While the framework itself provided clarity and structure, its 
success appeared to depend heavily on how it was introduced and managed locally. 
Limited change management efforts and a lack of consistent follow-up likely contributed 
to weaker outcomes in some areas. This highlights the point that the BSC alone does not 
drive change — rather, it provides a structure through which change can be guided, 
monitored, and sustained. 
Another consideration is whether the observed changes were driven by the HR-BSC 
itself or partly by increased awareness of existing HR issues. A Hawthorne effect may 
have played a role, with some changes emerging simply because these areas were 
under active discussion. However, the HR-BSC helped channel that awareness into 
concrete, strategic action. Overall, the evaluation shows that the HR-BSC has 
contributed meaningfully to SIU’s strategic development by improving performance 
transparency and creating a more structured approach to HR management. Its long-term 
impact will depend on consistent leadership support, staff engagement, and integration 
into daily practice. With ongoing refinement, the HR-BSC can continue to support long-
term improvements in HR effectiveness and institutional performance. 
 
 
209 
Although this research is based on a single institution, SIU’s international, multi-campus 
structure offers a form of a built-in comparative case study, capturing variations in 
regulatory environments, labour markets, and organisational cultures. This diversity 
provides a unique testing ground for the HR-BSC framework, demonstrating how it can 
be adapted and applied across different contexts within the same institution. As such, the 
study’s findings are not only relevant to SIU but also offer broader lessons for other 
globally oriented higher education institutions. The research highlights that, when tailored 
appropriately, the BSC can promote strategic alignment and enhance organisational 
maturity across complex, decentralised systems. This comparative dimension 
strengthens the generalisability and practical value of the findings, which will be further 
discussed in the thesis limitations section. 
 
6.1.1. 
Workforce Trends and Implications for HR-BSC 
 
This evolving landscape underscores the critical role of university employees, not only in 
fulfilling core academic and administrative functions but also in shaping institutional 
performance. As confirmed in this research study, the implementation of the HR-BSC at 
SIU brought greater awareness of the strategic value of human resources, highlighting 
the need for clear accountability, aligned development initiatives, and improved 
performance monitoring. As one senior administrator noted, “HR was no longer just about 
operations — it became part of the strategic conversation” (Global Position). Building on 
this insight, the following sections explore critical aspects that influence HR strategy and 
the application of the HR-BSC in higher education.  
 
6.1.1.1. 
HR Investment and the Cost of Academic Staffing 
 
A substantial proportion of university budgets is dedicated to salaries. As illustrated in 
Chapter 1.2., U.S. universities have consistently allocated a large share of their 
 
 
210 
resources to staffing costs over the past two decades. This financial commitment 
underscores the value of having structured, data-driven HR strategies in place, such as 
the HR-BSC, that help ensure institutions gain optimal value from their workforce. 
However, this emphasis on workforce value must be considered alongside broader shifts 
in the higher education labour market. Recent reports, including those from the American 
Association of University Professors (AAUP, 2023; Champlin & Knoedler, 2024), suggest 
that higher education may be moving beyond its “peak salary” era due to technological 
change and new working models. This trend is reflected in the growing use of automation 
and digital platforms, which in some cases reduce demand for traditional academic roles. 
As a result, institutions increasingly rely on more flexible and cost-effective labour 
models, such as part-time teaching staff and non-tenure track positions. 
Data from the National Center for Education Statistics (NCES, 2024) supports this 
observation, showing a notable decline in full-time tenured faculty positions over the past 
three years. While this shift may offer short-term budgetary relief, it also raises important 
strategic questions — many of which align with the issues identified in this study. For 
example, the HR-BSC at SIU highlighted the need for greater attention to workforce 
planning, employee development, and long-term performance management. A growing 
reliance on contingent staff may challenge these objectives, potentially undermining 
institutional reputation, research capacity, and the consistency of the student learning 
experience. 
As universities revisit their staffing models, leadership must balance financial 
sustainability with academic quality and long-term strategy. This research’s findings 
suggest that tools like the HR-BSC can support this by aligning HR practices with 
institutional goals and enabling more strategic, data-driven workforce planning.  
 
 
 
 
 
211 
6.1.1.2. 
AI and the Future of Human Resources in Higher Education 
 
Artificial intelligence (AI) is increasingly reshaping HRM across sectors, including higher 
education. From automating recruitment and administrative processes to enabling 
predictive analytics in workforce planning, AI offers some efficiency gains. However, its 
integration into university HR systems also raises questions about data governance, 
employee acceptance, and the evolving role of HR professionals.  
This shift was also reflected in the SIU case. One result of the HR-BSC implementation 
was the identification and standardisation of key HR processes — areas previously 
handled differently across campuses. While intended to improve operational consistency, 
these developments also laid a foundation for potential automation. Several 
administrative tasks, once manual, could now be further streamlined or eventually 
supported by AI systems. 
The HR-BSC’s structured approach enabled SIU to identify critical issues, such as 
inconsistent role definitions and feedback mechanisms, that are especially relevant in the 
context of emerging technologies. Future iterations of the HR-BSC could incorporate 
indicators related to AI readiness, digital tool adoption, and staff adaptability. By tracking 
shifts in job roles and required competencies, the framework can help institutions 
anticipate and manage the impact of automation. 
More broadly, tools such as intelligent tutoring systems, adaptive learning platforms, and 
AI-powered ‘chatbots’ are already reducing the need for human labour in areas like 
admissions, scheduling, and basic student support. However, as the findings from SIU 
suggest — and consistent with recent literature — human interaction remains essential, 
particularly in teaching, mentoring, and decision-making roles (Bearman et al., 2023; 
Crompton & Burke, 2023; Michel-Villarreal & Vitalta-Perdomo, 2023). Respondents 
highlighted the importance of interpersonal engagement and academic judgment, 
reinforcing the idea that AI is more likely to complement than replace human roles.  
 
 
212 
As institutions adopt these technologies, HR strategies must address evolving job 
profiles, training needs, and staff engagement. At SIU, the HR-BSC helped establish a 
clearer link between HR practices and institutional goals, which could support future 
digital transformation by enabling data-informed planning and monitoring. The 
framework’s value lies not only in what it measures today, but in how it can evolve to help 
institutions manage change. At SIU, it contributed to institutional alignment and improved 
HR structures while helping build capacity for navigating broader transformations, 
including those driven by AI. 
 
6.1.2. 
Relevance and Suitability of HR-BSC for a University 
 
As previously discussed, universities face growing challenges, including intensified 
competition for limited resources, deregulation, and emerging educational trends. These 
factors necessitate the use of management tools that enable continuous performance 
evaluation, adaptability, and swift responsiveness to changing conditions. 
One of the advantages often associated with the BSC is its multidimensional structure, 
incorporating both financial and non-financial KPIs. This design is considered more 
adaptable than traditional tools that often rely on financial metrics only (Kaplan & Norton, 
1996). At SIU, the ability to define indicators across strategic themes, such as employee 
engagement, teaching effectiveness, and process efficiency, allowed the BSC to be 
aligned with institutional goals without requiring major structural changes. While the 
framework did not eliminate the need for adjustments, its design supported integration 
into the academic environment, which typically values qualitative outcomes alongside 
quantitative ones. That said, the extent to which this flexibility reduces implementation 
disruption is difficult to quantify. Although non-financial KPIs can align well with academic 
values, they may lack the standardisation and comparability of financial indicators. At 
SIU, several HR processes had to be formalised and clarified before performance 
indicators could be meaningfully applied. This suggests that while the BSC framework 
 
 
213 
allows for adaptation, its successful implementation still depends on institutional 
readiness, leadership support, and the availability of relevant performance data, like KPI.  
Increasingly, universities are shifting from input-based assessments, such as student 
enrolment numbers or faculty-student ratios, to outcome-driven models that emphasize 
measurable results. This shift reflects a broader trend in higher education towards 
accountability, efficiency, and impact. KPIs are now commonly used to track outcomes 
such as graduation rates, student employability, research impact, as well as student 
satisfaction. These metrics help institutions demonstrate value to stakeholders, including 
government bodies, accreditation agencies, and prospective students. As a result, 
performance-based funding mechanisms are becoming more prevalent, linking financial 
support to demonstrable achievements rather than institutional capacity alone. Moreover, 
strategic decision-making is increasingly data-informed, with university leaders relying on 
KPI dashboards to allocate resources, assess program effectiveness, and support long-
term planning. As seen above, this outcome-oriented approach aligns well with 
performance management frameworks like the BSC. 
At SIU, the HR-BSC implementation helped bring inconsistencies in HR practices across 
campuses to the surface and prompted clearer performance tracking and role definitions. 
As one campus leader observed, the framework “forced us to think about HR in a more 
structured and strategic way” (Director Position), reflecting a shift from reactive to 
proactive HR planning.  
While previous literature has suggested that the BSC is adaptable to academic contexts, 
this research shows that successful implementation depends on more than the tool itself. 
The case revealed challenges around fragmented goals between academic and 
administrative units, as well as uneven buy-in across departments. For example, one 
manager noted that the model “added clarity” (Administrative Staff Member), while 
another commented that it was “still too top-down to fully reflect academic priorities” 
(Faculty Member). These insights highlight the importance of leadership support, 
communication, and ongoing engagement in embedding performance-driven HR models. 
 
 
214 
The analysis supports the BSC’s potential to enhance strategic clarity, resource 
allocation, and improvement efforts, while highlighting its strong dependence on 
institutional context. In light of trends like digitalization, cost pressures, and global 
competition, the SIU case suggests the HR-BSC could help other HEIs address common 
challenges, such as recruitment, performance measurement, and process 
standardisation, by supporting more strategic HR management. 
 
6.1.3. 
Methodological Reflection 
 
This research explored the potential for performance improvements at SIU through the 
implementation of an HR-BSC as a strategic human resource management tool. To 
guide this investigation, the research aimed to determine whether the BSC is an effective 
framework for enhancing HR performance at SIU. The central research question driving 
this analysis was: “Is the Balanced Scorecard in Human Resource Management an 
appropriate tool to measure, manage and improve performance at SIU?”  
An Action Research framework was used in this current research, reflecting the 
principles of pragmatism. While this approach was appropriate for addressing the 
research question, some methodological limitations emerged in the process. 
Action Research was an appropriate approach for this study, as it allowed for active 
engagement with institutional change at SIU and enabled the collaborative development 
and implementation of a tailored HR-BSC. However, the dual role as both participant and 
observer raises valid concerns regarding potential bias and subjectivity. Given the 
researcher’s position as an SIU employee with direct experience of the institution’s HR-
related challenges, maintaining objectivity was particularly important. To mitigate the risk 
of bias, a reflexive journal was kept throughout the research process (as outlined in sub-
chapter 4.5), allowing for critical self-reflection and transparency in decision-making. For 
example, a tendency to prioritise strategic-level observations was corrected by 
deliberately revisiting frontline staff perspectives, following a journal reflection on the 
 
 
215 
disproportionate weight being given to leadership narratives. In addition, interview 
questions were standardised to ensure consistency across participants. These questions 
were developed based on insights from the initial research phase and were designed to 
avoid leading language, thereby reducing the influence of personal assumptions on data 
collection. Despite these efforts, ensuring complete objectivity, especially during the 
interpretation of interview data, remained a challenge. This necessitated regular pauses 
in the analysis process to reflect on potential bias and reassess the lens through which 
emerging themes were being interpreted. 
To reflect SIU’s multi-campus structure and organisational complexity, a multiple 
embedded case study design was adopted. This approach made it possible to explore 
variation across different units and stakeholder groups. While the research focused on 
one organisation, SIU’s international footprint, diverse regulatory environments, and 
varied campus cultures offered a degree of internal comparison often found in multi-
institutional studies. As such, the findings may be relevant beyond SIU, particularly for 
other higher education institutions operating across multiple sites or within globally 
distributed contexts. 
Data collection primarily involved semi-structured interviews with key stakeholders, 
including faculty, campus directors, and administrative managers. While this qualitative 
approach was effective in capturing diverse perspectives and identifying recurring 
themes, mostly relevant to organisational change, certain limitations emerged. For 
example, interviewee bias may have influenced responses, particularly among 
participants directly involved in the HR-BSC implementation. In addition, perspectives 
varied notably depending on role and seniority, complicating efforts to draw uniform 
conclusions. 
NVivo software was used to code and analyse qualitative data, supporting a structured 
thematic approach. This helped enhance the reliability of pattern identification across 
interviews. Still, the reliance on researcher-led coding introduces a degree of 
interpretation which, while managed through iterative reviews, still carries the risk of 
confirmation bias. 
 
 
216 
Although the research design followed a mixed methods approach, the integration of 
quantitative data was limited due to the unavailability of relevant HR performance metrics 
at SIU. Several planned performance indicators linked to the HR-BSC, e.g. satisfaction 
scores, were not collected systematically by the institution prior to or during the study 
period. This lack of data restricted the ability to triangulate qualitative findings with 
measurable outcomes and limited the empirical assessment of the HR-BSC’s impact. 
Importantly, this gap reflects a broader issue in institutions that stand to benefit most 
from structured performance frameworks: the absence of consistent, reliable HR data 
collection practices. The experience at SIU illustrates that without baseline data and 
ongoing measurement systems, even well-designed frameworks like the HR-BSC face 
challenges in demonstrating their full value. This represents a key area for future 
research and institutional development, underscoring the need to establish robust data 
infrastructures as a foundation for performance management in higher education.   
In sum, while the chosen methodology was appropriate for the exploratory and practice-
oriented nature of the research, several limitations affected the scope and interpretive 
strength of the study. These include the constrained generalisability of a single case 
study, role-related bias in data collection, and the limited integration of quantitative 
measures. Future research may benefit from a more fully developed mixed methods 
approach and broader institutional sampling to strengthen both validity and applicability. 
 
6.2. 
Limitations  
 
While the analysis of HR-BSC implementation at SIU provided valuable insights into the 
transformation of HR processes, certain limitations must be acknowledged. Despite 
efforts to ensure the validity and reliability of the findings, it is important to recognize the 
constraints and challenges encountered during the research process. These limitations 
affect the depth, and scope of the findings, address the generalisability further, and 
highlight areas where additional research is needed. 
 
 
217 
Since this research was limited to a single institution, the findings may not be directly 
transferable to other universities with different structures, cultures, or regulatory 
environments. SIU’s unique characteristics — its international scope, multi-campus 
structure, and privately owned status — create a specific context that may not align with 
traditional public universities or single-campus institutions.  
However, despite structural and cultural differences across higher education institutions, 
several of the challenges addressed in this research are common, particularly as 
universities respond to globalisation, decentralisation, and growing demands for strategic 
alignment in HRM.  
SIU’s organisational model, characterised by decentralised operations across multiple 
campuses, mirrors structural complexities seen in an increasing number of higher 
education institutions with international branches or cross-border partnerships. These 
configurations often face similar difficulties in coordinating HR practices, aligning staff 
development with institutional goals, and standardising performance expectations — 
issues central to this case study. The implementation of the HR-BSC at SIU offers 
practical insights for other institutions managing comparable structural and strategic 
challenges. While SIU’s private governance model may differ from traditional public 
universities, the trend towards market-driven management in higher education has 
blurred these distinctions. As noted by Deem (2009), many public institutions are 
adopting managerial models that resemble corporate frameworks, placing new emphasis 
on performance metrics, resource efficiency, and strategic planning. In this context, the 
structured approach of the HR-BSC aligns with sector-wide shifts toward data-informed, 
accountable HR practices. 
Nonetheless, the degree of generalisability is constrained by key contextual factors. For 
example, universities with strong faculty governance or union influence may experience 
more resistance to performance frameworks, requiring broader consultation and 
adaptation. SIU’s relatively agile governance structure may have allowed for a smoother 
implementation process. Furthermore, national labour laws, accreditation standards, and 
 
 
218 
cultural norms influence how HRM reforms are received and applied across different 
higher education systems. 
Despite these limitations, several findings appear transferable. These include the value 
of leadership support, the need for clear performance indicators, and the benefits of 
standardising internal HR processes prior to implementation. While rooted in a single 
case, this research contributes to a broader understanding of how structured, strategy-
oriented HR tools like the HR-BSC can be adapted within higher education. It offers 
useful insights for institutions pursuing HR reform in similarly complex environments, 
while also underlining the importance of context-specific adaptation when applying 
performance frameworks across the sector. 
Furthermore, the short implementation and evaluation period of the HR-BSC presents 
another constraint. Given that the structured HR framework was introduced while the 
author was employed at the institution and assessed before her departure the case study 
primarily captures the initial phase of implementation, focusing on early successes and 
challenges. Thus, long-term effects, such as the sustainability of new HR policies and 
processes, acceptance and overall improvements, could not be assessed within the 
scope of this research.  
Likewise, the relatively small sample size may not fully capture the range of perspectives 
present within the broader institutional context. Given that several interview participants 
held senior leadership positions, there is a risk of sampling bias, with findings potentially 
reflecting strategic and managerial viewpoints more strongly than those of frontline staff. 
For instance, while campus directors and senior administrators often highlighted the 
strategic advantages of the HR-BSC (e.g., “It helped connect HR efforts to institutional 
priorities” – Campus Director, Paris), fewer insights were gathered regarding how the 
framework was experienced by the broader team, e.g. adjunct faculty. This imbalance 
may have limited the diversity of perspectives represented and constrained the depth of 
understanding across different organisational levels. As a result, the overall richness and 
transferability of the qualitative findings may be affected. Nevertheless, the selection of 
participants was based on SIU’s specific institutional context and focused on those 
 
 
219 
individuals most directly engaged in the HR-BSC implementation, as identified through 
initial baseline research. 
In addition, the small sample size raised ethical considerations, particularly regarding 
confidentiality and anonymity. In the context of a single institution, participants could 
potentially be identified based on their role, campus location, or the specificity of their 
comments — even when names are omitted. This increased risk of unintended 
disclosure may have influenced the openness with which some participants shared their 
views. The need to safeguard identities also limited the level of detail that could be 
included in the reporting of results, especially in the use of direct quotations. These 
constraints may have impacted both the data collection process and the depth of 
interpretation. Nonetheless, all quoted participants provided informed, written consent for 
their contributions to be included in this thesis.  
While these limitations impact the scope and interpretation of the findings, they also 
present opportunities for future research. Longitudinal studies, larger sample sizes, and 
comparative analyses with other institutions could further refine and validate the 
application of the HR-BSC in higher education. Nevertheless, the research provides 
valuable qualitative insights into how SIU’s HR-BSC implementation has influenced HR 
practices. The findings confirm that HR standardisation and technology integration can 
drive efficiency and transparency, but successful transformation requires consistent 
policy enforcement, strong communication strategies, and ongoing employee 
engagement efforts. Despite these limitations, this research provides valuable qualitative 
insights into how SIU’s HR-BSC implementation has influenced HR practices. 
Future Research 
While this study has offered valuable insights into the implementation of the HR-
Balanced Scorecard (HR-BSC) at Schiller International University (SIU), several areas 
merit further investigation. These include both addressing methodological limitations and 
expanding the theoretical and practical understanding of HR performance management 
in higher education. 
 
 
220 
First, future research could examine the long-term effectiveness of the HR-BSC. While 
this project focused on the initial design, introduction, and early effects of the framework, 
as part of an Action Research approach, a longitudinal perspective would offer valuable 
insights into whether early improvements in HR structure, staff engagement, and 
strategic alignment are sustained over time. Such research could help show when the 
HR-BSC leads to lasting improvements and whether it works best when there is strong 
leadership, ongoing learning, and active change management. 
Second, expanding the timeframe and participant pool would enhance the depth of future 
studies. While this research focused on those directly involved in the HR-BSC rollout, 
future work could include voices from academic staff not in leadership roles, more 
students, and external stakeholders. This broader perspective would offer a more 
comprehensive view of how HR initiatives are perceived across institutional levels. 
Incorporating quantitative performance data, such as staff turnover, training participation, 
or satisfaction metrics, would also strengthen future research. Such indicators would 
enable a more data-driven evaluation of the HR-BSC’s impact and support the 
triangulation of qualitative insights with measurable outcomes. 
Comparative studies across institutions with varying structures, governance models, and 
cultural contexts would also be valuable. Investigating whether the success of the HR-
BSC is due to the framework itself or to context-specific enablers, such as leadership 
commitment or organisational readiness, would contribute to both theoretical 
development and practical guidance. These comparisons would also help determine how 
adaptable the framework is to other institutional environments. 
This study also highlights a broader challenge — the lack of reliable HR data in 
institutions that could benefit most from structured performance frameworks. At SIU, the 
absence of historical quantitative metrics limited the scope for evaluating impact. 
Addressing this gap should be a priority for both future research and institutional 
development, as robust data systems are essential for meaningful HR performance 
management. 
 
 
221 
Lastly, as higher education continues to evolve in response to trends such as 
digitalisation, global competition, and increasing accountability, further research is 
needed to explore how frameworks like the HR-BSC can support strategic, responsive 
HR planning. In particular, future studies should examine how the HR-BSC can integrate 
emerging themes such as AI adoption, ensuring institutions are equipped to manage 
technological change and workforce transformation effectively. Continued investigation in 
this area can strengthen both theory and practice, helping institutions align HR functions 
with long-term goals in an increasingly complex sector. 
 
6.3. 
Conclusions  
 
The implementation of the HR-BSC at SIU was driven by the need to address 
fragmented HR processes, inconsistent recruitment and performance evaluations, and a 
lack of structured employee engagement strategies. As seen in chapter 5.6.1., its 
application has led to clear improvements, including the formalization of HR processes, 
the adoption of HR technology (Personio) for payroll and recruitment, standardised 
performance appraisals, and expanded professional development opportunities. The 
transition to a matrix organisational structure has enhanced communication and 
collaboration across campuses. However, challenges persist, including inconsistencies in 
HR policy enforcement, gaps in communication, and difficulties in translating strategic 
decisions into actionable steps. Additionally, while HR technology adoption is 
progressing, full implementation, such as the Student Information System (SIS), remains 
pending, and further staff training on digital tools is necessary. A structured change 
management framework is still lacking, and leadership evaluation processes have yet to 
be fully established.  
The findings of this research highlight the importance of structured HRM frameworks in 
higher education. SIU’s shift from a fragmented HR system to a more structured, 
technology-supported model suggests that standardisation, digital integration, and 
 
 
222 
process optimisation may support improvements in institutional efficiency, employee 
engagement, and performance — though the long-term impact remains to be fully 
assessed. To ensure long-term success, SIU should focus on strengthening HR training 
and accountability to enforce policies consistently and maximize the use of HR 
technology across campuses. Enhancing change management strategies is also crucial, 
particularly in supporting faculty and staff through digital transformation, evolving faculty 
roles, and emerging skill demands such as proficiency in the use of AI. Additionally, 
accelerating the integration of HR technology will help standardize performance tracking, 
recruitment, and employee development across all locations. Leadership commitment will 
be essential in sustaining these efforts, ensuring that faculty and staff not only adapt to 
institutional changes but actively contribute to their success. Moreover, systematically 
tracking HR performance metrics will also be key in refining the HR-BSC, allowing it to 
evolve and drive continuous improvement. 
SIU’s case exemplifies the broader challenges and opportunities associated with HR 
transformation in higher education. As universities continue to navigate financial 
constraints, evolving academic roles, and digital disruption, the need for structured and 
strategic HR frameworks has never been more critical.  
This research highlights the HR-BSC as a promising tool for academic institutions 
seeking to improve HR efficiency, align HR practices with strategic goals, and foster 
long-term sustainability. It contributes to both theory and practice. Theoretically, it 
reinforces strategic HRM and organisational change theories by showing that structured 
HR models can enhance efficiency, but require comprehensive change management. It 
also expands research on HR technology adoption, emphasizing that success depends 
not just on system functionality but also on employee training, policy enforcement, and 
stakeholder engagement. 
From a practical perspective, this research provides a case study for HR professionals 
and higher education institutions, illustrating the benefits and challenges of implementing 
a standardised HR framework. While HR process standardisation improves efficiency, 
continuous monitoring is necessary to ensure uniform application across campuses. 
 
 
223 
Digital HR tools enhance administrative functions, but their effectiveness relies on proper 
adoption and staff training. Additionally, communication strategies must extend beyond 
structured channels to include localized engagement. Without a structured change 
management approach, resistance and inconsistencies in implementation are likely to 
persist. 
Importantly, the research raises timely questions about the changing nature of academic 
employment. The growing reliance on non-permanent teaching staff, the digitalisation of 
(HR) operations, and shifting workforce expectations underline the urgency for 
universities to rethink their HR strategies. Ensuring fair and transparent performance 
evaluation, strengthening leadership development, and broadening engagement 
initiatives will be key to refining the HR-BSC model. 
Ultimately, the case of SIU demonstrates that with the right tools, leadership 
commitment, and a shared vision for change, HR transformation is not only possible, but 
can also serve as a catalyst for broader institutional growth. As higher education evolves, 
strategic HRM will play an increasingly central role in shaping sustainable, forward-
looking universities. 
 
Final Reflections 
HR performance evaluation remains inherently complex, as most HR-related KPIs are 
non-financial and difficult to quantify. Unlike traditional financial metrics, HR indicators, 
such as employee satisfaction, organisational culture, and talent retention, require 
nuanced measurement approaches. This challenge underscores why some 
organisations fail to prioritize HR development, leading to inefficiencies in hiring, training, 
and retention efforts. However, as demonstrated in this research, structured HR 
performance frameworks like the HR-BSC offer a pathway to overcoming these 
challenges by integrating qualitative and quantitative measures into a cohesive system. 
From my perspective as both a researcher and practitioner, this project has been far 
more than an academic exercise — it has been a personal and professional journey of 
 
 
224 
reflection and growth. Experiencing the practical challenges of HR management first-
hand at SIU, I became increasingly aware of how easily strategic intent can be lost 
without clear structures and accountability mechanisms. I found that the process of 
developing and testing the HR-BSC not only deepened my understanding of strategic 
HRM but also reshaped how I view leadership, organisational alignment, and change 
management within higher education. At times, navigating between my role as a leader 
and as a researcher was both challenging and enlightening. It required me to constantly 
balance analytical objectivity with empathy, critical evaluation with organisational loyalty, 
and academic rigour with real-world constraints. 
Through this process, I came to appreciate how strategic HRM, when thoughtfully 
structured, can become a genuine catalyst for institutional transformation. I learned that 
numbers alone rarely capture the full story of performance; rather, it is the combination of 
data, dialogue, and shared purpose that drives sustainable change. Personally, this 
experience strengthened my belief in evidence-based leadership and reinforced my 
commitment to developing HR systems that are not only efficient but also humane, 
inclusive, and empowering. 
In conclusion, this doctoral research has not only contributed to the academic 
understanding of strategic HRM in Higher Education but has also shaped my 
professional philosophy as a reflective, evidence-driven leader. The process reinforced 
the importance of aligning people, purpose and performance in achieving sustainable 
organisational growth.  
This research serves as a call to action for SIU and other institutions to continuously 
assess and refine their HR frameworks for long-term sustainability. The HR-BSC is not a 
one-time solution but an ongoing process requiring adaptability, continuous learning, and 
commitment. By fostering a data-driven, employee-centred approach, SIU can further 
enhance its HR operations and institutional success while serving as a model for other 
universities navigating similar transformations. 
 
 
 
225

Appendix D 
Interview Guide 
The interview guide entails:  
Before the interview (preparation phase): 
• Test recording equipment in advance to ensure quality and avoid technical issues. 
• Have a printed or digital copy of the interview questions or guide for easy reference. 
• Prepare a quiet, neutral setting (especially for online interviews) to minimise distractions or 
external influence. 
 
At the beginning of the interview:  
• provide personal information (status, purpose of the interview (DBA thesis), explanation of who 
is being interviewed and with what purpose)  
• confirm that the interviewee received and understood the information sheet, including the 
privacy policy, offering to clarify anything.  
• ask in advance for permission to record the interview  
• remind the participant that participation is voluntary and they can withdraw at any time without 
consequences. 
• briefly explain that all data will be anonymized at a later point in time and that no  
conclusions can be drawn about the interviewed person . 
•  Ask if the participant has any questions before starting. 
 
During the interview: 
• no ambiguous or misleading questions  
• no judgmental or aggressive-sounding questions 
• allow for silence or pauses to give the interviewee time to think—don’t rush to fill gaps 
• use a simple choice of words, or adapt to the vocabulary or sociolinguistic level of the 
interviewee 
• maintain a neutral and supportive tone throughout—even when sensitive or critical feedback is 
given  
• taboo topics should be address carefully and rather at the end of an interview 
• take brief notes (if appropriate) to mark interesting or unclear points for later follow-up 
 
After the interview: 
• Thank the participant for their time and contribution 
• 
Clarify next steps and offer to share the executive summary with the participant.

Appendix H 
Reflexive Research Journal 
 
Throughout this research, I maintained a reflexive journal to document my thoughts, biases, and 
interpretations. This journal helped me recognize and mitigate potential researcher bias, ensuring 
a more balanced approach to data collection and analysis. Below are selected excerpts from my 
journal, organized chronologically and thematically. 
Extract of Journal Entries  
a) Chronological Format (By Date): how reflections evolved over time. 
Date 
Reflections / Notes 
10 Nov 
2021 
Before interviews, I realize I expect faculty to express dissatisfaction with HR processes. I need 
to remain open to all responses. 
11 Dec 
2022 
Noticing patterns around lack of performance management. Many participants use similar 
language ("no system," "reactive," "ad hoc"). Emerging theme is clear. 
28 Jan 
2022 
Responses from different campuses feel fragmented. Might need to code by location to capture 
structural differences in implementation. 
19 Feb 
2022 
Starting to see the potential of HR-BSC as a framework, especially as participants highlight gaps 
in strategy and alignment. 
10 Mar 
2022 
Coding complete for Round 1. Surprised by how often communication breakdowns were 
mentioned. Theme of "information gaps" is stronger than initially expected. 
3 Nov 
2022 
Preparing Round 2 protocol. Realize I’m expecting improvement in perceptions due to HR-BSC 
rollout. Need to remain neutral and allow for nuanced responses. 
15 Dec 
2022 
First Round 2 interviews suggest improvement in technology adoption, but also reveal new 
challenges in training. An unexpected complexity is emerging. 
5 Feb 
2023 
Noticed that new global team members provide very different perspectives—often more 
optimistic, focused on system potential rather than past frustrations. 
22 Jan 
2023 
Several participants highlighted workload concerns, but reasons varied. I need to avoid 
overgeneralizing themes. 
 
 
 
355 
 
b) Thematic Format (Example Journal Entries, Grouped by Key Areas):   
to categorize reflections additionally by topic. 
 
1. Pre-Interview Reflections 
"I anticipated faculty dissatisfaction with HR policies. To prevent bias, I revised my questions to 
remain neutral." 
"Before conducting interviews, I realize I assume that faculty members will be frustrated with HR 
processes due to inefficiencies. I must be mindful not to lead interview questions in a way that 
confirms this assumption. Instead, I will ensure my questions remain neutral and open-ended." 
2. During Data Collection 
"I was surprised when an administrator downplayed HR challenges. This highlighted the need to 
gather diverse perspectives." 
"During this interview, I noticed I strongly agreed with the participant’s view on the need for 
standardized HR policies. I must ensure my agreement does not lead me to overemphasize this 
perspective in analysis. I will review responses from participants with differing views to balance my 
interpretation.”  
"During today's interview, a senior administrator argued that performance evaluations are 
unnecessary in academia. I was surprised and initially disagreed. To avoid bias, I will review my 
interview questions to ensure I am not favouring responses that support my view. I will also seek 
out perspectives from those who advocate for and against evaluations." 
3. Thematic Analysis Reflections 
"While workload was a recurring issue, its causes varied. I will re-examine participant responses 
to ensure a balanced interpretation." 
"As I analyse responses, I notice that I’m paying more attention to faculty complaints about HR 
policies rather than instances where HR processes worked well. To maintain balance, I will revisit 
positive responses and ensure they are equally represented in my findings." 
"Many participants mentioned workload issues, but their concerns varied. Some linked it to HR 
policy gaps, while others mentioned broader institutional challenges. Instead of assuming a direct 
link between HR policies and workload, I will carefully analyse supporting data before making this 
claim.” 
"I recognize that my interpretation of these findings is influenced by my previous experience in HR 
management. To counterbalance this, I will include direct participant quotes to ensure the findings 
are based on their perspectives, not just my interpretation."

Appendix L 
Communication and Change Management Plan 
 
1. Objective To ensure the successful implementation of the HR-BSC at Schiller International University 
(SIU), this communication and change management plan aims to: 
• 
Promote understanding and awareness of the HR-BSC framework. 
• 
Foster institutional alignment and buy-in from all stakeholders. 
• 
Reduce resistance and anxiety associated with organizational change. 
• 
Ensure smooth adoption of new HR processes and technologies across all SIU campuses. 
 
2. Key Stakeholders 
• 
SIU Leadership (CEO, Provost, Campus Directors, Dean of Academics) 
• 
Faculty Members 
• 
Administrative and Support Staff 
• 
HR Task Force 
• 
Students (as indirect stakeholders) 
 
3. Communication Strategy 
3.1 Communication Goals: 
• 
Ensure transparency regarding HR-BSC objectives and progress. 
• 
Tailor communication to each stakeholder group. 
• 
Maintain consistent messaging across all four campuses. 
 
3.2 Communication Channels: 
• 
Internal Newsletters (monthly email updates) 
• 
Town Hall Meetings (quarterly, virtual and in-person) 
• 
Campus-level Briefings (bi-weekly during rollout) 
• 
Regular Email Updates (targeted by role) 
• 
Feedback Survey and Open Forums 
 
3.3 Messaging Themes: 
• 
Purpose and strategic alignment of the HR-BSC 
• 
How it benefits faculty, staff, and the institution 
• 
What changes to expect and when 
• 
How to get involved or seek support 
 
4. Change Management Plan 
4.1 Change Drivers: 
• 
Strategic alignment of HR practices 
• 
Standardization of policies and performance evaluation 
 
 
13 
• 
Improved transparency and accountability 
 
4.2 Change Management Phases: 
Phase 1: Awareness & Engagement (March 2022) 
• 
Kick-off sessions introducing HR-BSC 
• 
Sharing success story from other institution (California University) 
• 
Formation of local “HR-BSC Champions” (Campus Directors) 
 
Phase 2: Capacity Building & Training (March-April 2022) 
• 
Hands-on training workshops for Personio and new HR procedures 
• 
Role-specific guides and instructions 
• 
Peer mentoring and campus-specific support (through project manager and HR agency) 
 
Phase 3: Implementation & Feedback (April –June 2022) 
• 
Rollout of standard processes across campuses 
• 
Feedback loops (3-month session, check-ins) 
• 
Adjustments based on stakeholder input  
• 
Embed HR-BSC practices in regular university operations 
 
Phase 4: Evaluation & Institutionalization (November 2022-Febrary 2023) 
• 
Evaluate success based on KPIs and sentiment analysis 
• 
Ongoing training and leadership reinforcement 
 
5. Timeline Overview 
• 
Month 1: Stakeholder engagement and messaging 
• 
Months 2-3: Training and workshops 
• 
Months 4-6: Pilot rollout and feedback collection 
• 
Month 7 onwards: Evaluation, optimization, and institutionalization 
 
6. Risk Mitigation Strategies 
• 
Address concerns early via Q&A sessions (March 2022 & June 2022) 
• 
Offer one-on-one support for resistant stakeholders (project manager, Campus Directors, and HR 
agency) 
 
7. Evaluation Metrics 
• 
Engagement levels (meeting attendance, intranet traffic) 
• 
Feedback session 
• 
Completion rates of training modules 
• 
Adoption rates of new processes and software
